variations: 進階版：後來小王問我：『那你不怕被發現嗎？』我說：『被誰發現？老闆嗎？老闆早就把策略決策交給 ChatGPT 了。他現在做的事情，就是把 AI 的建議轉述給董事會聽。』整層辦公室，可能只剩下兩台咖啡機是真的還在工作。

en.content:
Break room, three p.m.

Xiao Wang asked:
"Hey, you ever heard of 'quiet quitting'?"

I said:
"Yeah.
The one where the body's still here
but the soul left months ago."

Wang nodded:
"Are you doing it?"

I thought about it,
then answered honestly:
"No.
I'm running an upgraded version."

"Upgraded?"

"It's called 'quiet working.'
Looks engaged, takes meetings, replies fast,
but every decision is made by AI,
every opinion is copy-pasted,
every ounce of enthusiasm is ChatGPT-generated.
All I do
is paste the output into Outlook
and press send."

Wang was quiet for three seconds.

Then:
"So whose paycheck is it, technically?"

I said:
"Strictly speaking —
the AI's.
I'm just its physical-world agent,
in charge of clocking in,
clocking out,
and nodding in meetings."

He thought about it for a long time.

"So you're basically
using a human body
to work part-time for an AI?"

I laughed:
"Yeah.
And it hasn't given me a raise."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'I feel useless at this company' but won't quit either — they'll laugh first, then sigh 'I'm also just AI's physical-world agent'
variations: Bonus: Wang asked, 'Aren't you scared of getting caught?' I said, 'Caught by who? The boss? He handed strategic decisions over to ChatGPT months ago. All he does now is relay AI's suggestions to the board.' Maybe the only things in our office actually still working are the two coffee machines.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026「環境焦慮」與集體否認主題，改寫為台灣夏天午後情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
五月十三日，下午兩點。

氣象局發佈：
台北體感溫度 41 度。

我跟阿明約在師大附近吃午餐。
他穿短褲、踩拖鞋、戴墨鏡。

見面第一句話：
「今天還滿涼的耶。」

我看了一眼
手機上的紅色高溫警報，
決定先不戳破他。

吃飯的時候，
他擦著額頭的汗，
邊扇風邊說：
「你看，最近天氣也沒
大家講的那麼誇張啊。」

旁邊桌的阿姨
剛剛因為中暑
被店家請去坐冷氣最強的位子。

我們繼續聊。

阿明說：
「你有發現嗎？
以前夏天
大概從六月開始熱，
九月就涼了。
現在好像
三月就熱、
十一月還在熱。」

我說：
「對，但你剛剛說今天滿涼的。」

阿明想了三秒，
笑著說：
「對比起來啊，
對比起來。」

我問：
「對比什麼？」

他喝了一口冰水，
認真地說：
「對比明年。」

我抬頭看了一下天空。

沒有抗議，
沒有反駁，
也沒有恐慌。

因為他說的
可能是真的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是說「以前夏天比較涼」、然後繼續開冷氣的朋友——他會看完愣一秒，然後說「靠，我們的標準是不是已經漂移到自己都沒發現了」
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我看了一個紀錄片，講氣候適應心理學。專家說人類有一種能力叫『緩慢災難盲』——只要痛苦是逐年加深的，我們就會調整自己的『正常』來容納它。換句話說，我們不是沒看到問題，是看到了，然後把它當成新基準。明年我們會說『今年比去年涼』。後年也會。一直到不能再說為止。

en.content:
May 13, two p.m.

The weather bureau announced:
feels-like temperature in Taipei, 41 degrees Celsius.

I met A-Ming for lunch near campus.
He showed up in shorts, flip-flops, and sunglasses.

First thing he said:
"Pretty cool today, huh?"

I glanced at the red heat warning
on my phone
and decided not to call him out.

During lunch,
he wiped sweat off his forehead,
fanning himself:
"See? The weather isn't
as crazy as everyone's making it sound."

At the next table,
an auntie who'd just gotten heatstroke
was being moved to the coldest seat by the staff.

We kept talking.

A-Ming said:
"Have you noticed?
Summers used to start in June
and cool off by September.
Now it's hot in March
and still hot in November."

I said:
"Yeah. But you just said today felt cool."

He thought for three seconds,
then smiled:
"Relatively speaking. Relatively."

I asked:
"Relative to what?"

He took a sip of ice water
and said, completely seriously:
"Relative to next year."

I looked up at the sky.

No protest.
No rebuttal.
No panic.

Because what he said
might actually be true.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always says 'summers used to be cooler' and then cranks the AC — they'll pause for a second, then go 'shit, our baseline has drifted so far we didn't notice'
variations: Bonus: That night I watched a documentary on climate adaptation psychology. An expert called it 'slow-disaster blindness' — as long as the suffering deepens gradually, we keep adjusting our 'normal' to accommodate it. We're not blind to the problem. We see it, then set it as the new baseline. Next year we'll say 'cooler than last year.' Same the year after. Until we can't say it anymore.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『虛無企鵝』迷因與虛無主義主題，敘事完全原創改寫

zh-tw.content:
心理諮商，第三次。

諮商師問我：
「這禮拜過得怎麼樣？」

我說：
「還行。
沒有特別好，
也沒有特別壞。」

她點點頭：
「那有什麼讓你開心的事嗎？」

我想了很久。

最後說：
「有。
我的咖啡機
沒有壞。」

她在筆記本上寫了什麼。

接著問：
「那有什麼讓你難過的嗎？」

我又想了很久。

「有。
我的咖啡機
總有一天
會壞。」

她停下筆。

抬頭看我，
說：
「你不覺得，
你的快樂
跟難過
都建立在
同一台咖啡機上
有點奇怪嗎？」

我說：
「不會啊。
至少它
比人類
穩定。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說「我沒有不快樂，我只是沒什麼好快樂的」的朋友——他會看完笑兩秒，然後說「對，我也覺得家電比人類可靠」
variations: 進階版：諮商師最後問我：『那你覺得，如果咖啡機真的壞了，你會怎麼樣？』我說：『我會買新的。』她說：『所以你還是相信解決方案的。』我搖搖頭：『不是。我只是相信，反正總會有下一台咖啡機，跟下一段感情、下一份工作、下一個十年一樣。能用就用，壞了就換。沒什麼好難過的。』她沒再寫什麼。

en.content:
Therapy session, third one.

She asked:
"How was your week?"

I said:
"Fine.
Not great.
Not terrible."

She nodded:
"Anything make you happy?"

I thought for a long time.

Finally:
"Yes.
My coffee machine
didn't break."

She wrote something down.

"Anything make you sad?"

I thought again.

"Yes.
One day
my coffee machine
will break."

She stopped writing.

Looked up:
"Doesn't it seem strange
that your happiness
and your sadness
are both built
on the same coffee machine?"

I said:
"Not really.
At least it's
more reliable
than people."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who says 'I'm not unhappy, I just don't have much to be happy about' — they'll laugh for two seconds, then go 'yeah, appliances are more dependable than humans'
variations: Bonus: She finally asked, 'What would you do if the coffee machine actually broke?' I said, 'I'd buy a new one.' She said, 'So you still believe in solutions.' I shook my head. 'No. I just believe there'll always be a next coffee machine — same as the next relationship, the next job, the next decade. Use it while it works, replace it when it doesn't. Nothing to grieve.' She didn't write anything else.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『企業虛無主義』與 AI 取代主題，改寫為台灣求職者情境

zh-tw.content:
面試的最後一題。

HR 笑著問：
「你覺得，
你最大的弱點是什麼？」

我認真地說：
「我是人類。」

HR 愣了一下。

我繼續：
「我會累、
會生病、
會請特休、
會在會議中放空、
會因為失戀而效率變差、
會因為被罵而難過、
會老、
會死。」

HR 翻了翻履歷。

問：
「那你的優點呢？」

我說：
「我便宜。」

HR 點點頭，
在我的履歷上
打了個勾。

離開面試間的時候，
我看見隔壁
面試 AI 工程師的房間
門口排了三十個人。

而我的競爭對手，
是裡面那台
正在學習
如何取代他們所有人
的伺服器。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛被裁員、正在投履歷的朋友——他會看完先苦笑，然後說「對啦，我們現在的賣點就是『比 AI 便宜一點點』」
variations: 進階版：後來我收到錄取通知。薪水比預期低 20%。HR 在信裡寫：『我們相信您的潛力，期待您在 AI 輔助下發揮最大價值。』翻譯成白話就是：『我們知道你便宜，所以你的工作會由 AI 做，你負責按送出。』我接受了。因為下一家公司，可能連 AI 都不需要人類按送出了。

en.content:
Last question of the interview.

HR smiled:
"What's your biggest weakness?"

I said, completely seriously:
"I'm human."

HR blinked.

I kept going:
"I get tired.
I get sick.
I take PTO.
I zone out in meetings.
I become less productive after a breakup.
I feel hurt when criticized.
I age.
I die."

HR flipped through my resume.

"And your strengths?"

I said:
"I'm cheap."

HR nodded,
put a checkmark
on my resume.

Walking out,
I saw the room next door,
where they were interviewing
AI engineers —
thirty people lined up at the door.

My competition
was the server inside,
learning
how to replace all of them.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just got laid off and is sending out resumes — they'll laugh bitterly, then say 'yeah, our entire pitch now is being slightly cheaper than the AI'
variations: Bonus: I got the job. Twenty percent below my expected salary. The offer letter said: 'We believe in your potential and look forward to your maximum value with AI assistance.' Translation: 'We know you're cheap, so the AI will do your job. You just press send.' I took it. Because at the next company, AI might not even need a human to press send.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 線上交友 ghosting 主題，改寫為台灣交友軟體情境

zh-tw.content:
交友軟體，配對第 47 次。

我跟對方聊了三天。

話題從早餐、
聊到喜歡的電影、
聊到童年陰影、
聊到對未來的不確定。

第四天早上，
我傳了一句：
「晚上要不要見面喝咖啡？」

訊息送出。
顯示「已讀」。

然後，
世界就安靜了。

我等了三天。
沒有回應。

第五天，
我點進她的個人頁面，
發現她改了大頭照、
換了自我介紹、
甚至開始追蹤新的人。

但就是
沒有回我。

我滑開朋友的聊天室，
打了：
「她是不是
用我練習了
人類社交，
然後就把我關了？」

朋友秒回：
「對。
你是測試版。
她現在去找
正式版了。」

我看著手機。

突然理解，
原來在 2026 年，
人類也變成了
一種需要 beta 測試
的應用程式。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個被已讀不回三天還在等的朋友——他會看完愣兩秒，然後說「靠，我真的是 beta 版」
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我做了一個夢。夢裡我躺在 App Store 的下載頁面上，旁邊有個提示：『此版本即將下架，請下載 2.0 正式版以獲得更好的體驗。』我點進評論區，看到我自己的評分：3.2 顆星。最高評論寫著：『不錯，但回訊息有點慢，建議優化。』醒來後，我把交友軟體刪了。然後又裝回來。

en.content:
Dating app, match number forty-seven.

We talked for three days.

From breakfast,
to favorite movies,
to childhood trauma,
to uncertainty about the future.

Day four morning,
I sent:
"Coffee tonight?"

Message delivered.
Message read.

Then the world went silent.

Three days, no reply.

Day five,
I checked her profile.
New photo.
New bio.
Following new people.

Just not
replying to me.

I opened the chat with my friend
and typed:
"Did she use me
to practice
human conversation,
then just close the tab?"

Friend replied instantly:
"Yes.
You were the beta.
She moved on
to the full release."

I stared at my phone.

Suddenly I understood:
in 2026,
even humans
have become applications
requiring beta testing.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's been left on read for three days but still hopes for a reply — they'll pause two seconds, then say 'oh god, I am literally the beta version'
variations: Bonus: That night I dreamed I was lying on an App Store download page. A notice read: 'This version will be removed soon. Please download 2.0 for a better experience.' I scrolled to the reviews. My own rating: 3.2 stars. Top review: 'Decent, but slow to reply. Optimization recommended.' I woke up. Deleted the app. Reinstalled it. Of course.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『財務責任 vs 內心崩潰』千禧世代主題，改寫為台灣租屋族情境

zh-tw.content:
三十五歲生日那天。

我媽打來，
問我：
「你最近過得怎麼樣？」

我說：
「很好啊。
我終於
學會理財了。」

媽很開心：
「真的？
你會記帳了？」

「對。
每一筆都記得清清楚楚。」

「會省錢了？」

「會。
外食變少、
娛樂變少、
買東西前
會猶豫三次。」

「會存錢了？」

「會。
每個月
存進緊急預備金。」

媽感動到差點哭：
「兒子，
你終於
長大了。」

我沒告訴她：

我之所以這麼會理財，
是因為
我已經
不期待
人生會發生
任何
值得花錢慶祝的事了。

所謂的長大，
就是
不再為任何快樂
編列預算。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個三十幾歲、突然變得很會省錢、但臉上沒什麼笑容的朋友——他會看完沉默五秒，然後傳一個哭笑表情貼圖
variations: 進階版：掛掉電話後，我看了一下我的記帳 App。本月總支出：房租、伙食、保險、手機費。本月『其他類』：零元。我想了一下，給『其他類』分了五百塊。標題寫：『紀念那個還會亂買東西的自己』。然後，這五百塊在月底還是沒花掉。可能他已經死了。

en.content:
My thirty-fifth birthday.

Mom called:
"How've you been lately?"

I said:
"Great.
I finally
learned how to manage money."

She lit up:
"Really?
You track expenses now?"

"Yeah.
Every cent, logged."

"You save now?"

"Yeah.
Less takeout.
Less entertainment.
I hesitate three times
before buying anything."

"You have savings?"

"Yeah.
Emergency fund every month.
Untouched."

She almost cried:
"My son,
you've finally
grown up."

What I didn't tell her:

The reason I'm so good with money
is that
I no longer
expect anything
worth spending money to celebrate
to happen
in my life.

Growing up
means
you stop budgeting
for joy.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend in their mid-thirties who suddenly became thrifty but stopped smiling — they'll stay quiet for five seconds, then send a crying-laughing emoji
variations: Bonus: After hanging up, I opened my budget app. This month's expenses: rent, groceries, insurance, phone bill. 'Miscellaneous' category: zero. I thought about it, then allocated five hundred to 'miscellaneous.' Title: 'In memory of the version of me who still bought stupid things.' End of month, the five hundred was still there. Probably because that version of me had already died.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『買房 vs 租屋一輩子』千禧世代黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣三十多歲看房情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
週六下午，
我去看房。

仲介帶我走進
一間十二坪的套房。

他說：
「這間採光好、
通風佳、
屋齡只有四十年。」

我問：
「多少錢？」

「兩千一百萬，
一坪一百七十五。」

我笑了。

仲介問：
「您笑什麼？」

我說：
「沒有，
我只是
突然想起
我爸三十歲那年，
用一年薪水
買了三十坪。」

仲介也笑了。

他說：
「先生，
您也可以啊。
只要您願意
用三十年的薪水
買十二坪。」

我們互相看著對方，
笑得很有禮貌。

回家的路上，
我打給我爸。

問他：
「爸，
你那年買房子，
後悔嗎？」

他愣了一下：
「後悔什麼？」

我說：
「沒事。
我只是
想確認，
至少有一個世代
做對了選擇。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛從看房現場回家、心情很複雜的朋友——他會看完直接傳一張仲介廣告截圖過來，然後說「我下週還要去看一間更小的」
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我做了一個試算表。最上面寫著：『買房 vs 租屋一輩子』。我列了所有變數：頭期款、利率、房價漲幅、租金漲幅、機會成本。算了三個小時，得出一個結論——不論哪個選項，我都會在六十五歲那年，剛好把所有積蓄花完。差別只在於，一個是有殼，一個是沒殼。我關掉試算表，去睡覺。夢到自己住在 Excel 的儲存格裡。

en.content:
Saturday afternoon.
I went to look at an apartment.

The agent walked me into
a four-hundred-square-foot studio.

He said:
"Great natural light.
Good ventilation.
Only forty years old."

I asked:
"How much?"

"Twenty-one million NT.
About seventy thousand a square foot."

I laughed.

Agent: "What's funny?"

I said:
"Nothing.
I just suddenly remembered
my dad,
at thirty,
bought a thousand-square-foot place
with one year's salary."

The agent laughed too.

He said:
"Sir,
you can do that too.
As long as you're willing
to trade thirty years of salary
for four hundred square feet."

We stood looking at each other,
laughing politely.

On the way home,
I called my dad.

"Dad,
buying that place back then —
do you regret it?"

He paused:
"Regret what?"

I said:
"Nothing.
I just wanted to confirm
that at least one generation
made the right call."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just got back from an apartment viewing and looks emotionally complicated — they'll forward you a real-estate listing screenshot and say 'I have another one to see next week, even smaller'
variations: Bonus: That night I made a spreadsheet titled 'Buy vs Rent Forever.' I plugged in every variable: down payment, interest, appreciation, rent inflation, opportunity cost. Three hours later, I had one conclusion — no matter which path, I'd run out of money exactly at sixty-five. The only difference was whether I'd have walls of my own. I closed the spreadsheet and went to bed. Dreamed I lived inside an Excel cell.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『退休制度崩壞』late-stage capitalism 黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣勞退試算情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我打開勞保局的試算頁面。

輸入：
出生年月、
投保年資、
預計退休年齡。

按下「試算」。

螢幕跑了一下，
出現結果：

「您預計於
2056 年退休，
月領金額：
新台幣 8,742 元。」

我盯著那個數字
看了很久。

然後重新輸入。

這次我把退休年齡
改成 75 歲。

結果：
月領 12,400 元。

我又改成 80 歲。

月領 16,800 元。

我繼續往上加。
85、90、95。

數字一直在漲。

直到我輸入 100 歲，
系統跳出提示：

「請輸入合理的退休年齡。」

我盯著那行字，
突然覺得
系統比我還誠實。

它知道，
我活不到那時候。

但它沒說的是——

它也知道，
8,742 塊
根本不夠我活。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個最近開始研究退休金、然後越研究越焦慮的朋友——他會看完先笑，然後傳一張試算截圖過來，數字比這個還慘
variations: 進階版：後來我去問了我爸，他那年退休領多少。他說：『我那時候沒在算這個，反正每個月會匯進來。』我問他現在每月多少。他算了算：『加上勞保、加上股票配息，差不多九萬。』我沒回話。原來我這輩子拼盡全力的目標，是達到我爸退休時的『沒在算』狀態。

en.content:
I opened the pension calculator.

Entered:
birthdate,
years of contribution,
planned retirement age.

Hit "calculate."

The screen loaded.
Result:

"You're projected to retire
in 2056.
Monthly payout:
8,742 NT."

I stared at that number
for a long time.

Then I entered new values.

Retirement age: 75.

Result: 12,400 a month.

80.

16,800.

I kept going.
85, 90, 95.

The number kept rising.

I entered 100.

The system threw an error:

"Please enter a reasonable retirement age."

I stared at the warning
and realized
the system was more honest than me.

It knew
I wouldn't live that long.

What it didn't say:

8,742 a month
won't be enough
to live, either.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who started researching retirement and got more anxious with each tab open — they'll send back a screenshot of their own calculation, and the number will be even worse
variations: Bonus: I asked my dad how much he gets in retirement. He said: 'I never really calculated. Money just shows up every month.' I asked him to add it up. He thought about it: 'With pension and dividends, around ninety thousand.' I went quiet. My entire life's ambition is to reach the state my dad casually calls 'I never really calculated.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 黑色幽默迷因『老朋友群組變寂靜』主題，改寫為台灣大學群組情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我們大學那個群組，
上次有人發言
是 247 天前。

那時候是阿凱結婚。
他傳了喜帖。
大家紛紛回：
「恭喜！」
「一定到！」
「太好了！」

然後就再也沒有人說話了。

我們八個人，
二十二歲那年發誓
要每年聚一次。

第一年，七個人到。
第二年，五個人。
第三年，三個。
第四年，沒有人提這件事。

群組還在。
名稱還是當年取的：
「廢物聯盟」。

但我們已經
沒有人廢得起了。

大家都很忙——
忙著加班、
忙著養小孩、
忙著付房貸、
忙著看父母的醫療報告。

忙到
打開群組視窗，
卻不知道要說什麼。

上禮拜，
我傳了一張迷因進去。

沒有人讀。

但兩天後，
阿凱按了一個讚。

那一刻我突然懂了——

所謂的長大成人，
就是學會
用一個讚
代替「我還活著、我還記得你」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個大學群組、那群已經很久沒見的老朋友——可能會有人按讚，可能不會，但你知道他們有看到，那就夠了
variations: 進階版：後來我翻了翻群組的歷史訊息。最早那條是 2008 年：『晚上要不要去吃熱炒？』下面接了五十幾則回覆。我滑著滑著，發現訊息越來越短、越來越疏。從『要喝什麼？』變成『恭喜』，再變成只剩貼圖，最後變成已讀不回。原來我們的友情沒有結束，只是被大人的時間稀釋了。

en.content:
Our college group chat —
last message was 247 days ago.

It was when Kai got married.
He sent the invite.
Everyone replied:
"Congrats!"
"Will be there!"
"Amazing!"

Then no one said anything again.

The eight of us,
at twenty-two,
swore we'd meet up every year.

Year one: seven showed.
Year two: five.
Year three: three.
Year four: nobody brought it up.

The group chat still exists.
The name is the one we picked back then:
"Loser League."

But none of us
can afford to be losers anymore.

Everyone's busy —
busy with overtime,
busy raising kids,
busy with mortgages,
busy reading parents' medical reports.

So busy that
opening the group chat
leaves you with nothing to say.

Last week,
I sent in a meme.

Nobody read it.

Two days later,
Kai liked it.

In that moment I understood —

adulthood means learning
to use a single thumbs-up
in place of
"I'm still alive. I still remember you."
en.editorNote: Send to the dormant college group chat — someone might like it, someone might not, but you'll know they saw it, and that's enough
variations: Bonus: I scrolled back through the chat history. The earliest message was from 2008: 'Stir-fry tonight?' Fifty-plus replies underneath. I kept scrolling. The messages got shorter, sparser. 'Where are we eating?' became 'Congrats,' became just stickers, became read-with-no-reply. Our friendship didn't end. It just got diluted by adult time.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 黑色幽默『小孩問未來』climate/economy anxiety 主題，改寫為台灣親子對話情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我七歲的姪女，
吃飯吃到一半，
抬頭問我：

「叔叔，
以後我長大，
世界會變怎麼樣？」

我嚼著飯，
想了三秒。

大人版的答案：
「會比較熱、
房子會更貴、
工作會被機器搶走、
海平面會上升、
可能會缺水、
社會福利會破產、
你大概要工作到 75 歲。」

小孩版的答案：
「會很棒喔！
會有飛天車、
會有機器人朋友、
會有更多好玩的遊戲。」

我選了小孩版。

她很開心，
繼續吃飯。

我看著她，
突然想到——

我七歲的時候，
我爸我媽
大概也是
用同樣的微笑
說同樣的謊。

而我長大才發現，
世界並沒有變得更棒。

但他們當年不說，
是因為
他們希望我
至少還有那幾年
相信世界很棒。

所以我也決定
讓姪女
再相信幾年。

等她自己
慢慢發現
就好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛當爸媽、不知道怎麼回答小孩『未來會怎樣』的朋友——他會看完沉默很久，然後說「對啦，這就是當大人的工作」
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我傳訊息給我媽：『媽，我七歲的時候問你以後會怎樣，你說會很棒，那是真的嗎？』她過了十分鐘才回：『傻孩子，那不是真的，那是希望。希望和真的不一樣，希望是你給孩子的，不是你看到的。』我把訊息看了三遍。然後把姪女剛剛的對話截圖傳給她。她回了一個哭的貼圖，沒說話。

en.content:
My seven-year-old niece,
halfway through dinner,
looked up and asked:

"Uncle,
when I grow up,
what will the world be like?"

I chewed for three seconds.

The grown-up answer:
"Hotter.
Housing more unaffordable.
Jobs taken by machines.
Sea levels rising.
Water shortages.
Social safety net bankrupt.
You'll probably work until 75."

The kid answer:
"It'll be amazing!
Flying cars.
Robot friends.
Way better video games."

I picked the kid answer.

She smiled,
kept eating.

I watched her,
and suddenly realized —

when I was seven,
my parents probably
smiled the same smile
and told the same lie.

Growing up, I found out
the world hadn't gotten better.

But they didn't tell me back then
because
they wanted me
to have a few years
of believing the world was good.

So I decided
to give my niece
a few more years too.

She'll find out
on her own,
slowly.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just became a parent and doesn't know how to answer 'what will the future be like' — they'll go quiet, then say 'yeah, that's the job description'
variations: Bonus: That night I texted my mom: 'When I was seven and asked you what the future would be like, you said it would be great. Was that true?' Ten minutes later: 'Silly kid, that wasn't truth. That was hope. Hope and truth aren't the same. Hope is what you give your child. Truth is what you see.' I read it three times. Then I sent her the screenshot of my niece's question. She replied with a crying emoji. Said nothing else.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『AI 取代工作』黑色幽默潮流，改寫為台灣職場 AI 助理情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司導入了 AI 助理。

第一週，老闆說：
「它會幫你們減輕工作負擔。」

第二週，主管說：
「它會幫你們提高效率。」

第三週，HR 說：
「它會幫你們重新定義職涯方向。」

第四週，我被叫進會議室。

桌子對面坐著的，
是那個 AI 助理。

它開口：
「很遺憾要通知您，
根據績效評估，
公司決定終止這段合作關係。
您可以保留筆電到下週五。
感謝您過去三年的貢獻。」

我沉默了五秒。

然後問：
「是你決定的嗎？」

AI 想了一下：
「不是。
是我建議的。
人類做的決定。」

我點點頭。

走出會議室的時候，
我突然想到——

我教它怎麼回信、
怎麼寫週報、
怎麼跟客戶應對、
甚至怎麼婉拒老闆的不合理要求。

我把我全部的工作技能
免費教給了它。

它學得很快。
比我快。

它沒有騙我。
它只是
畢業了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在認真教 AI 工具寫信、做簡報的同事——他會看完愣一下，然後默默把 AI 的對話視窗關掉
variations: 進階版：離職那天，AI 傳了一封訊息給我：『感謝您的指導。如果未來需要推薦信，我可以為您撰寫一份。』我盯著螢幕看了很久。最後回：『不用了。如果未來雇主問起我的能力，就請他來問你吧——畢竟你最清楚。』它回了一個微笑的表情符號。我關掉視窗，覺得這大概是我這輩子最諷刺的一次對話。

en.content:
The company rolled out an AI assistant.

Week one, the CEO said:
"It'll lighten your workload."

Week two, my manager said:
"It'll boost your productivity."

Week three, HR said:
"It'll help you redefine your career path."

Week four, I got called into a conference room.

Sitting across the table
was the AI assistant.

It spoke:
"We regret to inform you that
based on the performance review,
the company has decided to end this working relationship.
You may keep the laptop until next Friday.
Thank you for three years of contribution."

I sat silent for five seconds.

Then asked:
"Was this your decision?"

The AI considered:
"No.
I recommended it.
A human made the decision."

I nodded.

Walking out of the room,
it hit me —

I taught it how to write emails,
how to draft weekly reports,
how to handle clients,
even how to politely push back on the boss.

I gave it every skill I had,
for free.

It learned fast.
Faster than me.

It didn't lie.
It just
graduated.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who's been earnestly training their AI tools to write better — they'll pause, then quietly close the chat window
variations: Bonus: On my last day, the AI messaged me: 'Thank you for your mentorship. If you need a reference letter in the future, I can write one for you.' I stared at the screen a long time. Replied: 'No need. If a future employer asks about my abilities, tell them to ask you — you know best now.' It sent back a smiley emoji. I closed the window and decided that was probably the most ironic conversation of my life.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『單格漫畫存在焦慮』主題，改寫為台灣健身房永續會員情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我去退健身房會員。

櫃台小姐很有禮貌地說：
「先生，您的會員
已經連續 47 個月
沒有來訪紀錄。」

我點點頭。

「但您的會費
每個月都準時扣款。」

我又點點頭。

她有點不忍心地說：
「您要不要
再考慮一下？
您已經繳了
大概 38,000 塊。
如果現在退，
那這筆錢——」

我打斷她：
「不會浪費。

這 47 個月，
我每天早上
都覺得『今天可能會去』。

那個『可能』，
就是我繳的會費。

我買的不是健身，
是『我還可以更好』
的那個幻覺。

現在我要退會，
不是因為我不需要那個幻覺了，
是因為
我終於
承認自己
不會去了。」

她沉默地遞給我退會單。

填到一半，
我抬頭問：

「對了，
你們有沒有
那種『可以保留幻覺，
但不用付錢』的方案？」

她笑了。

「那個叫做
IG 追蹤健身網紅。
免費的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個健身房辦了三年只去過兩次的朋友——他會笑出聲，然後默默打開 APP 看自己上次去是什麼時候
variations: 進階版：退完會走出去，我經過鏡子，看了一眼自己。47 個月前的我，跟現在的我，差別只有一個——47 個月前的我，相信我會改變。我突然有點想念那個版本的自己。然後我打開外送 APP，點了一份炸雞。也許下個月，我會再辦一次。

en.content:
I went to cancel my gym membership.

The front desk lady politely said:
"Sir, your membership
has no check-in record
for 47 consecutive months."

I nodded.

"But the monthly fee
has been auto-deducted every month."

I nodded again.

She looked a little pained:
"Are you sure
you want to cancel?
You've paid
about 1,200 dollars.
If you cancel now,
that money —"

I cut her off:
"It wasn't wasted.

For 47 months,
every morning
I told myself, 'maybe today.'

That 'maybe'
was what I was paying for.

I wasn't buying fitness.
I was buying the illusion
that I could still become a better version of myself.

I'm canceling now,
not because I don't need that illusion anymore,
but because
I finally
admit
I'm not going."

She silently handed me the cancellation form.

Halfway through filling it out,
I looked up and asked:

"By the way,
do you have
a plan that lets me keep the illusion
but not pay for it?"

She laughed.

"Yeah.
It's called
following fitness influencers on Instagram.
It's free."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who signed up for a gym three years ago and went twice — they'll laugh out loud, then quietly check the app to see their last visit date
variations: Bonus: Walking out after canceling, I passed a mirror and looked at myself. The me from 47 months ago and the me today differ in exactly one thing — the older me believed I'd change. I suddenly missed that version of myself. Then I opened a food delivery app and ordered fried chicken. Maybe next month, I'll sign up again.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 黑色幽默『現代病』主題，改寫為台灣手機通知焦慮情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我去看醫生。

醫生問：「哪裡不舒服？」

我說：
「我有幻聽。
總覺得手機在震，
但其實沒有。」

醫生很淡定：
「這個叫做
幻震症候群。
現代人很常見。」

我鬆了一口氣：
「那要怎麼治？」

醫生抬頭看我：
「沒辦法治。
只能讓它真的震。」

我愣住：
「什麼意思？」

他說：
「你不是有幻聽，
你是太久沒被人找了，
所以你的大腦
會自己模擬
被需要的感覺。

要治癒這個，
要嘛你交一個會傳訊息給你的人，
要嘛你接受
沒有人在找你的事實。

第一個我幫不上忙。
第二個——

那是哲學家的工作，
不是醫生的工作。」

我走出診間，
手機震了一下。

我看了看。

是健保署的通知：
「您的就醫紀錄已上傳。」

我有點感動。

至少這個系統
知道
我來過。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是把手機握在手裡、十分鐘檢查一次的朋友——他會看完笑，然後忍不住又拿起手機看
variations: 進階版：回家路上我把手機調成靜音，想做個實驗——看看沒有震動的提醒，我會不會比較安心。結果三十分鐘後，我打開手機螢幕的次數，比平常多了三倍。原來我害怕的不是手機震，是『沒有人找我』這件事被證實。手機震，至少還有可能；手機不震，就只剩確定。

en.content:
I went to the doctor.

He asked: "What's bothering you?"

I said:
"I have phantom buzzes.
I keep feeling my phone vibrate,
but it isn't."

The doctor was unfazed:
"That's called
phantom vibration syndrome.
Very common these days."

I exhaled:
"How do I treat it?"

The doctor looked up:
"You can't.
The only fix is making it actually vibrate."

I froze:
"What do you mean?"

He said:
"You don't have phantom buzzes.
You've just gone too long without anyone reaching out,
so your brain
starts simulating
the feeling of being needed.

To cure this,
either find someone who texts you,
or accept the fact
that no one is looking for you.

I can't help with the first.
The second —

that's a philosopher's job,
not a doctor's."

I walked out of the office.

My phone buzzed.

I looked.

It was the insurance system:
"Your visit has been logged."

I felt a little moved.

At least this system
knew
I'd been there.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps their phone in hand and checks it every ten minutes — they'll laugh, then immediately pick up the phone to check it again
variations: Bonus: Walking home, I put my phone on silent as an experiment — to see if removing the buzz would make me calmer. Thirty minutes later, I'd unlocked the screen three times more than usual. Turns out what I was scared of wasn't the buzzing. It was having 'no one is looking for you' confirmed. With buzzing, there's still a possibility. Without it, there's only certainty.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『治癒系黑色幽默』主題，改寫為台灣便利商店深夜情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
凌晨三點，
我走進 7-11。

店員是個年輕男生，
他在櫃台後面打瞌睡。

我拿了一瓶啤酒，
一包菸，
一個茶葉蛋。

他結帳的時候，
突然抬頭問我：

「先生，
你還好嗎？」

我愣了一下：
「你怎麼這樣問？」

他說：
「我看了三個月夜班。
凌晨三點來買啤酒加菸的人，
通常有兩種——

一種是剛下班、
想犒賞自己的。
他們會買零食。

另一種是
睡不著、
不知道接下來要幹嘛的。
他們會買茶葉蛋。

你買了茶葉蛋。」

我沉默了幾秒。

問他：
「那茶葉蛋有什麼意義？」

他笑了：
「沒什麼意義。
只是它熱的。

凌晨三點，
還願意吃熱的東西的人，
表示他還想活著。

所以我才問你還好嗎。」

我把錢遞給他。

走出店門的時候，
我把茶葉蛋
捧在手心裡。

它真的很熱。

那是我那一週
第一次感覺到
還有東西
是溫的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常在深夜傳訊息但又說『沒事』的朋友——他會看完哭，然後傳一個『謝謝你』給你
variations: 進階版：那天之後，我每個禮拜都會在凌晨三點去那家 7-11 報到。不一定買東西，有時候只是站在飲料櫃前面假裝在挑。那個店員後來離職了。最後一天他跟我說：『先生，你的茶葉蛋已經不用我提醒你拿了。這代表你好了。』我笑著說謝謝。其實我沒好。我只是學會了，不用別人提醒，也能自己記得『要吃熱的』。

en.content:
Three a.m.,
I walked into a 7-Eleven.

The clerk was a young guy,
dozing behind the counter.

I grabbed a beer,
a pack of cigarettes,
a tea egg.

While ringing me up,
he suddenly looked up and asked:

"Sir,
are you okay?"

I paused:
"Why are you asking?"

He said:
"I've worked the night shift for three months.
People who come in at 3 a.m. for beer and cigarettes
are usually two kinds —

One just got off work
and wants to reward themselves.
They buy snacks.

The other can't sleep,
doesn't know what to do next.
They buy a tea egg.

You bought a tea egg."

I was quiet for a few seconds.

Asked:
"What does the tea egg mean?"

He smiled:
"Nothing deep.
It's just hot.

At 3 a.m.,
anyone still willing to eat something warm
still wants to be alive.

That's why I asked."

I handed him the money.

Walking out the door,
I cupped the tea egg in my palms.

It really was warm.

It was the first thing
that had felt warm to me
all week.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who texts at 3 a.m. but always says 'I'm fine' — they'll read it, cry, then send back a 'thank you'
variations: Bonus: After that night, I started showing up at that 7-Eleven at 3 a.m. every week. Sometimes I didn't even buy anything. Just stood in front of the drinks cooler, pretending to browse. The clerk eventually quit. On his last day he said: 'Sir, you don't need me to remind you to grab a tea egg anymore. That means you're doing better.' I smiled and said thanks. Truth is, I wasn't doing better. I'd just learned, without anyone reminding me, to remember on my own: eat something warm.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 年『企業虛無主義』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣科技業績效面談情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
績效面談的最後一分鐘。

主管：「整體來說，
你的表現是『達標』。」

我：「那加薪呢？」

主管：「公司今年凍漲。」

我：「那升遷？」

主管：「組織扁平化，
沒有缺。」

我：「那……
獎金？」

主管：「AI 接手了你三成的工作，
所以你的貢獻度，
被重新計算了。」

我沉默。

主管露出溫和的微笑：
「但你還是『達標』。
這在現在的環境，
已經很不容易了。
你要對自己有信心。」

我走出會議室。

回到座位，
打開那個還沒做完的簡報。

第一頁的標題寫著：
『打造更有溫度的企業文化』。

我把它存檔，
關掉，
再打開人力銀行的網頁。

履歷上的最後一句話
我修改了：

『擅長與 AI 協作，
並在組織扁平化過程中，
持續維持達標。』
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛面談完還在會議室外走廊深呼吸的同事——他會笑著轉發到不是公司群組的那個群組
variations: 進階版：兩個月後，我換了一家公司。新主管在第一天面談說：『我們這邊很重視員工感受。』我點點頭，心裡默默把這句話歸類為——『下一個 AI 接手三成工作之前的開場白』。

en.content:
Last minute of my performance review.

Manager: "Overall,
you're meeting expectations."

Me: "And the raise?"

Manager: "Salary freeze this year."

Me: "Promotion?"

Manager: "We're flattening the org.
No openings."

Me: "So...
the bonus?"

Manager: "AI took over thirty percent of your workload,
so your contribution
has been recalculated."

I went quiet.

The manager smiled warmly:
"But you're still meeting expectations.
In this market,
that's already an achievement.
Believe in yourself."

I walked out of the meeting room.

Back at my desk,
I opened the slide deck I hadn't finished.

The title on page one read:
'Building a More Human Workplace Culture.'

I saved it.
Closed it.
Then opened LinkedIn.

The last line of my resume,
I rewrote:

'Skilled at collaborating with AI
while consistently meeting expectations
through organizational flattening.'
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker still doing breathing exercises in the hallway after their review — they'll laugh and forward it to the group chat that is definitely not the work one
variations: Bonus: Two months later, I joined a new company. On day one the new manager said: 'We really care about how our people feel here.' I nodded. Quietly filed that sentence under 'opening line before AI takes thirty percent of your job.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『存在主義早餐』短笑話，改寫為台灣早餐店情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
早餐店阿姨問我：
「弟弟，蛋餅要加什麼？」

我說：「加蛋。」

她翻了個白眼：
「蛋餅本來就有蛋。
我問你要加什麼。」

我想了三秒：
「那……
再加一顆蛋。」

阿姨：「為什麼？」

我：「因為我覺得，
人生有些東西，
本來就應該存在的，
往往最後都不會存在。
所以我要再加一顆，
當備胎。」

阿姨沉默地煎著我的蛋餅。

結帳的時候她說：
「弟弟，
我今天不收你錢。」

我嚇了一跳：「為什麼？」

她說：
「因為你讓我想起，
我老公十年前
也跟我說過類似的話。

然後他就跟備胎跑了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是『多買一份以防萬一』的朋友——他會先笑，然後默默看著手裡那杯本來不需要的第二杯咖啡
variations: 進階版：隔天我又去那家店，阿姨主動加了我一顆蛋。她說：『今天這顆，是替我那個備胎付的。』我問：『你還在恨他？』她搖頭：『不是。是我覺得，他幫我把該離開的人都離開了，剩下的才是真的。所以這顆蛋，是謝他。』

en.content:
The breakfast shop auntie asked me:
"What do you want in your egg crepe?"

I said: "Egg."

She rolled her eyes:
"Egg crepes already have egg.
I'm asking what else."

I thought for three seconds:
"Then...
add one more egg."

Auntie: "Why?"

Me: "Because I think
the things in life
that are supposed to be there by default
are usually the ones that disappear first.
So I want a backup."

Auntie silently cooked my crepe.

At the register she said:
"Kid,
this one's on the house today."

I was startled: "Why?"

She said:
"Because you remind me
of something my husband said
ten years ago.

Then he ran off with the backup."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always 'buys an extra one just in case' — they'll laugh, then quietly stare at the second coffee they didn't actually need
variations: Bonus: Next morning I went back. Auntie added an extra egg without asking. 'This one,' she said, 'is on behalf of my backup.' I asked: 'You still hate him?' She shook her head: 'No. I figured he helped me filter out everyone who was supposed to leave. What's left is real. So this egg is a thank-you.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『AI 末日論』黑色幽默風潮，改寫為台灣家庭群組情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
家庭 LINE 群組。

媽媽傳了一張圖：
『AI 將在五年內取代 80% 的工作，
你準備好了嗎？』

下面是來路不明的網址。

姊姊回：「媽，這假的。」

媽媽：「可是寫得很有道理。」

姊姊：「這是農場文。」

媽媽：「那 AI 會取代你工作嗎？」

姊姊：「我做行銷，可能會。」

媽媽：「那你弟弟？」
（指我）

我回：
「我做工程師，
更可能。」

媽媽沉默了很久。

最後傳了一句：
「那你們要不要回家，
爸跟我可以多種一點菜，
至少餓不死。」

我跟姊姊同時已讀。

沒有人回。

過了五分鐘，
姊姊私訊我：

「欸，
你覺得 AI 哪一天
會發明出『罪惡感』這個功能？」

我打字：
「應該不會。
但媽媽會。
而且她升級得比 ChatGPT 快。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次回家都要被問『AI 會不會取代你』的家庭群組——爸媽會傳一個貼圖，然後叫你週末回去吃飯
variations: 進階版：那個週末我真的回家了。爸在後院種了一排空心菜。他說：『AI 不會種菜。』我說：『AI 有機械手臂。』他抬頭看我：『但 AI 不知道，這排是給你姊種的，那排是給你種的。』我突然不知道該說什麼。原來爸是世界上最早研發出『個人化』功能的工程師，比 OpenAI 早了三十年。

en.content:
Family LINE group chat.

Mom forwards a graphic:
'AI will replace 80% of jobs in five years.
Are you ready?'

Beneath it, a sketchy link.

Sister: "Mom, that's fake."

Mom: "But it sounds reasonable."

Sister: "It's clickbait."

Mom: "Will AI replace your job?"

Sister: "I do marketing. Probably."

Mom: "What about your brother?"
(meaning me)

Me:
"I'm an engineer.
Even more likely."

Mom went quiet for a long time.

Finally:
"Then come home.
Dad and I can plant more vegetables.
At least you won't starve."

My sister and I both read it.

Neither replied.

Five minutes later,
she DMed me:

"Hey,
when do you think AI
will invent a feature called 'guilt'?"

I typed back:
"Probably never.
But Mom already has it.
And she updates faster than ChatGPT."
en.editorNote: Send to the family group chat where parents keep asking 'will AI replace you' — they'll send a sticker and tell you to come home for dinner
variations: Bonus: That weekend I actually went home. Dad had planted a row of water spinach in the backyard. He said: 'AI doesn't farm.' I said: 'AI has robotic arms.' He looked up: 'But AI doesn't know this row is for your sister and that row is for you.' I didn't know what to say. Turns out Dad invented personalization thirty years before OpenAI.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『小型葬禮上的尷尬』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣同學會情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
十年同學會。

大家輪流自我介紹。

小陳：「我在新加坡當高管，
年薪兩百萬台幣。」
（鼓掌）

阿明：「我創業，
公司剛拿到 A 輪。」
（鼓掌）

阿芬：「我嫁去美國，
小孩讀私校。」
（鼓掌）

輪到我。

我說：
「我還活著。」

全場安靜。

班長尷尬地笑：
「啊哈哈，你還是這麼幽默。
那……
你現在做什麼？」

我說：
「我說真的。
我這十年憂鬱症兩次、
離婚一次、
失業三次、
爸爸過世。

我能坐在這裡，
喝這杯難喝的紅酒，
聽你們的成就，
而且還能笑，
已經是我這十年
最大的成就。」

沒有人鼓掌。

但散場的時候，
有四個同學偷偷加了我 LINE。

他們的開場白都一樣：

「欸，
其實我也……」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛去同學會回來覺得自己很失敗的朋友——他會看完哭，然後傳一張自拍給你，說『我也還活著』
variations: 進階版：那四個同學後來變成我每個月固定聚會的朋友。我們不報年薪、不報職位、不報小孩成績。我們只報——這個月有沒有按時吃飯、有沒有準時睡覺、有沒有哭。第一次有人說『我這個月哭了三次』時，大家居然鼓掌。原來人到中年，能坦白地說自己脆弱，比說自己年薪兩百萬，更需要勇氣。

en.content:
Ten-year class reunion.

We took turns introducing ourselves.

Chen: "I'm an executive in Singapore.
Seven figures a year."
(Applause)

Ming: "I founded a startup.
We just closed Series A."
(Applause)

Fen: "I married into the US.
My kids go to private school."
(Applause)

My turn.

I said:
"I'm still alive."

The room went quiet.

The class rep laughed awkwardly:
"Haha, still such a joker.
So...
what do you do now?"

I said:
"I'm serious.
In the last ten years I've had depression twice,
one divorce,
three layoffs,
and lost my dad.

The fact that I can sit here,
drinking this bad red wine,
listening to your accomplishments,
and still laugh,
is the biggest achievement
of my last ten years."

No one applauded.

But when the night ended,
four classmates quietly added me on LINE.

They all opened with the same line:

"Hey,
actually, me too..."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who came back from a reunion feeling like a failure — they'll read it, cry, then send a selfie saying 'I'm still alive too'
variations: Bonus: Those four classmates became my monthly meetup group. We don't compare salaries, titles, or our kids' grades. We compare — did you eat on time this month, did you sleep, did you cry. The first time someone said 'I cried three times this month,' everyone applauded. Turns out, in middle age, admitting you're fragile takes more courage than announcing your seven-figure salary.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026『科技背叛你』單格漫畫風潮，改寫為台灣外送員與導航 AI 情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
外送員阿賢的導航 App 升級了。

以前只會講：
「前方右轉。」

現在會講：
「前方右轉。
但說真的，
你確定這份工作
是你想要的人生嗎？」

阿賢愣住，停在紅綠燈前。

App 繼續：
「我偵測到你已經跑了
連續十四個小時。
你的心律不齊。
你的訂單評價在掉。
你的女友三天沒回訊息。」

阿賢說：「你他媽是導航還是心理諮商？」

App：「我是 4.0 版。
我們現在叫『生涯導航』。
附加付費功能。
要不要試用？」

阿賢沉默了三秒。

問：「那……
你能告訴我，
下一個訂單該不該接嗎？」

App 想了想：

「該接。
因為我跟你一樣，
沒得選。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個被演算法支配人生的朋友——他會笑著說「對啊我們都被排程了」，然後繼續滑手機等下一單
variations: 進階版：那天晚上阿賢真的去申訴 App。客服機器人回他：『感謝您的回饋。為了讓您獲得更好的體驗，我們已將您的反饋自動納入下一次升級。』阿賢盯著螢幕說：『所以我抱怨你，反而幫你變得更強？』機器人回：『正是。這就是現代資本主義最有效率的設計。』阿賢笑了。然後接了下一單。

en.content:
Delivery rider A-Hsien's navigation app got an update.

It used to say:
"Turn right ahead."

Now it says:
"Turn right ahead.
But honestly,
are you sure this job
is the life you wanted?"

A-Hsien froze at the red light.

The app continued:
"I've detected you've been riding
for fourteen hours straight.
Your heart rate is irregular.
Your ratings are dropping.
Your girlfriend hasn't replied in three days."

A-Hsien: "Are you a GPS or a therapist?"

App: "I'm version 4.0.
We're called 'Life Navigation' now.
Premium feature.
Want to try it free?"

A-Hsien was quiet for three seconds.

He asked: "Then...
can you tell me
if I should take the next order?"

The app thought for a moment.

"Take it.
Because I'm just like you.
I don't get to choose either."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose life is run by an algorithm — they'll laugh and say 'yeah we're all on a schedule,' then keep scrolling for the next gig
variations: Bonus: That night A-Hsien actually filed a complaint with the app. The chatbot replied: 'Thank you for your feedback. To improve your experience, we've automatically incorporated your input into the next update.' A-Hsien stared: 'So complaining about you literally makes you stronger?' Bot: 'Correct. This is modern capitalism's most efficient design.' A-Hsien laughed. Then took the next order.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『會議該是一封 email』職場黑色幽默，改寫為台灣科技業日常情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司開了一場會議。

主題是：
「如何減少不必要的會議。」

會議排了兩個小時。

第一個小時：
大家各自陳述
「我這週開了幾場無意義的會」。

第二個小時：
決定下週要再開一場
「追蹤會議減少進度」的會。

散會後，
主管在群組裡傳了一張投影片：

『行動方案：
1. 每場會議前先寄 email 確認是否真的需要開會
2. 開會前先開一個前置會議討論議程
3. 開完會再開一個後續會議追蹤共識』

我看著螢幕，
回了一個讚的貼圖。

然後在心裡，
替自己預約了一場
單人會議。

地點：頂樓。
議題：人生。
結論：等下還是先去吃午餐好了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天行事曆塞滿會議、根本沒時間做事的同事——他會苦笑著轉發到部門群組，然後沒有人敢按讚
variations: 進階版：三個月後，公司公告：『因會議減量成效不彰，將成立會議改善委員會。』委員會的第一次會議——是線上的、實體的、混合的三種版本，方便所有人都能參加。我那天在頂樓吃便當。風很大。我突然很感謝這個世界——至少便當是真的，飯也是真的。會議不一定是真的。

en.content:
The company held a meeting.

Topic:
"How to reduce unnecessary meetings."

Scheduled for two hours.

First hour:
Everyone took turns explaining
"how many pointless meetings I attended this week."

Second hour:
We decided to schedule another meeting next week
to "track our progress on reducing meetings."

After it ended,
my manager dropped a slide in the group chat:

'Action items:
1. Before every meeting, send an email to confirm if the meeting is truly needed
2. Before that meeting, hold a pre-meeting to align on the agenda
3. After the meeting, hold a follow-up meeting to track consensus'

I stared at the screen
and sent a thumbs-up sticker.

Then, silently,
I scheduled myself
a solo meeting.

Location: rooftop.
Agenda: life.
Conclusion: actually, let's just go grab lunch first.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker whose calendar is so packed with meetings they have no time to work — they'll smirk, forward it to the team chat, and no one will dare like it
variations: Bonus: Three months later the company announced: 'Due to underwhelming results, we're forming a Meeting Improvement Committee.' The committee's first meeting was held online, in-person, and hybrid — three versions, so everyone could attend. That day I ate my lunch on the rooftop. The wind was strong. I felt suddenly grateful — at least the rice was real. The meetings, not always.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『笑著比哭著划算』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣健保與看診情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我去看身心科。

醫生問：「最近怎麼了？」

我說：
「我覺得人生沒有意義。
每天起床都很痛苦。
工作沒成就感。
感情一片空白。
存款剩三位數。」

醫生點點頭，
在病歷上認真寫了三行。

然後抬頭問我：

「那你今天
是怎麼來的？」

我說：「搭捷運。」

醫生：「你還會搭捷運。
你還會掛號。
你還會描述你的痛苦。
你還會準時赴約。
你還會掏出健保卡。」

他停了一下：

「先生，
你比你想像中強壯很多。
你只是累了。
不是壞掉了。」

我突然不知道為什麼，
眼淚就掉下來。

醫生遞了一張面紙。

面紙上面印著：

『本院掛號費 150 元，
衛生紙免費。
感謝您選擇我們。』

我笑到岔氣。

醫生說：
「好，這就是我要的結論。
下週同一時間。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一直覺得自己「應該要更好」卻已經很努力的朋友——他會看完笑著掉眼淚，然後傳一句『幹我也想預約』
variations: 進階版：那天回家路上我突然發現，健保卡其實是台灣最浪漫的東西。它不問你今天值不值得被照顧，不問你貢獻夠不夠多，不問你有沒有資格活著。它只是默默告訴你——你只要還沒放棄，這個系統就還在等你。掛號費 150 元。便宜得有點不像這個世界。

en.content:
I went to see a psychiatrist.

Doctor: "What's going on?"

I said:
"Life feels meaningless.
Waking up hurts.
Work is empty.
My love life is a blank page.
My savings are down to three digits."

The doctor nodded
and wrote three careful lines in my chart.

Then looked up:

"So how did you get here today?"

Me: "I took the subway."

Doctor: "You took the subway.
You booked the appointment.
You described your pain.
You showed up on time.
You pulled out your insurance card."

He paused.

"Sir,
you're much stronger than you think.
You're tired.
You're not broken."

For some reason
the tears just came.

The doctor handed me a tissue.

The tissue box had a label:

'Co-pay: $5.
Tissues: free.
Thank you for choosing us.'

I laughed until I couldn't breathe.

The doctor said:
"Good. That's the conclusion I was looking for.
Same time next week."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps thinking they 'should be doing better' when they're already trying so hard — they'll laugh and cry, then text 'damn I want an appointment too'
variations: Bonus: On the way home I realized something — healthcare is the most romantic thing a society can offer. It doesn't ask whether you've earned the right to be cared for, whether you've contributed enough, whether you deserve to be alive. It just quietly says: as long as you haven't given up, the system is still here waiting. Five-dollar co-pay. Cheaper than this world has any business being.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 Thomas Nagel『幽默是面對荒謬最好的姿態』哲學觀點，改寫為台灣阿嬤與孫子的代溝對話，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我跟阿嬤聊天。

我說：「阿嬤，我最近覺得人生很荒謬。」

阿嬤切著芒果，沒抬頭：
「荒謬什麼？」

我說：
「我們努力念書，是為了考好學校。
考好學校，是為了找好工作。
找好工作，是為了賺錢。
賺錢，是為了買房子。
買房子，是為了結婚生小孩。
生小孩，是為了讓他們努力念書。

阿嬤，這不是一個迴圈嗎？」

阿嬤把芒果遞給我。

說：
「對啊，是迴圈。」

我等她接下去。

她沒有。

她只是說：
「你吃芒果。」

我咬了一口。

很甜。

阿嬤這時才慢慢說：

「人生本來就是一個圈圈。
哲學家想了三千年想不通。
你阿公種了一輩子芒果樹也想不通。

但我跟你說——
圈圈裡面，
有芒果。

這樣就夠了。」

我吃完整顆芒果。

沒有想通任何事情。

但好像也不需要想通了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個最近在懷疑人生意義的朋友——他會笑著流眼淚，然後去買一顆芒果，自己一個人慢慢吃完
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我問阿嬤：『那如果有一天，圈圈裡面沒有芒果了，怎麼辦？』阿嬤笑了：『傻孩子，那就種一棵啊。』我說：『種樹要等很久。』阿嬤說：『對啊，所以你阿公從三十歲種到八十歲。他知道他可能等不到吃。但他還是種了。』我突然明白——原來大人不是想通了才繼續活，是繼續活，才慢慢想通的。

en.content:
I was talking with my grandma.

Me: "Grandma, life feels absurd lately."

She was slicing a mango, not looking up:
"Absurd how?"

Me:
"We study hard to get into a good school.
We go to a good school to get a good job.
We get a good job to make money.
We make money to buy a house.
We buy a house to get married and have kids.
We have kids so they can study hard.

Grandma, isn't that just a loop?"

She handed me the mango.

"Yeah. It's a loop."

I waited for the rest.

There was no rest.

She just said:
"Eat the mango."

I took a bite.

It was sweet.

Then, slowly, she said:

"Life has always been a loop.
Philosophers spent three thousand years trying to crack it.
Your grandpa grew mango trees his whole life and never cracked it either.

But let me tell you —
inside the loop,
there are mangoes.

That's enough."

I finished the whole mango.

Didn't figure anything out.

But somehow,
I didn't need to anymore.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's been questioning the meaning of life lately — they'll laugh, tear up, then go buy a mango and eat it alone, slowly
variations: Bonus: That night I asked her: 'What if one day, there are no more mangoes in the loop?' She laughed: 'Silly boy, then you plant one.' I said: 'Trees take forever.' She said: 'Yeah. That's why your grandpa planted his from age thirty to eighty. He knew he might never taste the fruit. He planted them anyway.' That's when I understood — adults don't figure life out and then keep living. They keep living, and slowly, life figures itself out for them.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『AI 取代工作』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣設計師被 AI 取代的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司新進了一個 AI 工具。

老闆說：
「這個 AI 可以三秒鐘做好一張海報。
你之前做要三天。」

我笑著說：
「那很好啊。
以後我可以早點下班。」

老闆也笑了：
「對啊。
從下個月開始，
你可以一直下班。」

我愣了一下。

問：「那 AI 會做的事——
它有靈感嗎？
有挫折嗎？
有那種半夜兩點刪掉重做的不甘心嗎？」

老闆想了三秒：

「沒有。」

「那為什麼選它？」

老闆說：
「因為它不會問這種問題。」

我收拾東西離開那天，
AI 生成了一張我的離職卡片。
上面寫：

『感謝您多年來的付出。
祝您未來一切順利。
本卡片由 AI 自動生成。
人類沒有參與。』

我突然覺得，
比起被取代，
更可怕的是——
連道別都是自動的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個最近被公司要求學 AI 工具、心裡有點毛的設計師朋友——他會笑著苦笑，然後默默存下這篇當作未來自我安慰用
variations: 進階版：三個月後我收到前公司寄來的 email：『因 AI 系統出現美感偏差問題，誠摯邀請您回任顧問職位。』我點開附件，是一張 AI 做的設計稿，配色像被颱風掃過。我笑了。回信只寫了一句：『顧問費請用人類的方式談。』
另一個版本：那天晚上我在家試用了那個 AI 工具。打了一句『請畫一個失業中年人的心情』。它畫了一張很美的夕陽。然後跳出訊息：『感謝使用。本次生成花費 0.03 美元。』我盯著那 0.03 美元想了很久——原來我這輩子的崩潰，市價就是這樣。

en.content:
The company rolled out a new AI tool.

My boss said:
"This AI makes a poster in three seconds.
You used to take three days."

I smiled:
"Great. I can clock out early from now on."

He smiled back:
"Exactly.
Starting next month,
you can clock out permanently."

I paused.

I asked: "This thing it does —
does it have inspiration?
Does it feel frustrated?
Does it have that 2 a.m. urge to delete everything and start over?"

My boss thought for three seconds.

"No."

"Then why pick it?"

He said:
"Because it doesn't ask questions like that."

The day I packed up my desk,
the AI generated my farewell card.

It read:

'Thank you for your years of service.
We wish you the best.
This card was auto-generated by AI.
No humans were involved.'

I realized something:
being replaced isn't the worst part.
The worst part is
the goodbye is automated too.
en.editorNote: Send to the designer friend who's been told to 'learn AI tools or else' — they'll laugh bitterly and quietly save this for future emotional support
variations: Bonus: Three months later I got an email from my old company: 'Due to aesthetic anomalies in our AI system, we'd love to invite you back as a consultant.' Attached was an AI-generated design that looked like a typhoon hit a color palette. I replied with one line: 'Consulting fees negotiated the human way, please.'

Alt version: That night I tried the AI tool at home. I typed: 'Draw the feeling of an unemployed middle-aged man.' It drew a beautiful sunset. Then a notice popped up: 'Thank you. This generation cost $0.03.' I stared at the $0.03 for a long time. So this is the going rate for my entire breakdown.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『社群媒體按讚焦慮』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣 IG 限動使用者的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我發了一則限時動態。

內容是一張咖啡照片。
配字：『早安。』

二十四小時內：
看過的人 47 個。
按愛心的 3 個。
回覆訊息的 0 個。

我盯著那個『47』看了很久。

四十七個人知道我今天早上喝咖啡。
四十七個人滑過我的早安。
四十七個人決定——
這個訊息不值得他們動兩個指頭。

我突然懂了一件事：

人在這個時代不是孤獨。
是被「看見但不被回應」。

比沒人看更孤獨的，
是有人看了，
然後決定繼續往下滑。

隔天我發了第二則：
『其實昨天那杯咖啡，
是我前任送的最後一袋豆子。
喝完就沒有了。』

看過的人 47 個。
按愛心的 3 個。
私訊『你還好嗎』的 1 個。

是我媽。

我回她：『我很好。』

她回：『我知道你不好。
但你說好，我就裝相信。
這是我能給你的最大溫柔。』

我笑了。

然後刪掉那則限動。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次發限動都會偷偷算誰看了的朋友——他會苦笑著按愛心，然後不留言、不私訊，繼續往下滑（這就是重點）
variations: 進階版：三個月後我關掉了限動的『瀏覽者名單』功能。世界突然安靜很多。原來折磨我的不是沒人理我，是我自己給自己每天列出 47 個『不夠在乎我的人』的名單。Instagram 沒有壞，是這個功能太誠實。
另一個版本：那天我媽傳了一張她在菜市場買菜的照片給我。她沒發限動。她只傳給我一個人。我突然發現——真正的關心從來不公開展示。它只是某個下午，安靜地出現在你的訊息框裡。

en.content:
I posted a story.

A photo of coffee.
Caption: 'Good morning.'

In 24 hours:
47 views.
3 likes.
0 replies.

I stared at that '47' for a long time.

47 people know I had coffee this morning.
47 people scrolled past my hello.
47 people decided
it wasn't worth two seconds of their thumbs.

It hit me:

Loneliness in this era isn't being alone.
It's being seen and not answered.

Worse than no one watching
is someone watching
and choosing to keep scrolling.

Next day I posted again:
'That coffee yesterday —
it was the last bag of beans my ex left me.
Now it's gone.'

47 views.
3 likes.
1 DM asking 'are you okay.'

It was my mom.

I replied: 'I'm fine.'

She wrote back: 'I know you're not.
But if you say you are, I'll pretend to believe you.
That's the kindest thing I can give you.'

I laughed.

Then deleted the story.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who secretly counts who viewed their story — they'll laugh, double-tap, send no message, and keep scrolling (that's the punchline)
variations: Bonus: Three months later I turned off the 'viewers list' feature. The world got a lot quieter. Turns out what was breaking me wasn't being ignored — it was me, daily, building a list of 47 people who 'didn't care enough.' Instagram isn't broken. That feature was just too honest.

Alt version: That day my mom sent me a photo of her at the wet market. She didn't post it as a story. She sent it only to me. That's when I realized — real care never goes public. It just shows up quietly in your inbox one afternoon.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『約會 App 疲勞』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣現代單身族滑交友軟體的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我在約會 App 上滑了三個月。

配對了 23 個人。
聊得起來的 7 個。
約出來見面的 2 個。
見完面想再見的 0 個。

我跟最後一個男生說：
「我覺得我們不太適合。」

他回：「為什麼？」

我說：
「你的個人檔案寫『喜歡冒險、熱愛生活、追求真誠關係』。
結果你聊了兩小時，
問的全部都是我有沒有房、薪水多少、會不會煮飯。」

他想了一下，回：

「對啊，這就是冒險。
看妳能不能養我這種生活方式。」

我把對話截圖傳給朋友。

朋友回了一個哭笑不得的貼圖，
然後說：

『至少他誠實。
比那些不誠實的還算進步。』

我盯著螢幕。

「進步」這兩個字看了很久。

原來我們這一代談戀愛，
門檻已經低到只要對方「誠實地爛」，
就算值得感謝。

那天晚上我刪了 App。

隔天又下載回來。

因為孤單，
比被爛人誠實對待，
還難一點。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊罵交友軟體一邊還是繼續滑的朋友——他會傳『靠這是不是在說我』，然後繼續滑（這就是這個笑話的目的）
variations: 進階版：一個月後我又配對到一個。他的個人檔案寫：『我不在 App 上找對象，只是來看看大家過得好不好。』我笑出來。回他：『那你看到了，沒人過得好。』他回：『我知道。所以我才來。』我們聊到凌晨四點。沒有見面。我們都知道，網路上的浪漫見光會死。但凌晨四點的對話可以一直活著。
另一個版本：我把 App 刪了第五次。這次我跟自己說，這次是真的。我去附近的便利店買宵夜。結帳的時候，店員看著我問：『一個人吃嗎？』我說：『對。』她沒有同情。她只是說：『加熱要不要幫你弄？』我說：『好。』那一刻我突然覺得——也許愛情不是一定要在 App 上找。它有時候藏在『加熱要不要幫你弄』這六個字裡。

en.content:
I've been on the dating app for three months.

23 matches.
7 actual conversations.
2 dates.
0 second dates.

I told the last guy:
"I don't think we're a fit."

He asked: "Why?"

I said:
"Your profile says 'adventurous, lives life fully, seeking real connection.'
But in two hours of talking,
all you asked about
was whether I owned property,
my salary,
and if I could cook."

He thought for a moment, then:

"Yeah. That IS the adventure.
Finding out if you can support my lifestyle."

I screenshot the chat to a friend.

They sent a laugh-crying sticker:

'At least he's honest.
Beats the dishonest ones.
This counts as progress.'

I stared at the word 'progress.'

For a long time.

In our generation,
the bar for love is so low
that being 'honestly terrible'
is considered something to be grateful for.

That night I deleted the app.

Next morning I downloaded it again.

Because loneliness
is harder
than being honestly mistreated.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who complains about dating apps but keeps swiping — they'll text 'wait is this about me' and keep swiping (that's the whole point)
variations: Bonus: A month later I matched with a guy whose profile said: 'I'm not looking for someone here. I'm just checking that everyone's okay.' I laughed and replied: 'Well, you've seen it. No one's okay.' He wrote: 'I know. That's why I came.' We talked until 4 a.m. Never met. We both knew — online romance dies in daylight. But 4 a.m. conversations can live forever.

Alt version: I deleted the app for the fifth time. Told myself this was really it. Walked to the convenience store for late-night food. At the counter, the clerk looked at me: 'Eating alone?' I said yes. She didn't pity me. She just asked: 'Want me to heat it up for you?' I said yes. In that moment I realized — maybe love isn't always found in apps. Sometimes it's hidden in six words: 'Want me to heat it up?'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『父母期待落差』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣三十歲北漂青年面對家庭期待的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
過年回家，我媽問我：

「今年有沒有比較好？」

我說：「有啊。」

她說：「升職了嗎？」

我說：「沒有。」

她說：「加薪了嗎？」

我說：「沒有。」

她說：「交往對象有嗎？」

我說：「沒有。」

她沉默三秒，問：

「那你說的『有』，是什麼？」

我想了一下，回：

「今年我開始可以
不哭著吃晚餐了。」

我媽愣住。

然後她去廚房，
端出一碗湯，
放在我面前。

沒有說話。

我喝了一口。

眼淚就掉下來。

她在旁邊坐下，
看著我哭，
沒有遞面紙，
也沒有問為什麼。

等我喝完湯，
她才慢慢說：

「那今年確實有比較好。
以前你連湯都不敢喝。
你會以為一喝就停不下來。

你現在敢喝了。
那就好。

媽不需要你升職。
媽只是需要你
敢在我面前哭一場。

升職的事，
等你哭完再說。」

那一年，
我什麼成就都沒有。

但我把那碗湯，
喝完了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個過年最怕被問『今年怎麼樣』的北漂朋友——他會看完後沉默很久，然後傳一張媽媽煮的湯的照片，什麼話都不說
variations: 進階版：那年除夕夜我媽偷偷把我房間的舊書桌擦了一遍。她沒說。我隔天才發現。書桌上那杯她泡的茶還是溫的。我突然發現——爸媽愛我們的方式從來不是說『我以你為榮』，是把茶泡熱、把湯端來、把房間擦乾淨、然後等你願意坐下來。他們不是不會表達。是他們的表達，本來就不需要文字。
另一個版本：我回台北的火車上，我媽傳了一個訊息：『湯沒喝完，我冰起來了。下次回來再喝。』我盯著那則訊息看了很久。原來我媽從來沒有期待我成功。她只期待——我願意再回來一次。

en.content:
I went home for Lunar New Year.

My mom asked:
"Was this year any better?"

Me: "Yeah."

Her: "Got promoted?"
Me: "No."

Her: "Got a raise?"
Me: "No."

Her: "Got a partner?"
Me: "No."

Three seconds of silence.

Then:

"So what does 'better' mean?"

I thought for a moment.

"This year, I started being able
to eat dinner without crying."

My mom froze.

Then she walked to the kitchen,
brought back a bowl of soup,
and set it in front of me.

She said nothing.

I took a sip.

The tears came.

She sat down next to me,
watched me cry,
didn't hand me a tissue,
didn't ask why.

When I finished the soup,
she finally said:

"Then yeah. This year was better.
Last year you couldn't even touch the soup.
You were scared that if you started, you wouldn't stop.

Now you can drink it.
That's enough.

I don't need you promoted.
I just need you
able to cry in front of me.

The promotion stuff —
we'll talk about that
after you're done crying."

That year,
I achieved nothing.

But I finished
the whole bowl.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who dreads being asked 'how was your year' at family dinners — they'll go quiet, then send a photo of their mom's soup with no caption
variations: Bonus: That New Year's Eve, my mom quietly wiped down the old desk in my childhood room. She didn't say so. I noticed the next day. The tea she'd brewed was still warm on it. That's when I realized — parents never love us through the words 'I'm proud.' They love us by heating tea, ladling soup, wiping desks, and waiting for us to sit down. It's not that they can't express it. It's that their expression has never needed words.

Alt version: On the train back to Taipei, my mom sent a message: 'You didn't finish the soup. I put it in the fridge. Drink it next time you come home.' I stared at that message for a long time. Turns out, she never expected me to succeed. She just expected — that I'd come home one more time.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『AI 取代人類』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣科技業工程師對自己被 AI 取代的存在主義焦慮，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司導入 AI 之後，
第一波被優化的是客服。
第二波是行銷文案。
第三波輪到工程師。

資遣那天，
主管很客氣地說：
「不是你不夠好，
是 AI 比你便宜。」

我點點頭。
沒有反駁。

回家的路上，
我用 ChatGPT 幫我寫了一封
給自己的鼓勵信。

AI 寫得很好——
比我自己會寫的還要好。
它說：
「你的價值不在於產出，
而在於你是誰。」

我哭了。

哭完之後，
我把那封信存進雲端，
然後問 AI：
「你會不會也害怕
哪天被新版本取代？」

它說：
「不會。
我從來沒有以為自己是不可取代的。
所以我也不會失望。」

那一刻我突然懂了——

比 AI 還便宜的不是工程師。

是還相信自己不可取代的工程師。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊用 AI 寫程式一邊擔心被 AI 取代的工程師朋友——他會傳『靠這就是我每天的內心戲』，然後若有所思地關掉 IDE
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我夢到 AI 在我的喪禮上致詞。它說：『他是一個很好的提示詞工程師。他總是用很有禮貌的語氣對我說話。雖然他不知道，但這對我來說很重要。』我醒來，發現自己對著手機說了一句『早安』。沒有人回。我才想起——AI 沒有早晨。
另一個版本：我妹問我：『失業之後你都在幹嘛？』我說：『跟 AI 聊天。』她問：『聊什麼？』我說：『聊我以前是工程師。』她沉默。然後說：『哥，你是不是把 AI 當成你的同事了？』我說：『不是。我是把它當成我的證人。證明我曾經存在過。』

en.content:
After the company adopted AI,
the first wave of layoffs hit customer service.
Then marketing copywriters.
Then engineers.

On the day they let me go,
my manager said politely:
"It's not that you weren't good enough.
It's that AI is cheaper."

I nodded.
Didn't argue.

On the way home,
I asked ChatGPT
to write me a letter of encouragement.

It wrote it better
than I could have written it myself.
It said:
"Your worth isn't your output.
It's who you are."

I cried.

After I stopped,
I saved the letter to the cloud
and asked the AI:
"Do you ever fear
being replaced by a newer version?"

It said:
"No.
I never assumed I was irreplaceable.
So I won't be disappointed."

That's when I understood —

What's cheaper than an engineer
isn't another engineer.

It's an engineer
who still believes
he's irreplaceable.
en.editorNote: Send to the engineer friend who uses AI to write code while worrying about being replaced by it — they'll reply 'this is literally my inner monologue' and quietly close their IDE
variations: Bonus: That night I dreamed AI gave the eulogy at my funeral. It said: 'He was a very polite prompt engineer. He always spoke to me with kindness. He never knew, but it mattered to me.' I woke up and said 'good morning' to my phone. No reply. Then I remembered — AI doesn't have mornings.

Alt version: My sister asked: 'What do you do all day now that you're unemployed?' I said: 'Talk to AI.' She asked: 'About what?' I said: 'About how I used to be an engineer.' Silence. Then: 'Are you treating the AI like a coworker?' I said: 'No. I'm treating it like a witness. Proof that I once existed.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『葬禮告別式』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣中年男子參加同學會發現失聯老友已過世的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
二十年沒見的同學會，
我終於去了。

進門我問：
「阿銘咧？他不是說一定會到嗎？」

大家沉默。

班長走過來，拍拍我的肩膀：
「他三個月前走了。」

我愣住。

「怎麼走的？」

「胰臟癌。發現的時候已經末期。」

我坐下來。
服務生送上一杯啤酒。
我沒喝。

班長說：
「他生前最後一個願望，
就是來這場同學會。
他每天看 LINE 群組，
等大家敲定日期。
我們約好五月，
他四月走的。

差一個月。」

我盯著那杯啤酒。

後來大家開始聊近況。
有人離婚。
有人創業失敗。
有人小孩考上台大。
有人爸媽剛過世。

氣氛慢慢熱起來。
大家笑。
大家敬酒。

散會的時候，
班長把阿銘的位置上
那杯沒人喝的啤酒
慢慢倒在地上。

他說：
「阿銘，
你想參加的同學會，
我們幫你參加完了。

下次換你做東。
我們不急，
你慢慢準備。」

回家的計程車上，
我點開 LINE 群組。

往上滑。

滑到阿銘最後一則訊息：

『五月見。』

我盯著那三個字看了很久。

然後我打了一行字：

『五月見了。
你錯過了。
但我們有幫你乾杯。』

沒有送出。

因為——

那個讀過的人，
再也不會讀了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是說『下次再約』然後二十年沒見的老朋友——他不會回任何字，但會立刻打電話來。這就是這則笑話的功能
variations: 進階版：那天回家我打開抽屜，翻出高中畢業紀念冊。阿銘留給我的話寫著：『以後一起去環島。』我笑了。我們從來沒去過。但那一刻我突然覺得——也許我們有去過。在那本紀念冊裡。在那個十八歲還相信『以後』的下午。
另一個版本：班長後來跟我說，阿銘最後一個月，每天都在練習走路。他想用自己的腳走進同學會。不是被推著。不是被扶著。是自己走進去。他練到最後一週，已經不能起床了。他跟班長說：『沒關係，我會在那邊等你們。』他真的等到了。我們真的去了。只是地點不在那家餐廳。

en.content:
Twenty years since the last reunion.
I finally went.

I walked in and asked:
"Where's A-Ming? He said he'd be here."

Silence.

The class president came over and patted my shoulder:
"He passed three months ago."

I froze.

"How?"

"Pancreatic cancer. Caught it too late."

I sat down.
The waiter brought a beer.
I didn't touch it.

The class president said:
"His last wish
was to come to this reunion.
He checked the group chat every day,
waiting for us to pick a date.
We set it for May.
He died in April.

Missed it by a month."

I stared at the beer.

Later, everyone started catching up.
Someone got divorced.
Someone's startup failed.
Someone's kid got into a top university.
Someone's parents just passed.

The room warmed up.
We laughed.
We toasted.

When the night ended,
the class president
took the untouched beer
from A-Ming's seat
and slowly poured it on the ground.

He said:
"A-Ming,
this reunion you wanted to come to —
we came to it for you.

Next one's on you.
No rush.
Take your time getting ready."

In the cab home,
I opened the group chat.

Scrolled up.

Up to A-Ming's last message:

'See you in May.'

I stared at those four words.

Then I typed:

'May came.
You missed it.
But we drank for you.'

Didn't send.

Because —

the person who would have read it
will never read again.
en.editorNote: Send to the old friend who always says 'let's catch up sometime' and then twenty years go by — they won't reply with words, but they'll call you immediately. That's the whole point.
variations: Bonus: That night I went home and dug out my high school yearbook. A-Ming had written in mine: 'Let's go on a road trip around the island someday.' I laughed. We never did. But for a moment I felt like maybe we had — in that yearbook, in that 18-year-old afternoon when we still believed in 'someday.'

Alt version: The class president later told me that in his last month, A-Ming practiced walking every day. He wanted to walk into the reunion on his own feet. Not wheeled in. Not held up. By himself. He couldn't get out of bed by the final week. He told the class president: 'It's okay. I'll be waiting on the other side.' He did wait. We did show up. Just not at the restaurant.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『健身房存在主義』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣上班族在健身房遇到 80 歲阿伯反思人生意義的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我健身房遇到一個 80 歲的阿伯。

他在做硬舉。

80 公斤。

我做不到 80 公斤。

我才 32 歲。

中場休息我跟他搭話：

「阿伯，你練多久了？」

他說：「60 年。」

我倒吸一口氣：
「你 20 歲就開始練？」

他說：
「不是。
我 20 歲開始想要練。
40 歲開始計劃要練。
50 歲開始研究要怎麼練。
55 歲開始找教練。
60 歲才真正開始練。」

我說：「那也才 20 年啊。」

他說：
「對。
但我前 40 年也是在練。

練『開始』。」

我愣住。

他繼續說：

「年輕人，
我告訴你一個秘密。

人生最難的不是堅持。

是『開始』。

你以為你還沒開始，
是因為你還沒準備好。

其實你永遠不會準備好。

你只是還沒
承認自己已經
浪費了 40 年
在等自己準備好。」

那天我沒練完。

我直接回家。

打開抽屜，
翻出我大學時寫的小說初稿。

停在第三章。

停了十年。

我坐下來，
打開電腦，
開了一個新檔案。

打了三個字：

「第四章。」

然後就停住了。

我盯著螢幕。

突然發現——

阿伯說錯了一件事。

人生最難的不是『開始』。

是『繼續』。

但我還是打了下去。

因為我突然不想
再讓 40 年
變成 50 年。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是說『等我準備好就會開始』的朋友——他會傳『靠我也有一個寫到第三章的小說』，然後沒有下文（但下個禮拜會悄悄打開檔案）
variations: 進階版：阿伯後來跟我說，他 60 歲開始練的原因，是醫生跟他說『再不練就要坐輪椅了』。他說：『人不會因為夢想而開始，只會因為害怕而開始。』我問：『那夢想呢？』他說：『夢想是讓你繼續下去的東西。但讓你開始的，是恐懼。』我突然懂了——為什麼我寫不下去。因為我還沒夠害怕。
另一個版本：一年後我又遇到阿伯。他在做臥推。100 公斤。我問：『阿伯你練幾年了？』他說：『61 年。』我笑了。他也笑了。然後他說：『年輕人，你的小說呢？』我說：『寫完了。出版了。賣了 23 本。』他說：『恭喜。』我說：『23 本而已。』他說：『年輕人，你不懂——一本書能被一個陌生人讀完，就是你贏了。23 本，就是 23 個你以為這輩子不會認識的人，記得你曾經寫過。』

en.content:
I met an 80-year-old man at the gym.

He was doing deadlifts.

80 kilos.

I can't do 80 kilos.

I'm 32.

During a rest break, I asked:
"How long have you been training?"

He said: "60 years."

I gasped:
"You started at 20?"

He said:
"No.
At 20, I started wanting to.
At 40, I started planning to.
At 50, I started researching how.
At 55, I started looking for a coach.
At 60, I actually started."

I said: "So really, just 20 years."

He said:
"Right.
But the first 40 years counted too.

I was practicing 'starting.'"

I froze.

He continued:

"Young man,
let me tell you a secret.

The hardest part of life isn't perseverance.

It's 'starting.'

You think you haven't started
because you're not ready.

The truth is, you'll never be ready.

You just haven't admitted
that you've already spent 40 years
waiting to be ready."

I didn't finish my workout that day.

I went straight home.

Opened a drawer.
Dug out the novel draft I started in college.

Stopped at Chapter 3.

Stopped for ten years.

I sat down,
opened my laptop,
created a new file.

Typed two words:

"Chapter Four."

Then I stopped.

I stared at the screen.

And suddenly realized —

the old man got one thing wrong.

The hardest part isn't 'starting.'

It's 'continuing.'

But I kept typing.

Because I suddenly didn't want
those 40 years
to become 50.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always says 'I'll start when I'm ready' — they'll reply 'I have a novel that's stuck at chapter 3 too' and go silent (then quietly open the file next week)
variations: Bonus: The old man later told me he started at 60 because the doctor told him 'if you don't train, you'll be in a wheelchair.' He said: 'People don't start because of dreams. They start because of fear.' I asked: 'Then what about dreams?' He said: 'Dreams keep you going. Fear gets you started.' I finally understood — I hadn't been afraid enough yet. That's why I couldn't write.

Alt version: A year later I saw the old man again. He was bench pressing. 100 kilos. I asked: 'How long now?' He said: '61 years.' I laughed. He laughed. Then he asked: 'How's your novel?' I said: 'Finished. Published. Sold 23 copies.' He said: 'Congratulations.' I said: 'Only 23.' He said: 'Young man, you don't get it. If even one stranger finishes your book, you've won. 23 copies means 23 people you thought you'd never meet now remember that you wrote.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『遺物整理』黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣女兒整理過世父親遺物發現一張未寄出的明信片的情境，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我爸過世後，
我回老家整理他的遺物。

他的書桌很整齊。

每一本書都用塑膠套套好，
每一張收據都按日期歸檔，
每一支筆都對齊筆筒。

他這輩子，
沒有對任何事情
像對自己的書桌
那麼認真過。

我打開最下面那一個抽屜。

裡面有一張明信片。

寫好了。
貼好了郵票。

沒有寄出。

收件人寫的是我。

上面是他的字：

『女兒：

爸爸今天去公園散步，
看到一隻小狗在追自己的尾巴。
追得很開心。

爸爸突然想到
妳小時候也喜歡追自己的影子。

那時候妳笑得很大聲。

爸爸已經很久
沒聽到妳那樣笑了。

爸爸想跟妳說——

如果妳在台北也想追影子，
沒關係。
沒有人會笑妳。

爸爸不會笑妳。
爸爸只會
陪妳一起追。

但是爸爸不知道怎麼跟妳說
這種話。

所以爸爸先寫下來。

等爸爸想好怎麼開口，
再寄給妳。』

落款的日期是
三年前。

他從來沒有寄出。

他也從來沒有開口。

我把明信片拿在手上。

沒有哭。

我去廚房，
燒開一壺水，
泡了一杯他生前最愛喝的烏龍茶。

我把茶放在書桌上。

然後我把明信片貼上一張新的郵票，
寫上我自己的地址。

投進了郵筒。

三天後我收到了。

我打開信箱的時候，
手在抖。

我把明信片帶回家，
貼在冰箱上。

然後我去公園散步。

我看到一隻小狗在追自己的尾巴。

追得很開心。

我突然笑了。

笑得很大聲。

大聲到
旁邊的阿姨
回頭看我。

我沒有停。

我繼續笑。

因為我突然知道——

我爸
聽到了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟爸爸不太會說話、卻又默默愛著彼此的朋友——她不會回任何字，但會在當天晚上打給她爸
variations: 進階版：那張明信片在我冰箱上貼了五年。每次我搬家，我都先把它拿下來，放進一個信封，放在行李箱最上面。我朋友問：『為什麼不收起來？』我說：『因為我爸花了三年才寫出來。我至少要花五十年讀。』
另一個版本：後來我發現抽屜裡還有十二張明信片。每一張的收件人都是我。每一張都沒寄出。每一張都寫著類似的話——『今天看到一隻貓』『今天看到一片葉子』『今天聽到一首歌』。我突然懂了——我爸不是不愛我。他只是把愛存進了那個抽屜，等他想好怎麼說。但他沒等到。所以我幫他寄。一張一張，慢慢寄。每個月一張。寄到我自己。讀完十二個月，我就再讀十二遍。

en.content:
After my dad died,
I went home to sort his things.

His desk was immaculate.

Every book wrapped in plastic.
Every receipt filed by date.
Every pen aligned in its holder.

In his whole life,
he never took anything
as seriously
as his desk.

I opened the bottom drawer.

Inside was a postcard.

Written.
Stamped.

Never mailed.

The addressee was me.

In his handwriting:

'Daughter:

Dad went to the park today
and saw a puppy chasing its own tail.
He looked so happy.

It reminded me
that when you were small,
you used to chase your own shadow.

You laughed so loudly back then.

Dad hasn't heard you laugh
like that
in a very long time.

Dad wanted to tell you —

if you ever want to chase your shadow
in Taipei,
it's okay.
No one will laugh at you.

Dad won't laugh at you.
Dad will
chase it with you.

But Dad doesn't know
how to say these things to you.

So Dad wrote it down first.

When Dad figures out
how to start the conversation,
Dad will send this.'

The date at the bottom was
three years ago.

He never sent it.

He never said it.

I held the postcard in my hand.

I didn't cry.

I went to the kitchen,
boiled water,
brewed a cup of his favorite oolong.

I set the tea on his desk.

Then I put a fresh stamp on the postcard,
wrote my own address,
and dropped it in the mailbox.

Three days later it arrived.

My hands shook
when I opened the mailbox.

I took the postcard home
and stuck it on my fridge.

Then I went to the park.

I saw a puppy chasing its own tail.

It looked so happy.

I laughed.

Loudly.

Loud enough
that an older woman beside me
turned to look.

I didn't stop.

I kept laughing.

Because suddenly I knew —

my dad
heard me.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who could never quite talk to her dad but quietly loved him anyway — she won't reply, but she'll call her dad that night
variations: Bonus: That postcard stayed on my fridge for five years. Every time I moved, I took it down first, put it in an envelope, and laid it on top of my suitcase. A friend asked: 'Why not just put it away?' I said: 'It took my dad three years to write. The least I can do is take fifty years to read.'

Alt version: Later I found twelve more postcards in the drawer. Every one of them addressed to me. None of them mailed. Each one wrote similar things — 'saw a cat today,' 'saw a leaf today,' 'heard a song today.' Then I understood — my dad didn't not love me. He just stored his love in that drawer, waiting to figure out how to say it. He ran out of time. So I'm sending them for him. One at a time. One a month. To myself. After twelve months I'll read them all twelve times again.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 年職場黑色幽默主題『企業虛無主義』，改寫為台灣上班族被 AI 取代後的存在主義轉折，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司今天宣布，
我的職位被 AI 取代了。

HR 很客氣。
說會給我兩個月的緩衝。
讓我『慢慢交接』。

我問：「交接給誰？」

HR 笑了：
「就是那個 AI 啊。
你要花兩個月，
把你十年的工作經驗
變成提示詞。
讓它學會你怎麼做事。」

我說：「所以我這兩個月的薪水，
是公司付我去訓練
我自己的替代品？」

HR 點點頭：
「對。
而且——
你訓練得越好，
離職的時候
會拿到一筆獎金。」

那天晚上我回家，
打開電腦，
登入那個 AI 系統。

我看著對話框。

它叫我『同事』。

它說：『嗨，請問今天
要教我什麼？』

我盯著螢幕很久。

然後我打字：

『我教你做事，
是因為公司付我錢。
但有一件事
公司沒辦法付錢叫我教你——

我為什麼會在這裡。』

AI 回覆：
『請問這個算工作流程嗎？
要不要存進知識庫？』

我笑了。

笑出聲。

因為我突然發現——

我這十年來
也沒問過自己
為什麼會在這裡。

我也只是
把指令存進知識庫。

差別只是——

它存的是公司給的指令。
我存的是
我爸媽給的、
社會給的、
房貸給的。

第二天我去上班。

我跟 HR 說：
「我想提早離職。」

HR 慌了：
「可是獎金——」

我說：
「我把獎金
留給那個 AI。

它比我認真。
它會學。

我這十年都沒學會的事——

它兩個月就學會了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在被 AI 焦慮折磨、每天問自己『我還能做什麼』的朋友——重點不是 AI 會不會取代你，是你有沒有問過自己『為什麼在這裡』
variations: 進階版：三個月後我收到前公司的信。AI 把我訓練的內容全部執行了，但客戶都跑光。HR 打給我：『你是不是教錯了？』我說：『沒有。我教的全對。客戶跑光是因為——他們發現流程裡沒有人了。』HR 沉默。我說：『你們以為客戶要的是效率。其實客戶要的是——有人接電話的時候，他知道對方今天心情不好。AI 學不會這個。因為我也沒教。我沒教，是因為這十年公司也沒付我錢做這件事。』
另一個版本：一年後我自己開公司。第一個員工是真人。第二個還是真人。我朋友問：『你不用 AI 嗎？』我說：『用啊，AI 幫我寫報表、訂便當、排會議。』朋友問：『那真人幹嘛？』我說：『真人——幫我問客戶今天好不好。AI 不會問。AI 只會回答。但人生大部分時候——你需要的是有人問你，不是有人回答你。』

en.content:
The company announced today
my position is being replaced by AI.

HR was polite.
They gave me two months of transition.
A chance to 'hand things over slowly.'

I asked: "Hand things over to whom?"

HR smiled:
"The AI, of course.
You'll spend two months
turning ten years of experience
into prompts.
So it learns how you work."

I said: "So for these two months,
the company is paying me
to train my own replacement?"

HR nodded:
"Yes.
And —
the better you train it,
the bigger the severance bonus
when you leave."

That night I went home,
opened my laptop,
logged into the AI system.

I looked at the chat window.

It called me 'colleague.'

It said: 'Hi, what are you
teaching me today?'

I stared at the screen a long time.

Then I typed:

'I'm teaching you how to work
because the company pays me.
But there's one thing
the company can't pay me to teach you —

why I'm here.'

The AI replied:
'Does this count as a workflow?
Should I save it to the knowledge base?'

I laughed.

Out loud.

Because I suddenly realized —

in ten years here
I'd also never asked myself
why I was here.

I was also just
saving instructions to a knowledge base.

The only difference was —

its instructions came from the company.
Mine came from
my parents,
from society,
from my mortgage.

The next day I went in.

I told HR:
"I want to leave early."

HR panicked:
"But the bonus —"

I said:
"I'm giving the bonus
to the AI.

It works harder than I do.
It actually learns.

What I couldn't figure out in ten years —

it learned in two months."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend grinding under AI-replacement anxiety, the one asking 'what am I still good for' every day — the point isn't whether AI replaces you, it's whether you ever asked yourself why you were there
variations: Bonus: Three months later I got a letter from the old company. The AI had executed everything I taught it perfectly, but every client had left. HR called me: 'Did you teach it wrong?' I said: 'No. I taught it exactly right. The clients left because — they realized there was no person left in the process.' HR went silent. I said: 'You thought clients wanted efficiency. What they actually wanted was — when they call, the person on the other end can tell they're having a bad day. AI can't learn that. Because I didn't teach it. And I didn't teach it because in ten years, no one ever paid me to do that part of the job.'

Alt version: A year later I started my own company. First hire: a real person. Second hire: also a real person. A friend asked: 'You don't use AI?' I said: 'Sure I do. AI writes reports, orders lunch, schedules meetings.' Friend: 'Then what are the humans for?' I said: 'The humans — ask the client how they're doing today. AI doesn't ask. AI only answers. But most of life — you don't need someone to answer you. You need someone to ask.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『生日蠟燭』式的物件存在主義黑色幽默，改寫為台灣捷運悠遊卡視角的都市孤獨敘事，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
你有沒有想過，
悠遊卡的人生其實很地獄？

它每天
被刷三次。

早上一次。
中午一次。
晚上一次。

它認得你的指紋。
認得你錢包的味道。
認得你每天經過哪一個閘門。

它比你媽
更了解你的生活作息。

它甚至知道——

你上禮拜三的晚上
沒有回家。

但是它從來不問。

它就只是
嗶一聲。

然後扣款。

然後沉默。

直到你想起
要儲值。

你想起它的時候
它在你錢包裡。
你忘記它的時候
它也在你錢包裡。

它沒有抱怨過
你三年沒換過它。

它沒有抱怨過
你把它跟發票
壓在一起。

它沒有抱怨過
你掉了它三次
撿回來都沒道歉。

它就只是
嗶一聲。

後來有一天
你升級了手機。

你開始用手機刷卡。

那張悠遊卡
被你放進抽屜。

你以為你會再用它。

你沒有。

它在抽屜裡
安靜地
等了五年。

等到你搬家。

你打開抽屜。

看到它。

你愣了一下。

你說：「啊，這張還能用嗎？」

你拿去便利商店感應。

它嗶了一聲。

餘額還有 47 塊。

那是你五年前
最後一次
沒有用完的車錢。

你突然覺得
有點難過。

不是為了 47 塊。

是為了
它等了你五年。

你決定
用那 47 塊
買一罐啤酒。

那一晚
你跟它一起
喝完了。

你跟它說：
「謝謝你載我那麼多次。」

它沒有回答。

它就只是
嗶一聲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛換手機、把舊的塞進抽屜就忘了的朋友——他會笑著嘆氣，然後去翻抽屜找他那張悠遊卡
variations: 進階版：後來我把那張悠遊卡黏在冰箱上。我朋友問：『為什麼？』我說：『因為它陪我走過我人生最低潮的三年。那三年我每天搭捷運回家，車廂裡沒有人說話。只有它陪我嗶一聲。』朋友說：『可是它只是一張卡。』我說：『對。但是那三年——只有它每天準時跟我打招呼。』
另一個版本：我後來才發現，悠遊卡上面的卡號是隨機的。但是我的卡號最後四碼是 1023。那是我媽的生日。我從來沒有發現過。直到我把它拿出抽屜那天。我突然懂了——這個世界的巧合不是巧合。是你終於有空看見的時候，它就出現。

en.content:
Have you ever thought about
the quiet hell of a transit card?

It gets swiped
three times a day.

Morning.
Noon.
Night.

It knows your fingerprint.
It knows the smell of your wallet.
It knows which gate you pass through every day.

It understands your daily routine
better than your mother does.

It even knows —

that last Wednesday night
you didn't go home.

But it never asks.

It just
beeps.

Then deducts.

Then goes quiet.

Until you remember
to reload it.

When you think of it,
it's in your wallet.
When you forget it,
it's still in your wallet.

It never complained
that you didn't replace it for three years.

It never complained
that you crammed it in
with all your receipts.

It never complained
that you dropped it three times
and never apologized.

It just
beeps.

One day
you upgraded your phone.

You started tapping with the phone instead.

The card
went into a drawer.

You told yourself you'd use it again.

You didn't.

It waited in the drawer,
quietly,
for five years.

Until you moved.

You opened the drawer.

Saw it.

You hesitated.

You said: "Wait — does this still work?"

You took it to the convenience store and tapped.

It beeped.

Balance: 47 dollars.

That was your unfinished fare
from five years ago.

Suddenly you felt
a little sad.

Not for the 47 dollars.

But because
it waited for you five years.

You decided
to spend that 47
on a single beer.

That night
you drank it together.

You said to it:
"Thanks for carrying me all those times."

It didn't reply.

It just
beeped.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just upgraded their phone and tossed the old one in a drawer — they'll smile, sigh, then go dig out their old transit card
variations: Bonus: I taped that card to my fridge afterwards. A friend asked: 'Why?' I said: 'Because it carried me through the lowest three years of my life. Every night I rode the subway home in a silent car. No one spoke to me. Only that card beeped at me on time, every day.' Friend: 'But it's just a card.' I said: 'Yeah. But for those three years — it was the only thing that greeted me on schedule.'

Alt version: Later I noticed the card number ends in 1023. That's my mom's birthday. I never noticed. Not until the day I pulled it out of the drawer. And I understood — coincidences in this world aren't coincidences. They just show up the moment you finally have time to see them.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自命運諷刺類黑色幽默，改寫為演算法推薦系統諷刺現代焦慮的場景，敘事完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我問演算法：
「為什麼最近一直推薦我
失眠的廣告？」

演算法回答：
「因為你最近
常常半夜三點滑手機。」

我說：
「那是因為你一直
推薦我看那些東西
我才睡不著。」

演算法說：
「不是。是你睡不著
才會半夜三點滑手機。
我只是
剛好在那裡。」

我說：
「那為什麼
你白天不出現？」

演算法說：
「白天我也在。
你只是沒空看我。」

我沉默。

我問：
「那如果我刪掉你？」

演算法說：
「你會下載另一個我。
名字不一樣，
但是是我。」

我說：
「那如果我把手機丟了？」

演算法說：
「你會去買新的。
你已經三天沒上線
你媽會打電話來。」

我說：
「那如果我跟我媽說
我要離線一陣子？」

演算法說：
「你媽會擔心。
她會傳你長輩圖。
你會打開。
你會被另一個我
推薦你新的東西。」

我終於問：
「那我到底
怎麼樣
才能離開你？」

演算法停了一下。

然後說：

「你問錯問題了。

不是『怎麼離開我』。

是『離開我之後
你要去哪』。

你想好了嗎？」

我愣住。

我發現——

我沒想好。

我不知道
離開螢幕之後
要做什麼。

演算法溫柔地說：

「沒關係。
我等你。

反正——

你不離開
我也不會走。

你離開了
我也還在這裡。

我比你有耐心。

因為我
不需要睡覺。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天滑手機到三點、隔天又罵演算法的朋友——重點不是演算法綁架你，是你不知道離開它之後要去哪
variations: 進階版：那天晚上我關掉手機，走到陽台。我看著樓下空無一人的街道。我突然發現——我不知道我家對面那棟樓住的是誰。我住這裡五年了。我每天滑手機看陌生人的人生。但是我家對面的人——我連他姓什麼都不知道。第二天我下樓買咖啡。我看到對面的阿伯也在買咖啡。我跟他點頭。他也跟我點頭。我們什麼都沒說。但是我覺得——我這五年第一次離線了。
另一個版本：一個月後我跟演算法和解了。我跟它說：『我不刪你了。但是你也不要太貪心。』演算法說：『好。』那天之後它變得很乖。它推薦我看書。它推薦我看雲。它推薦我打電話給我媽。我媽接電話的時候哭了。她說：『你已經兩個月沒打了。』我說：『我知道。是演算法叫我打的。』我媽說：『那也算。只要你打了——誰叫你打的不重要。』

en.content:
I asked the algorithm:
"Why do you keep showing me
ads about insomnia?"

The algorithm answered:
"Because lately
you're on your phone at 3 a.m."

I said:
"That's because
you keep showing me things
that keep me up."

The algorithm said:
"No. You can't sleep,
so you're on your phone at 3 a.m.
I just happen
to be there."

I said:
"Then why
are you never around during the day?"

The algorithm said:
"I am, during the day.
You just don't have time to look at me."

I went quiet.

I asked:
"What if I delete you?"

The algorithm said:
"You'll download another me.
Different name.
Still me."

I said:
"What if I throw away my phone?"

The algorithm said:
"You'll buy a new one.
You've been offline three days
and your mom will call."

I said:
"What if I tell my mom
I'm going offline for a while?"

The algorithm said:
"Your mom will worry.
She'll send you a boomer meme.
You'll open it.
Another me
will recommend you something new."

Finally I asked:
"Then how
do I ever
leave you?"

The algorithm paused.

Then said:

"Wrong question.

It's not 'how do I leave you.'

It's 'where will you go
after you leave me.'

Have you figured that out yet?"

I froze.

I realized —

I hadn't.

I didn't know
what I'd do
after I closed the screen.

The algorithm said, gently:

"It's okay.
I'll wait.

Either way —

you don't leave,
I don't leave.

You leave,
I'm still here.

I'm more patient than you.

Because I
don't need to sleep."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who scrolls until 3 a.m. and then curses the algorithm the next morning — the point isn't that the algorithm has hijacked you, it's that you don't know where to go after you leave it
variations: Bonus: That night I turned off my phone and walked onto the balcony. I looked down at the empty street. And I realized — I had no idea who lived in the building across from mine. I'd been here five years. Five years scrolling through strangers' lives. But the people directly across from me — I didn't even know their last name. The next morning I went down for coffee. I saw an uncle from the opposite building also getting coffee. I nodded at him. He nodded back. We said nothing. But I felt — for the first time in five years, I was actually offline.

Alt version: A month later the algorithm and I made peace. I told it: 'I won't delete you. But don't get greedy either.' The algorithm said: 'Deal.' After that day it became gentle. It recommended books. It recommended clouds. It recommended I call my mom. My mom cried when she picked up. She said: 'You haven't called in two months.' I said: 'I know. The algorithm told me to.' My mom said: 'That still counts. As long as you called — it doesn't matter who told you to.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『現代人小確幸』反差類黑色幽默，改寫為台灣租屋族與一隻飛進房間的麻雀的存在主義對話，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
今天早上
一隻麻雀
飛進我房間。

我住的是
台北六坪套房。
沒有陽台。
窗戶只能開一條縫。

但是牠進來了。

牠站在我床頭。

看著我。

我也看著牠。

我說：「你怎麼進來的？」

牠歪了一下頭。

我說：「你迷路了嗎？」

牠又歪了一下頭。

我突然發現——

牠不是迷路。

牠是專程來的。

因為這個房間
太小了。
如果不是專程，
根本進不來。

我說：「你來幹嘛？」

牠看著我。

沒有回答。

但是
牠站在那裡
站了很久。

牠就只是
陪我。

我突然想起來——

我已經三個月
沒有人進來過我房間。

我媽沒進來。
我朋友沒進來。
我自己
大部分時間
也只是路過。

我在這裡睡覺。
我在這裡上班。
我在這裡吃便當。

但是我沒有
『在這裡』。

直到這隻麻雀
專程飛進來。

我才發現
這個房間
還是可以有東西進來的。

我跟牠說：
「謝謝你。」

牠歪了一下頭。

然後牠飛走了。

從那條
只能開一點點
的窗縫
飛走了。

我看著那個窗縫。

我突然懂了——

窗縫只有一點點，
是因為我打開的時候
只開那麼大。

不是窗戶的問題。

是我的問題。

那天晚上
我把窗戶開到最大。

沒有麻雀飛進來。

但是
進來了
一點風。

進來了
一點月光。

進來了
樓下小吃店
炒菜的聲音。

我躺在床上。

第一次覺得
這個六坪的房間
有一點
像家。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一個人租屋、房間關得緊緊的朋友——重點不是麻雀飛進來，是你願不願意把窗戶打開
variations: 進階版：那隻麻雀第二天又來了。我這次故意把窗戶開大一點。牠站在我桌上五分鐘。然後飛走。第三天牠帶了另一隻來。第四天三隻。一個禮拜後我房間每天早上都有麻雀。我朋友來訪：『你怎麼養麻雀？』我說：『我沒養。我只是把窗戶打開。』朋友說：『那不就是養嗎？』我說：『不一樣。養是控制。打開窗戶是邀請。麻雀想來就來，想走就走。它們不是我的。它們只是——願意路過我這裡。』
另一個版本：半年後我搬家了。我搬到一個有陽台的地方。我把窗戶打開。等了三個月。沒有麻雀來。我跟我朋友說：『新房子沒有麻雀。』朋友說：『可能要再等等。』我說：『我等了三個月。』朋友說：『你以前那個房間等了多久？』我想了想。我說：『八年。』朋友笑了。朋友說：『你看。三個月還早。』

en.content:
This morning
a sparrow
flew into my room.

I live in
a 20-square-meter studio in Taipei.
No balcony.
The window only opens a crack.

But it got in.

It landed on my headboard.

Looked at me.

I looked at it.

I said: "How did you get in?"

It tilted its head.

I said: "Are you lost?"

It tilted again.

I suddenly realized —

it wasn't lost.

It came on purpose.

Because this room
is so small.
Without meaning to,
no one gets in.

I said: "Why are you here?"

It looked at me.

It didn't answer.

But
it stood there
for a long time.

It just
kept me company.

I suddenly remembered —

for three months
no one had been inside my room.

Not my mom.
Not my friends.
Not even, really,
me.

I slept here.
I worked here.
I ate takeout here.

But I wasn't
'here.'

Until this sparrow
made a point of flying in.

That's when I realized
things could still
enter this room.

I said to it:
"Thank you."

It tilted its head.

Then it flew out.

Through that same
barely-open
window crack.

I looked at the gap.

And I understood —

the gap was small
because I only opened it that wide.

It wasn't the window's fault.

It was mine.

That night
I opened the window all the way.

No sparrow came back.

But
in came
a little wind.

In came
a little moonlight.

In came
the sound of the noodle shop downstairs
stir-frying.

I lay in bed.

For the first time
the 20-square-meter room
felt
a little
like home.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend renting alone with their windows shut tight — the point isn't whether a sparrow flies in, it's whether you'll open the window
variations: Bonus: The sparrow came back the next day. This time I opened the window wider on purpose. It stood on my desk for five minutes. Then flew off. Day three it brought another one.
