en.editorNote: Send to the friend who feels their life is ordinary, with nothing big to show for it. It flips the bleak ending 'tried hard' into 'you were the source of joy in so many people's stories' — being ordinary isn't failure, and being someone others remember with a laugh is its own kind of greatness.
variations: Short version: "My life's ending is just two words: tried hard. But the middle part, I laughed a long time."
Follow-up: Fen asks: "Can I rewrite the script?" Fate: "You can, but this version is already the funniest. You sure?"

items.sourceNote: 改編自跳傘梗『跳傘不需要降落傘，跳第二次才需要』，重寫成地獄敘事

zh-tw.content:
阿凱第一次去跳傘，
教練在門口貼心提醒：
「第一次跳，其實不用帶降落傘。」

阿凱嚇到：「為什麼？」

教練：「因為降落傘是給
想跳第二次的人準備的。」

阿凱沉默了三秒：
「那第一次的人怎麼辦？」

教練拍拍他的肩：
「第一次的人，
通常不會有意見。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是衝動報名極限活動、卻從來不看安全須知的朋友。笑點在於把『安全裝備』反轉成『只有想活下來的人才需要』，先讓人笑，再讓人默默想去檢查保險有沒有過期
variations: 短版：「跳傘不用帶降落傘啦——除非你打算跳第二次。」
進階版：阿凱問：「那你怎麼跳這麼多次？」教練：「因為我每次都記得帶。」

en.content:
Kai showed up for his first skydive.
The instructor said helpfully at the door:
"First time? You don't actually need a parachute."

Kai panicked: "Why not?"

Instructor: "Because the parachute
is for people who want to jump
a second time."

Kai went quiet for three seconds:
"So what about the first-timers?"

The instructor patted his shoulder:
"First-timers
usually don't have complaints."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who impulsively signs up for extreme activities but never reads the safety briefing. The punchline flips 'safety gear' into 'only the people who want to survive need it' — laugh first, then quietly go check whether your life insurance is still valid.
variations: Short version: "You don't need a parachute to skydive — unless you're planning on a second jump."
Follow-up: Kai asks, "Then how have you jumped so many times?" Instructor: "Because I remember the parachute every time."

items.sourceNote: 改編自孤兒院『沒有首頁』雙關，重寫成中文情境地獄笑話

zh-tw.content:
工程師小杰失業後，
為了證明自己還行，
決定幫孤兒院架一個網站。

做完那天，院長很感動：
「謝謝你，可是——
為什麼沒有首頁（home）？」

小杰愣了一下，
認真地說：
「我本來想做，
但每次點進去，
它都自己跳回登入頁，
好像不知道家在哪。」

院長沉默了。
小杰也沉默了。

最後小杰小聲說：
「我懂這種感覺，
我失業之後也是。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛失業、或剛搬離家鄉一個人在外打拼的朋友。表面是 home page（首頁）的工程師雙關，底層其實在講『找不到歸屬感』——適合在對方說『最近好累、不知道為了什麼』的時候丟出來，笑完會有種被理解的暖意
variations: 短版：「我幫孤兒院做了網站，但它沒有首頁（home）——很合理。」
進階版：院長問：「那要怎麼回首頁？」小杰：「點『關於我們』，那裡至少有人。」

en.content:
After getting laid off,
Jay the engineer wanted to prove
he still had it,
so he built a website for an orphanage.

The day he finished, the director was moved:
"Thank you — but
why is there no home page?"

Jay paused, then said,
perfectly serious:
"I tried to make one,
but every time you click in,
it just bounces back to the login screen,
like it doesn't know where home is."

The director went silent.
Jay went silent too.

Finally Jay said quietly:
"I get it.
Same thing happened to me
after the layoff."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just lost a job, or just moved far from home and is grinding alone. On the surface it's the engineer's 'home page' pun, but underneath it's about not having a place that feels like yours. Good to drop when someone says 'I'm so tired lately, not even sure what for' — the laugh lands soft and understood.
variations: Short version: "I built a website for an orphanage. It has no home page. Checks out."
Follow-up: Director asks, "How do I get back to home?" Jay: "Click 'About Us.' At least there are people there."

items.sourceNote: 改編自『失業的笑話都不會 work』雙關，重寫成地獄敘事

zh-tw.content:
阿哲想當脫口秀演員，
寫了一整本關於失業的笑話。

他上台，講了三十分鐘，
台下一個人都沒笑。

下台後，朋友安慰他：
「沒關係啦，
可能是這些失業笑話
剛好都不會 work（運作／工作）。」

阿哲苦笑：
「對，跟我一樣。」

隔週，他真的失業了，
變成了自己段子的素材。

他說：
「至少現在，
我終於有資格講這些笑話了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一直說要追夢、但現實一直打臉的朋友。雙關在 work（笑話奏效／找到工作），把『笑話不好笑』和『人失業』疊在同一個詞上——適合在對方夢想暫時受挫、需要一點自嘲解圍的時候，提醒他低谷本身就是最好的創作素材
variations: 短版：「我有一堆關於失業的笑話，可惜它們跟我一樣，都不 work。」
進階版：朋友問：「那你下一場要講什麼？」阿哲：「講這場為什麼沒人笑，這個一定有人懂。」

en.content:
Zhe wanted to be a stand-up comedian,
so he wrote a whole set about unemployment.

He went up, did thirty minutes,
and not one person laughed.

Backstage, a friend consoled him:
"It's fine —
maybe those unemployment jokes
just didn't work."

Zhe gave a bitter smile:
"Yeah. Same as me."

The next week, he actually got laid off,
and became the material for his own act.

He said:
"At least now
I'm finally qualified
to tell these jokes."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend chasing a dream while reality keeps slapping them. The pun rides on 'work' (the joke landing / having a job), stacking 'the joke didn't work' onto 'the person is out of work.' Good for when someone's dream hits a wall and they need a self-deprecating laugh — a reminder that the low point is the best material there is.
variations: Short version: "I've got a ton of unemployment jokes. Sadly, like me, none of them work."
Follow-up: Friend asks, "So what's your next set about?" Zhe: "Why nobody laughed at this one. That one'll definitely connect."

items.sourceNote: 改編自墓園『大家都搶著要進去（dying to get in）』雙關，重寫成地獄敘事

zh-tw.content:
新開的墓園生意太好，
門口排了長長的隊。

業務員得意地說：
「你看，多搶手——
大家都『死』要進來。」

阿婷路過，疑惑地問：
「排這麼長，要等多久？」

業務員笑著：
「放心，
這裡的客人都很有耐心，
從來沒有人趕時間。」

阿婷想了想：
「那會不會額滿？」

業務員：
「不會，
因為走進去的人，
從來不會再出來佔位子。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總把『我快累死了』『塞到我想死』掛在嘴邊的朋友。把墓園的『搶著要進去』『不趕時間』反轉成黑色喜劇，先笑、再體會到一種『活著還能排隊抱怨已經很幸福』的荒謬感
variations: 短版：「這墓園超搶手，大家都死要進來。」
進階版：阿婷問：「有 VIP 通道嗎？」業務員：「有，叫做意外。」

en.content:
The new cemetery was doing such great business
that there was a long line at the gate.

The salesman said proudly:
"See how popular we are —
everyone's just dying to get in."

Ting walked by and asked, puzzled:
"This line's huge. How long's the wait?"

The salesman smiled:
"Don't worry,
our clients are very patient.
Nobody here is ever in a hurry."

Ting thought about it:
"Won't it fill up?"

Salesman:
"Never.
The ones who walk in
never come back out to hold their spot."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's always saying 'I'm dying of exhaustion' or 'the traffic was killing me.' It flips the cemetery's 'dying to get in' and 'never in a hurry' into black comedy — laugh first, then feel the absurd relief that being alive enough to stand in line and complain is its own kind of luck.
variations: Short version: "This cemetery is so popular. Everyone's dying to get in."
Follow-up: Ting asks, "Is there a VIP fast lane?" Salesman: "There is. It's called an accident."

items.sourceNote: 改編自絞刑台幽默（Galgenhumor）面對死亡的最後一句俏皮話傳統，原創地獄敘事

zh-tw.content:
死神來接阿德的那天，
阿德正在收快遞。

死神有點不好意思：
「抱歉，時間到了。」

阿德看了看手上的包裹：
「等一下，
這個我才剛下單，
還沒拆耶。」

死神：「來不及了。」

阿德嘆氣：
「我這輩子最後悔的，
不是沒做什麼大事，
是這個包裹，
我永遠不知道裡面是什麼了。」

死神沉默了一下，
破例幫他拆開——

裡面是一本書，
書名叫《如何延長壽命》。

兩個人都笑了。
阿德說：「至少我走得很準時。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 絞刑台幽默的精神：在最無法逃避的時刻，還能笑著吐槽自己。傳給那個總說『等我有空再開始養生／運動／好好生活』的朋友——笑點在於『永遠在等一個來不及的包裹』，提醒人別把『開始好好活』這件事一直延後到收貨之後
variations: 短版：「死神來接我那天，我的快遞還沒到。我最大的遺憾，是永遠不知道裡面是什麼。」
進階版：死神：「下次早點下單。」阿德：「下次？你是說……還有下次？」死神：「呃，當我沒說。」

en.content:
The day Death came for De,
De was signing for a package.

Death looked a little awkward:
"Sorry. Time's up."

De glanced at the box in his hands:
"Hang on,
I just ordered this.
I haven't even opened it."

Death: "Too late."

De sighed:
"My biggest regret in this whole life
isn't that I never did anything big.
It's this package —
I'll never know what's inside."

Death paused,
then made an exception and opened it —

inside was a book.
The title: How to Live Longer.

They both laughed.
De said: "At least I'm right on time."
en.editorNote: Pure gallows humor: cracking a joke at yourself in the one moment you can't escape. Send to the friend who keeps saying 'I'll start eating healthy / working out / actually living once I have time.' The punchline is the package that's always one delivery too late — a nudge not to keep postponing 'starting to really live' until after it arrives.
variations: Short version: "The day Death came, my package hadn't arrived. My one regret: I'll never know what was inside."
Follow-up: Death: "Order earlier next time." De: "Next time? You mean... there's a next time?" Death: "Uh. Forget I said that."

items.sourceNote: 存在主義早餐梗，重寫並擴充

zh-tw.content:
現代人最強的技能，
是一邊吃早餐，一邊思考自己存在的意義，
然後在思考出結論之前，
被通勤提醒打斷。

你以為你在追問人生，
其實你只是在等吐司跳起來。

人類最偉大的地方就在這裡——
我們可以同時面對宇宙的虛無
跟「再五分鐘就遲到」的現實，
而且每天都選擇了後者。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種早上一邊滑手機一邊嘆氣的同事，他會苦笑——因為最後那句「每天都選擇了後者」精準命中：我們不是不懂虛無，只是房租比虛無更急
variations: 短版：「我不是沒在思考人生，我只是每次思考到一半就被鬧鐘打斷。」
進階版：吐司跳起來的那一刻，所有存在主義危機自動延後到明天早上同一時段。

en.content:
The most advanced skill of modern humans
is pondering the meaning of our existence over breakfast,
then getting interrupted by the commute alert
right before reaching any conclusion.

You think you're interrogating life.
Really you're just waiting for the toast to pop.

This is humanity at its finest —
we can face the void of the universe
and the reality of 'five more minutes and I'm late'
at the exact same time,
and every single day we pick the second one.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who scrolls and sighs over their morning coffee. They'll smile that tired smile — because the last line lands clean: it's not that we don't get the void, it's that rent is more urgent than the void.
variations: Short version: "It's not that I never think about life — I just keep getting interrupted by my alarm halfway through."
Follow-up: The moment the toast pops, every existential crisis automatically reschedules to the same slot tomorrow morning.

items.sourceNote: 葬禮小聊恐懼梗，重寫

zh-tw.content:
有人說他不怕死，他怕的是死後的世界。

我說我也不怕死，
我怕的是自己的告別式上，
大家圍著我的照片，
陷入那種尷尬的沉默，
沒有人知道該講什麼。

活著的時候我最怕冷場，
沒想到我能把冷場帶到下輩子。

所以拜託，
那天請放點音樂，
讓我最後一次不用負責炒熱氣氛。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個聚會中總是默默負責找話題的朋友——社恐的人連自己的葬禮都在擔心冷場，這個笑話的溫柔在於：你終於可以不用負責了
variations: 短版：「我不怕死，我怕告別式上沒人知道要講什麼，然後全場安靜得像我生前每一場尷尬的飯局。」
進階版：遺願清單第一條——找個會主持的朋友，確保我最後一場也不冷場。

en.content:
Someone said he's not afraid of death, he's afraid of the afterlife.

I said I'm not afraid of death either.
What scares me is my own funeral —
everyone standing around my photo,
sinking into that awkward silence,
nobody knowing what to say.

In life my biggest fear was a dead conversation.
Turns out I can carry the dead air into the next life too.

So please,
that day, play some music.
Let me, for once, not be the one
responsible for keeping things lively.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's always quietly hunting for a topic at gatherings. People with social anxiety worry about dead air even at their own funeral — and the kindness here is: you finally get to clock out.
variations: Short version: "I don't fear death. I fear the silence at my funeral when nobody knows what to say — just like every awkward dinner I ever sat through."
Follow-up: First item on my bucket list — find a friend who can host, so even my last event doesn't fall flat.

items.sourceNote: 上班慢性消耗梗，重寫

zh-tw.content:
朝九晚五，是現代人慢慢死去的方式。

不過認真說，
這個說法對死亡不太公平——
死亡至少是一次性的，
而上班是每天重新死一次，
隔天還會自動復活，
繼續上班。

你以為你在賺錢，
其實你在用八小時，
換一頓晚餐，
跟一句「明天還要早起」。

但奇怪的是，
等真的放長假，
第三天你又開始想念那個慢慢死去的地方了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個週日晚上開始焦慮的上班族——前半段是吐槽，最後那句「又開始想念」才是真正的地獄：我們連被消耗都會上癮
variations: 短版：「上班最可怕的不是慢慢死去，是每天死完隔天還會復活，繼續上。」
進階版：放假第一天解脫，第三天空虛，第五天默默打開工作信箱——斯德哥爾摩症候群，職場版。

en.content:
Nine to five is the modern way of slowly dying.

But honestly,
that's unfair to death —
death is at least a one-time thing,
while work is dying a little every day,
then auto-respawning the next morning
to go right back to work.

You think you're earning money.
Really you're trading eight hours
for one dinner
and a 'gotta get up early tomorrow.'

But here's the weird part:
the moment you finally take a long vacation,
by day three you start missing
that place that was slowly killing you.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose dread kicks in on Sunday night. The first half is the easy gripe — it's the last line, 'you start missing it,' that's the real gallows: we even get addicted to being drained.
variations: Short version: "The scary part of work isn't dying slowly — it's respawning every morning to do it again."
Follow-up: Day one of vacation, freedom. Day three, emptiness. Day five, quietly opening the work inbox — Stockholm syndrome, office edition.

items.sourceNote: 科技依賴世代梗，重寫成黑色幽默對話

zh-tw.content:
爺爺說：「你們這代人太依賴科技了。」

我說：「是喔。」
然後幫他把老花眼鏡找出來、
把助聽器調好、
把降血壓的藥按時間排好、
把遙控器上的字放大、
再教他第七次怎麼接視訊。

爺爺戴上眼鏡，滿意地看著我：
「還是你們年輕人懂得自己解決問題。」

我沒說話。
有些依賴，是不會出現在帳單上的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給家裡有長輩、又常被念「太依賴手機」的人——這個笑話的轉折很溫柔：誰依賴誰，從來不是表面那樣，最後那句把吐槽收成了心軟
variations: 短版：「爺爺說我們太依賴科技，說完叫我幫他開電視、找眼鏡、調冷氣、回訊息。」
進階版：真正的科技依賴，是有人願意當你的全自動客服，而且不會掛你電話。

en.content:
Grandpa said: "Your generation relies way too much on technology."

I said: "Sure."
Then I found his reading glasses,
adjusted his hearing aid,
sorted his blood pressure pills by time,
bumped up the font on the remote,
and explained — for the seventh time — how to answer a video call.

Grandpa put on his glasses, looked at me, satisfied:
"See, you young people know how to solve your own problems."

I didn't say anything.
Some kinds of reliance never show up on a bill.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone with elders at home who keep getting told they're 'too glued to their phone.' The turn is gentle: who relies on whom is never what it looks like on the surface — and the last line folds the gripe into something tender.
variations: Short version: "Grandpa said we rely too much on tech, then asked me to turn on the TV, find his glasses, fix the AC, and answer his messages."
Follow-up: Real tech dependence is having someone willing to be your 24/7 help desk who never puts you on hold.

items.sourceNote: 成年崩潰主題，重寫成黑色幽默語錄

zh-tw.content:
小時候我以為長大後會有很多午睡。

結果長大後只有兩種狀態：
咖啡因撐著，
或存在危機撐著。

睡覺反而變成一種獎勵，
要先把待辦清單做完才能領。

而那張清單，
從來沒有做完過。

所以嚴格來說——
我已經很多年沒領過薪水了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說「等我忙完這陣子」的朋友——重點是最後那句反轉：休息變成永遠領不到的獎金，因為「忙完」這件事根本不存在
variations: 短版：「成年人的睡眠不是需求，是一種要先還清待辦事項才能兌換的點數。」
進階版：我不是失眠，我是清醒地在思考為什麼小時候那麼抗拒午睡，真是個敗家子。

en.content:
As a kid I assumed adulthood came with lots of naps.

Turns out there are only two settings:
running on caffeine,
or running on existential dread.

Sleep became a reward instead —
something you unlock
only after the to-do list is done.

And that list
has never once been done.

So technically
I haven't collected a paycheck in years.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'once things calm down.' The kicker is the reversal: rest becomes a bonus you'll never cash out, because 'things calming down' was never on the table.
variations: Short version: "Adult sleep isn't a need, it's a reward points balance you can only redeem after clearing your to-do list."
Follow-up: I'm not an insomniac, I'm just lucidly wondering why my younger self fought naps so hard. What a spendthrift.

items.sourceNote: 預算與夢想梗，重寫成黑色幽默語錄

zh-tw.content:
做預算，
本質上就是坐下來，
認真地告訴你的每一個夢想：
「你太貴了。」

然後夢想問：「那哪個我負擔得起？」

你翻了翻帳本，
沉默了一下，說：
「活著就好，先這樣。」

夢想點點頭，
默默搬到了清單最下面，
標記為「等有錢再說」。

那一欄，現在排了很長。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給月底在記帳 App 前嘆氣的朋友——這個笑話的後勁在「等有錢再說」那一欄越排越長，每個人都有一個被無限延後的版本的自己
variations: 短版：「預算就是有禮貌地通知你的夢想，它們暫時不在這次的營業項目裡。」
進階版：我不是沒有夢想，我只是把它們做了風險控管，全部移到了下一個會計年度。

en.content:
Budgeting is really just sitting down
and sincerely informing
every one of your dreams:
"You're too expensive."

Then a dream asks: "Which one can I afford?"

You flip through the ledger,
pause, and say:
"Just staying alive. That's it for now."

The dream nods,
quietly relocates to the bottom of the list,
labeled 'when I have money.'

That column's gotten pretty long.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who sighs at their budgeting app at month's end. The lingering part is that 'when I have money' column getting longer — everyone has a perpetually postponed version of themselves.
variations: Short version: "A budget is just politely notifying your dreams that they're not in this quarter's lineup."
Follow-up: I do have dreams. I just risk-managed them and moved them all to the next fiscal year.

items.sourceNote: 「我以為30歲會成為某號人物」梗，重寫成黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
我以為三十歲的時候，
我已經是個「某號人物」了。

結果三十歲的我打開抽屜，
發現自己主要是由——
發票、
收據、
還沒繳的帳單，
和一堆「之後再處理」組成的。

我不是沒有成為任何人，
我只是成為了一個資料夾。

而且還是沒有命名的那種。
標題就叫「新增資料夾」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給快到、或剛過某個整數歲數、開始焦慮的朋友——「成為一個資料夾」這個比喻會讓人先笑，再認真想：我到底在替誰歸檔自己的人生
variations: 短版：「我以為三十歲會成為某號人物，結果我成為了一疊還沒對帳的收據。」
進階版：好消息是我終於找到了人生的定位，壞消息是定位顯示我在「待辦事項」資料夾裡。

en.content:
I figured by thirty
I'd be Somebody.

Then thirty-year-old me opened a drawer
and realized I'm mostly made of —
invoices,
receipts,
unpaid bills,
and a big pile of 'deal with it later.'

It's not that I became no one.
I became a folder.

The unnamed kind, too.
Title: 'New Folder.'
en.editorNote: Send to the friend approaching or just past a milestone birthday and starting to spiral. 'Becoming a folder' makes you laugh first, then quietly wonder who exactly you're filing your own life away for.
variations: Short version: "Thought I'd be Somebody by thirty. Turns out I'm a stack of receipts I haven't reconciled."
Follow-up: Good news, I finally found my place in life. Bad news, it's inside the 'To-Do' folder.

items.sourceNote: 看醫生誤以為關節炎梗，重寫成黑色幽默對話

zh-tw.content:
我去看醫生，
說我覺得自己得了關節炎。

醫生看了看報告，搖搖頭：
「不是關節炎。」

我鬆了一口氣：「太好了。」

醫生：「是身體在提早幫你預習
一些更長遠的安排。」

我：「……什麼意思？」

醫生微笑：
「意思是你的零件開始陸續到期，
而原廠已經不再生產了。」

我走出診間，
第一次覺得「保固期」這三個字
這麼有哲理。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一過某個年紀、起床就這裡痠那裡痛的朋友——「原廠不再生產」這句話把身體老化講得很機械、很冷，但也很真，笑完會默默多喝一口溫水
variations: 短版：「醫生說我沒有關節炎，只是零件陸續到期，而且原廠停產了。」
進階版：年輕時擔心沒人愛，現在擔心保固過期——成長就是煩惱從售前換到了售後。

en.content:
I went to the doctor
and said I thought I had arthritis.

The doctor looked at the chart, shook their head:
"It's not arthritis."

I exhaled: "Oh, good."

Doctor: "It's your body
giving you an early preview
of some more long-term arrangements."

Me: "...meaning?"

Doctor smiled:
"Meaning your parts are starting to expire,
and the manufacturer
stopped making replacements."

I walked out of the office
feeling, for the first time,
that the phrase 'warranty period'
was weirdly profound.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who, past a certain age, wakes up sore in three new places. 'Manufacturer stopped making replacements' frames aging as cold and mechanical — but true. You laugh, then quietly take a sip of warm water.
variations: Short version: "Doctor said it's not arthritis, my parts are just expiring — and the manufacturer discontinued the model."
Follow-up: Young, you worry no one will love you. Older, you worry the warranty's run out. Growing up is just moving your anxieties from pre-sale to post-sale.

items.sourceNote: 「我以前很有趣，現在財務負責、內心死透」梗，重寫成黑色幽默語錄

zh-tw.content:
我以前是個很有趣的人。

會臨時起意買機票，
會在凌晨三點唱歌，
會為了一個爛笑話笑到岔氣。

現在我很有責任感、
很穩定、
很準時繳稅，
內心很死。

朋友問：「你變了。」

我說：「對啊，我升級了。
從一個會做夢的人，
升級成一個會準時收到帳單的人。」

他們說這叫成熟。
我覺得這比較像是
把自己外包給了現實，
而且沒簽退場條款。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個曾經很瘋、現在每天準時下班回家睡覺的老朋友——這個笑話的痛點在「沒簽退場條款」，我們都在某個時刻安靜地把有趣的自己交了出去，還忘了留後路
variations: 短版：「我以前很有趣，現在很準時繳稅，內心很死。他們說這叫成熟。」
進階版：我沒有變無聊，我只是把『好玩』這個功能設成了付費解鎖，而我捨不得花那筆錢。

en.content:
I used to be a fun person.

I'd book a flight on a whim,
sing at three in the morning,
laugh till it hurt at a terrible joke.

Now I'm responsible,
stable,
I pay my taxes on time,
and I'm dead inside.

A friend said: "You've changed."

I said: "Yeah, I upgraded.
From someone who dreams
to someone who receives bills punctually."

They call it maturity.
Feels more like
I outsourced myself to reality —
with no exit clause.
en.editorNote: Send to the old friend who used to be wild and now clocks out and goes straight home to sleep. The ache is 'no exit clause' — at some point we all quietly handed over our fun selves and forgot to keep a way back.
variations: Short version: "I used to be fun. Now I pay taxes on time and I'm dead inside. They call it maturity."
Follow-up: I didn't get boring, I just set 'fun' to paid-unlock — and I can't bring myself to spend the money.

items.sourceNote: 玻璃杯半空被下毒，改寫自網路一行黑色幽默梗

zh-tw.content:
他們說，樂觀的人看半杯水覺得是半滿，
悲觀的人覺得是半空。

我看著那半杯水，想了很久，
說：「重點不是滿不滿，
是這杯水到底還能不能喝。」

大家笑了。

但其實這才是最務實的活法——
別人在吵杯子，
我在確認自己會不會被毒死。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個朋友圈裡最冷靜、永遠在問「先確認一下這安全嗎」的人——他會笑，因為這正是他的生存哲學：在所有人忙著辯論心態的時候，他在確認自己活不活得下去
variations: 短版：「半滿還是半空我沒意見，我只想知道誰在裡面加了東西。」
進階版：後來我發現，會問這杯能不能喝的人，往往活得比樂觀的人久——悲觀只是提早做了風險評估。

en.content:
They say an optimist sees the glass as half full,
and a pessimist sees it as half empty.

I looked at that glass for a long time and said:
"The real question isn't how full it is —
it's whether the water's still safe to drink."

Everyone laughed.

But honestly, that's the most practical way to live —
everyone else is arguing about the glass,
while I'm checking if it's going to kill me.
en.editorNote: Send to the calmest person in your group chat — the one who always asks 'wait, is this safe first?' They'll laugh, because that's literally their survival philosophy: while everyone debates mindset, they're confirming they'll make it out alive.
variations: Short version: "Half full or half empty, no opinion. I just want to know who put something in it."
Follow-up: Turns out the people asking if it's safe to drink tend to outlive the optimists. Pessimism is just early risk assessment.

items.sourceNote: 死神說我等你，改寫自網路一行黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
死神來敲門的時候，
我說：「不好意思，我最近很忙，能不能改天？」

死神看了我一眼，
緩緩坐下：「沒事，我等你。」

那一刻我才意識到——
他不是趕時間的人。
趕時間的一直都是我。

而他唯一的工作，
就是耐心地，
等每個說自己很忙的人，
終於有空。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個永遠把『等我忙完這陣子』掛在嘴邊、然後一忙就是三年的朋友——笑點在於死神的耐心，因為我們都用『忙』推掉了太多事，而那唯一一個不會被推掉的，正在很有耐心地等
variations: 短版：「我跟死神說我很忙，他說沒關係他不急——急的從來都是我。」
進階版：後來我學會了，真正重要的事不要對它說『等我有空』，因為有些東西不會等，只有它，會一直等。

en.content:
When Death knocked on my door,
I said: "Sorry, I've been swamped lately. Can we reschedule?"

Death looked at me,
sat down slowly, and said: "No rush. I'll wait."

That's when it hit me —
he was never the one in a hurry.
I was.

His only job
is to wait, patiently,
for every person who says they're busy
to finally have some free time.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's perpetually 'just gotta get through this busy stretch' — a stretch that somehow lasts three years. The punchline is Death's patience: we wave off so much with 'I'm busy,' and the one thing we can't wave off is waiting, very patiently.
variations: Short version: "I told Death I was busy. He said no rush. Turns out I was the only one in a hurry."
Follow-up: I learned not to tell the important things 'when I'm free.' Some of them won't wait. Only that one waits forever.

items.sourceNote: 求穩定未來大笑，改寫自網路一行黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
我向人生許願：「拜託，給我一點穩定就好。」

人生想了一下，
說：「好啊。」

然後給了我穩定的房貸、
穩定的加班、
穩定的胃食道逆流、
還有每個月穩定地懷疑自己。

你看，它確實有實現我的願望。

只是我忘了講清楚，
我要的是穩定的『快樂』，
不是穩定的『折磨』。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個終於有了穩定工作、卻好像也同時失去了什麼的朋友——痛點在於我們小時候求的『穩定』，長大後真的拿到了，只是它穩定的方式跟我們想的完全不一樣
variations: 短版：「我求穩定，人生給了我穩定的焦慮，從不間斷，全年無休。」
進階版：許願的時候一定要把形容詞講清楚，不然你會發現，宇宙是個非常嚴格、而且完全照字面執行的客服。

en.content:
I made a wish to life: "Please, just give me a little stability."

Life thought it over
and said: "Sure."

Then handed me a stable mortgage,
stable overtime,
stable acid reflux,
and a stable monthly habit of doubting myself.

See? It really did grant my wish.

I just forgot to specify
that I wanted stable *happiness* —
not stable *suffering*.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who finally landed the stable job but seems to have lost something in the trade. The ache is that the 'stability' we begged for as kids really did arrive — just not in any of the ways we pictured.
variations: Short version: "I asked for stability. Life gave me stable anxiety — never a day off, never a holiday."
Follow-up: Always specify your adjectives when wishing. The universe is a very strict customer-service rep that follows instructions to the letter.

items.sourceNote: 葬禮小聊比死亡可怕，改寫自網路一行黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
有人問我怕不怕死。

我說：「死本身還好，
我真正怕的是葬禮上的尬聊。」

你想想——
躺在那裡，
聽著一群只在年節見過你三次的親戚，
圍著你討論你『其實人很好』，
然後話題不到五分鐘就轉到房價跟誰家小孩考上哪。

連最後一場，
都還是逃不掉那種寒暄。

那才是真正的地獄。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個跟你一樣，寧願多加班也不想參加家族聚會、最怕尷尬寒暄的社恐朋友——笑點在於我們連自己的葬禮都想翹掉，因為比死亡更難熬的，是那種沒有靈魂的客套
variations: 短版：「我不怕死，我怕葬禮上有人問我『最近過得好嗎』。」
進階版：所以我已經寫好遺囑了——葬禮不準閒聊，要聊房價的請自行移駕到外面走廊，謝謝。

en.content:
Someone asked if I was afraid of dying.

I said: "Death itself is fine.
What I'm actually scared of is the small talk at the funeral."

Think about it —
you're lying there,
listening to a crowd of relatives who saw you maybe three times,
standing around agreeing that you were 'actually a good person,'
and within five minutes the topic drifts to housing prices
and whose kid got into which school.

Even your final event,
and you still can't escape the pleasantries.

That's the real hell.
en.editorNote: Send to your fellow introvert — the one who'd rather work late than show up to a family gathering and dreads forced small talk most of all. The punchline is that we'd skip our own funerals, because worse than death is that soulless polite chitchat.
variations: Short version: "I'm not scared of death. I'm scared of someone at the funeral asking how I've been lately."
Follow-up: So I've already written it into my will — no small talk at the funeral. Anyone who wants to discuss housing prices, kindly step out into the hallway. Thank you.

items.sourceNote: 童年怕黑成年怕電費帳單，改寫自網路黑色幽默一行

zh-tw.content:
小時候我最怕黑，
睡覺一定要開盞小燈，
不然會覺得有東西躲在衣櫃裡。

長大以後我克服了——
不是因為勇敢，
是因為我看到了這個月的電費帳單。

現在我房間越黑越安心，
反正衣櫃裡也沒東西了，
值錢的早就被通膨吃光了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個夏天捨不得開冷氣、坐在黑漆漆房間裡省電的朋友——笑點是我們小時候怕的是黑暗本身，長大後怕的是把燈打開要付的代價，恐懼的對象換了，但人還是一樣不敢開燈
variations: 短版：「我已經不怕黑了，我怕的是把燈打開之後收到的帳單。」
進階版：所以說成長就是——從怕衣櫃裡的怪物，變成怕信箱裡的帳單，而後者是真的會把你吃掉。

en.content:
As a kid, I was terrified of the dark.
I needed a little nightlight to sleep,
or I'd swear something was hiding in the closet.

I got over it as an adult —
not out of bravery,
but because I saw this month's electricity bill.

Now the darker my room, the calmer I feel.
There's nothing in the closet anyway —
inflation ate everything worth taking years ago.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who sits in a pitch-black room all summer rather than turn on the AC. The punchline: as kids we feared the dark itself; as adults we fear the cost of switching the light on. The monster changed addresses, but we're still too scared to flip the switch.
variations: Short version: "I'm not scared of the dark anymore. I'm scared of the bill that comes after I turn the light on."
Follow-up: So growing up is just trading the monster in the closet for the bill in the mailbox — and only one of those actually eats you alive.

items.sourceNote: 悲傷輔導師太厲害結果自己也走了，改寫自網路黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
我去看了悲傷輔導師，
因為養了十年的貓走了，我哭得停不下來。

她真的很厲害，
講話溫柔、邏輯清楚，
短短幾次就把我從谷底拉了上來。

上個月，她自己也過世了。

奇怪的是，我居然一點都不難過。

我想，這就是她最後留給我的——
一個再也用不到她的我。
畢業證書，是她用自己換的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛走出低潮、開始覺得『其實沒那麼難』的朋友——笑點先是荒謬（輔導師太強到把你治到對她的死都無感），但後座力是：真正的療癒，是讓你不再需要那個人，哪怕代價是失去他
variations: 短版：「我的悲傷輔導師過世了，她實在太厲害——厲害到連她走了我都不難過。」
進階版：後來我才懂，最好的告別，就是有一天你發現自己已經不需要再回去那間診間了。

en.content:
I saw a grief counselor
after the cat I'd had for ten years passed and I couldn't stop crying.

She was incredible —
gentle voice, crystal-clear logic,
and in just a few sessions she pulled me up off the floor.

Last month, she passed away too.

The strange thing is, I didn't feel sad at all.

I think that was her final gift to me —
a version of me that would never need her again.
The diploma cost her her life.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's just climbed out of a rough patch and started thinking 'maybe it wasn't that bad.' The laugh comes first from the absurdity — a counselor so good she cured you of grieving even her. The aftershock: real healing means you stop needing the person, even if losing them is the price.
variations: Short version: "My grief counselor passed away. She was so good I don't even feel sad about it."
Follow-up: I get it now — the best goodbye is the day you realize you never have to walk back into that office again.

items.sourceNote: 老闆葬禮上員工的跳脫框架冷笑話，改寫自網路黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
老闆過世那天，公司放了半天假。

葬禮上，平常被他罵到最慘的小陳，
默默走到棺木旁邊，
彎下腰，輕聲說了一句：

「老闆，現在……
是誰被裝在框框裡走不出來了？」

說完他擦了擦眼角，
大家以為他在哭。

其實他是笑到流淚。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天被『要跳脫框架思考』『要有狼性』疲勞轟炸的上班族朋友——笑點是員工終於等到一個老闆永遠出不來的『框框』，黑色幽默的反差就在於：那句話既是報復，也是一種釋懷
variations: 短版：員工在老闆棺木旁低聲說：「老闆，這次換誰跳不出框框了？」
進階版：散場時人資還補了一句：「他生前最愛說的就是不要待在舒適圈——這下他可舒適了。」

en.content:
The day the boss died, the company gave us half a day off.

At the funeral, the new guy he used to chew out the hardest
quietly walked up to the casket,
leaned in, and whispered:

"So, boss…
who's stuck inside the box now?"

Then he dabbed at his eyes,
and everyone assumed he was crying.

He was actually laughing so hard he teared up.
en.editorNote: Send to the office friend who gets carpet-bombed daily with 'think outside the box' and 'we need more hustle.' The punchline: the employee finally found a box the boss can never climb out of. The dark twist is that the line is both revenge and, somehow, closure.
variations: Short version: At the casket, the employee whispered, "Who's thinking outside the box now, boss?"
Follow-up: On the way out, HR added: "He always told us to leave our comfort zone — well, he's pretty comfortable now."

items.sourceNote: 向虛空問人生意義結果被反問，改寫自網路存在主義黑色幽默

zh-tw.content:
那天我撐不住了，
對著天空大喊：
「拜託，告訴我活著到底有什麼意義！」

沉默了很久，
虛空終於回了我一句。

它說：
「你先告訴我，
這三十年來，你都拿這條命做了什麼？」

我張開嘴，
發現自己一個字都答不上來。

從此我再也不問了——
不是怕沒有答案，
是怕它真的有，而那個答案是我自己。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜睡不著、滑著手機問自己『我到底在幹嘛』的朋友——笑點是你向宇宙討答案，宇宙卻把問題丟回來；後座力在於，最可怕的不是人生沒有意義，而是意義一直握在你手上，你只是不敢看
variations: 短版：我問虛空人生有什麼意義，它反問我：「那你這些年都拿去幹嘛了？」我就閉嘴了。
進階版：所以現在我不問意義了，我改問晚餐吃什麼——至少這個問題我答得出來，而且答錯了傷害比較小。

en.content:
One night I couldn't take it anymore.
I yelled up at the sky:
"Please — just tell me what the point of being alive is!"

There was a long silence.
Then the void finally answered.

It said:
"You first.
What exactly have you done with this life
over the last thirty years?"

I opened my mouth
and couldn't get a single word out.

I never asked again —
not because I was scared there's no answer,
but because I was scared there is one, and the answer is me.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's up at 3 a.m. scrolling and quietly asking 'what am I even doing.' The laugh: you demand answers from the universe, and the universe lobs the question right back. The aftershock — the scary part isn't that life has no meaning, it's that the meaning was in your hands the whole time and you couldn't bear to look.
variations: Short version: I asked the void what life means. It asked, "What have you been doing with yours?" I had nothing.
Follow-up: So now I don't ask about meaning anymore. I ask what's for dinner — at least that one I can answer, and getting it wrong does less damage.

items.sourceNote: 墓園諷刺梗改寫，核心是『大家都搶著進去』的反差

zh-tw.content:
我問管理員：「你們墓園為什麼從來不會客滿？」

他抽了口菸，慢慢說：
「因為大家都是搶著進來的，
沒有人會想出去。」

我笑了一下，正要走，
他在我背後補了一句：

「而且你看那些排隊的——
他們以為自己還在外面排隊，
其實號碼牌早就拿了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種把『等我有空再說』掛在嘴邊、永遠在排隊等人生開始的朋友——笑點是墓園不會客滿這個冷酷事實，後座力是『號碼牌早就拿了』：你以為你在等，其實隊伍一直在前進
variations: 短版：墓園永遠不會客滿，因為大家都是搶著進來，沒人想出去。
進階版：「那我能插隊嗎？」管理員笑了：「不用插，你站著不動，隊伍自己會把你推到最前面。」

en.content:
I asked the groundskeeper:
"Why does this cemetery never fill up?"

He took a slow drag and said,
"Because everyone's fighting to get in.
Nobody's ever asked to leave."

I laughed and turned to go.
He called after me:

"And see all those people out there, waiting in line?
They think they haven't entered yet.
They've already pulled their number."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'I'll get to it when things calm down' — forever in line, waiting for their real life to start. The laugh is the cold fact that a cemetery never runs out of room. The aftershock: you've already pulled your number. You think you're waiting, but the line keeps moving.
variations: Short version: The cemetery never fills up. Everyone's fighting to get in, and nobody asks to leave.
Follow-up: "Can I cut the line?" He smiled. "No need. Just stand still — the line pushes you to the front all on its own."

items.sourceNote: 心理諮商『開始快樂』梗改寫成台灣口吻，核心是治療師的冷答案

zh-tw.content:
我問心理師：
「我要怎樣才能開始快樂？」

她想了三秒，回答：
「先從『不要再期待自己會快樂』開始。」

我愣住：「這樣有用嗎？」

她點頭：
「至少從今天起，
你不會再因為不快樂
而又多一層不快樂。」

那天我走出診間，
第一次覺得，
好像真的輕了一點點。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊焦慮、一邊又因為『我怎麼還在焦慮』而更焦慮的朋友——笑點是心理師給了一個聽起來很喪的答案，後座力是它其實真的有效：很多痛苦不是來自情緒本身，而是來自我們對情緒的二次譴責
variations: 短版：「怎樣才能快樂？」心理師：「先別再要求自己快樂。」結果還真的有用。
進階版：「那如果我連這個都做不到呢？」心理師：「那就接受你連放棄都做不好——恭喜，你又少一層壓力了。」

en.content:
I asked my therapist,
"How do I start being happy?"

She thought for three seconds.
"Start by no longer expecting yourself to be happy."

I blinked. "Does that actually work?"

She nodded.
"At least from today,
you'll stop being unhappy
about being unhappy."

I walked out of that office
and for the first time
felt a little lighter.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's anxious — and then more anxious because they're still anxious. The laugh is a therapist handing over a bleak-sounding answer. The aftershock is that it actually works: a lot of suffering isn't the feeling itself, it's the second layer where we punish ourselves for having it.
variations: Short version: "How do I get happy?" "Stop demanding it of yourself." Annoyingly, it worked.
Follow-up: "What if I can't even do that?" "Then accept you're bad at quitting too. Congrats — that's one less thing to be stressed about."

items.sourceNote: 工程師『被榨乾』地獄梗，改寫成吸血鬼提早下班的雙關

zh-tw.content:
公司來了個新同事，是吸血鬼。

大家都很緊張，
結果他每天五點準時下班。

我問他：「你怎麼都不加班？」

他淡淡地說：
「我靠吸血維生，
但這間公司的血——
早就被加班榨乾了，
我留下來也吸不到東西。」

那天我看著自己的手臂，
發現我才是那個
撐到半夜還在這裡的怪物。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個明明累到不行還在硬撐加班的同事——笑點是吸血鬼嫌公司的血被榨乾，後座力是最後那句反轉：真正不正常、把自己耗到深夜的，是還在座位上的我們，連吸血鬼都比我們懂得準時離開
variations: 短版：吸血鬼新同事每天五點下班，理由是『這公司的血早被加班榨乾，吸不到了』。
進階版：後來他離職了，留下一句話：「我見過很多獵物，但第一次看到獵物自己掛在牆上不下來。」

en.content:
We hired a vampire.

Everyone was on edge —
but he clocked out at five sharp, every single day.

I asked him, "Why don't you ever stay late?"

He said flatly:
"I live on blood.
But the blood in this place
was drained dry by overtime long ago.
There's nothing left for me to take."

That night I looked at my own arm
and realized
I was the monster
still here at midnight.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who's running on fumes but still grinding overtime. The laugh is a vampire complaining the office blood is already tapped out. The aftershock is the flip at the end: the real freak, the one burning themselves down to nothing past midnight, is us — even the vampire knows when to leave on time.
variations: Short version: The vampire hire leaves at five every day. Says the office blood was 'drained dry by overtime' ages ago.
Follow-up: He quit eventually, with a parting line: "I've stalked a lot of prey, but never one that hangs itself on the wall and refuses to come down."

items.sourceNote: 『死前願望』梗改寫，核心是願望被字面實現的反差

zh-tw.content:
精靈說可以實現我一個願望。

我想了很久，說：
「我希望我死之前，
能把該做的事都做完。」

精靈點點頭，
打了個響指。

從那天起，
我每天的待辦清單
都神奇地清空了，
一件都不剩。

後來我才懂——
它不是讓我變勤勞，
是把我『死之前』
一直往後推到
剛好沒事可做的那一刻。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說『等我把事情忙完就去玩』、結果一輩子都在忙的朋友——笑點是願望被精靈用字面意思實現了，後座力是那句『往後推到剛好沒事可做的那一刻』：你以為你在追求做完，其實做完的那天，就是停下來的那天
variations: 短版：我許願『死前把事做完』，結果待辦清單天天清空——因為它把死期一直推到我沒事做為止。
進階版：我趕緊改口：「那我要永遠有事做！」精靈嘆氣：「恭喜，你剛剛把自己變成不死的社畜。」

en.content:
A genie offered me one wish.

I thought hard, then said:
"I wish that before I die,
I'll have finished everything I need to do."

The genie nodded
and snapped his fingers.

From that day on,
my to-do list
miraculously emptied itself
every single night. Nothing left.

Later I understood —
he didn't make me productive.
He kept pushing 'before I die'
back and back,
to the exact moment
I'd have nothing left to do.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always says 'once I clear my plate I'll go have fun' — and stays busy their whole life. The laugh is the genie granting the wish literally. The aftershock is that last line: you think you're chasing 'done,' but the day you're finally done is the day everything stops.
variations: Short version: I wished to 'finish everything before I die.' My to-do list clears nightly now — because it just keeps pushing the dying back until I'm out of tasks.
Follow-up: I panicked: "Then I want to always have something to do!" The genie sighed. "Congratulations. You're now an immortal wage slave."

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『工作給的保險剛好夠付治療工作造成的傷』類職場黑色幽默，改寫為台灣公司健檢與承辦人員的對話，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司今年加碼員工福利，
發了一封信：
「即日起，
全員享有更完整的醫療保險。」

我感動了一秒。

下一行寫著：
「保障範圍包含——
過勞、
胃食道逆流、
慢性失眠、
以及焦慮症。」

我看著這份保單，
突然懂了：
它不是在照顧我，
它是在預測我。

它早就知道
這份工作
會把我變成
什麼樣子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種公司福利講得很好聽、但你心知肚明那些福利剛好對應你被操出來的病的朋友。笑點是保險範圍精準到像預言，後座力是那句『它不是在照顧我，是在預測我』——保單比你更清楚這份工作的代價
variations: 短版：公司新保險範圍包含過勞、失眠、焦慮——這不是福利，這是它對我未來的精準預測。
進階版：我打去問：「為什麼沒保憂鬱症？」客服頓了一下：「那個要等您做滿三年才會自動生效。」

en.content:
My company upgraded our benefits this year
and sent out an email:
"Effective immediately,
everyone now has fuller medical coverage."

I was touched for a second.

The next line read:
"Coverage includes —
burnout,
acid reflux,
chronic insomnia,
and anxiety disorder."

I stared at the policy
and suddenly got it:
it's not taking care of me.
It's predicting me.

It already knew
exactly what this job
would turn me into.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose company brags about its benefits — while you both quietly notice those benefits map perfectly onto the conditions the job gives you. The laugh is how the coverage reads like a prophecy. The aftershock: 'it's not taking care of me, it's predicting me.'
variations: Short version: New company insurance covers burnout, insomnia, and anxiety. That's not a benefit — that's an accurate forecast of my future.
Follow-up: I called and asked, "Why isn't depression covered?" The rep paused. "That one auto-activates after you've been here three years."

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『我不怕死，我怕葬禮上的尬聊』式社交焦慮黑色幽默，改寫為台灣告別式排隊場景，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
說真的，
我不怕死。

我怕的是
我的告別式上，
那些很久沒聯絡的人
排著隊，
輪流走到我遺照前，
硬擠出一句：

「他人很好。」

然後
互相點頭，
陷入沉默，
看手機，
等下一個人
來接話。

活著的時候
那麼怕冷場，
沒想到
連最後一場
都還是
靠別人尬聊撐完。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種一進電梯就怕跟人對到眼、聚會永遠躲在角落的社恐朋友。笑點是把死亡的恐懼換成『冷場』的恐懼，後座力是那句『連最後一場都靠別人尬聊撐完』——原來社交焦慮是會跟你到墳墓裡的
variations: 短版：我不怕死，我怕告別式上一堆人對著我遺照尬聊、互看手機、等下一個接話。
進階版：所以我立了遺囑：『請放背景音樂蓋過沉默，並準備話題卡，謝謝。』

en.content:
Honestly,
I'm not afraid of dying.

What scares me
is my own funeral —
all those people
I haven't talked to in years
lining up,
taking turns
in front of my photo,
squeezing out:

"He was a really good guy."

Then
nodding at each other,
falling silent,
checking their phones,
waiting for the next person
to pick up the conversation.

I spent my whole life
so afraid of awkward pauses.
Never guessed
even the final event
would be carried
by other people's small talk.
en.editorNote: Send to the socially anxious friend who avoids eye contact in elevators and hides in the corner at every party. The laugh swaps the fear of death for the fear of an awkward silence. The aftershock: even your funeral runs on other people's small talk — turns out social anxiety follows you to the grave.
variations: Short version: I'm not scared of death. I'm scared of everyone at my funeral making awkward small talk over my photo, checking their phones, waiting for someone else to speak.
Follow-up: So I put it in my will: 'Please play background music to cover the silence, and prepare conversation cards. Thanks.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『我有夠用一輩子的錢，只要我下午四點前死掉』Henny Youngman 式金錢黑色幽默，改寫為台灣理財顧問與客戶的對話，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
理財顧問翻著我的存款，
很有信心地說：
「以你現在的資產，
足夠你過完下半輩子。」

我鬆了一口氣。

他補了一句：
「前提是，
你大概在
今天傍晚六點左右
離開人世。」

我問：
「那如果我想活久一點呢？」

他闔上資料，
語氣很溫柔：
「那我們就得
認真聊聊
你要不要
繼續工作
到地老天荒了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每次看到帳戶餘額就倒抽一口氣、不敢算自己能撐到幾歲的朋友。笑點是『夠用一輩子』的前提是今晚就死，後座力是那句『工作到地老天荒』——原來退休不是時間問題，是你敢不敢活那麼久的問題
variations: 短版：顧問說我的錢夠過完下半輩子，前提是我今晚六點就死。我想活久一點，他說那就得工作到地老天荒。
進階版：我問：「有沒有折衷方案？」他想了想：「有，把『下半輩子』的定義改短一點就好。」

en.content:
My financial advisor flipped through my savings
and said confidently:
"With your current assets,
you've got enough
to last the rest of your life."

I exhaled in relief.

Then he added:
"Assuming, of course,
you pass away
around six o'clock
this evening."

I asked:
"And if I want to live a bit longer?"

He closed the folder,
his voice gentle:
"Then we should have
a serious conversation
about whether you plan
to keep working
until the heat death of the universe."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who gasps every time they open their bank app and refuses to calculate how long their savings will actually last. The laugh is that 'enough for life' assumes you die tonight. The aftershock: retirement isn't a money question, it's a question of how long you dare to stay alive.
variations: Short version: My advisor said I have enough to last the rest of my life — assuming I die at six tonight. I asked to live longer. He said then I'd be working until the heat death of the universe.
Follow-up: I asked, "Any compromise?" He thought for a second. "Sure. We just shorten the definition of 'the rest of your life.'"

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『治療師說我要找到人生意義，結果意義一次要價兩百美金』類心理諮商黑色幽默，改寫為台灣諮商所與案主的對話，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
諮商師很溫柔地說：
「你需要的，
是找到屬於你自己的
人生意義。」

我點點頭，
覺得被理解了。

走到櫃台，
結帳。

一次五十分鐘，
一千八。

我站在門口算了一下：
要找到人生的意義，
我大概需要
談個四十次，
總共七萬二。

原來
意義不是免費的。

它只是
還沒被標價而已——
現在標好了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊喊著人生好空虛、一邊看到諮商費用就決定再撐一下的朋友。笑點是『找意義』本身被明碼標價，後座力是那句『意義不是免費的，只是還沒被標價』——在這個時代，連自我救贖都要先看荷包
variations: 短版：諮商師叫我找到人生意義。結帳一次一千八。算了算，找到意義大概要七萬二——原來意義只是還沒被標價而已。
進階版：我問能不能分期，櫃台小姐笑著說：「可以，但意義也會分期到貨喔。」

en.content:
My therapist said gently:
"What you need
is to find your own
meaning in life."

I nodded,
feeling understood.

Then I walked to the front desk
to pay.

Fifty minutes:
sixty dollars.

I stood at the door and did the math:
to find the meaning of life,
I'd need
about forty sessions.
Twenty-four hundred dollars total.

Turns out
meaning isn't free.

It just
hadn't been priced yet —
now it has.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying life feels empty but takes one look at therapy rates and decides to tough it out a little longer. The laugh is 'finding meaning' coming with a literal price tag. The aftershock: meaning was never free, it just hadn't been priced — these days even saving yourself goes through your wallet first.
variations: Short version: My therapist told me to find life's meaning. Each session is sixty bucks. Did the math: finding meaning runs about twenty-four hundred. Turns out it just hadn't been priced yet.
Follow-up: I asked about a payment plan. The receptionist smiled: "Sure — but the meaning gets delivered in installments too."

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『早晨穀片配存在焦慮』『又是虛無裡的一個星期二』類日常存在主義黑色幽默，改寫為台灣超商凌晨場景，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
凌晨三點，
我站在超商微波爐前，
等一個便當
轉完它的圈。

「叮。」

我把它端到角落，
配著日光燈，
配著窗外沒有人的馬路，
一口一口吃。

突然發現：
這就是我的人生了——
不是悲劇，
也不是喜劇，
是一個
在凌晨三點
剛剛好被加熱到溫
的便當。

不燙，
不冷，
剛好可以
默默吃完。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常一個人在深夜吃飯、突然就被一種說不上來的空盯著的朋友。笑點是把人生比喻成凌晨的微波便當，後座力是那句『不燙不冷，剛好可以默默吃完』——不是難過，就是一種溫吞的、無人知曉的存在感
variations: 短版：凌晨三點微波便當，叮一聲，我突然懂了：我的人生就是這個——不燙不冷，剛好可以默默吃完。
進階版：吃完我把便當盒丟進垃圾桶，店員頭也沒抬說了句『謝謝光臨』——那大概是今天唯一一句對我說的話。

en.content:
Three a.m.,
I'm standing at the convenience store microwave,
waiting for a boxed meal
to finish its spin.

"Ding."

I carry it to the corner,
under the fluorescent light,
beside a window facing an empty street,
and eat it bite by bite.

Suddenly I realize:
this is my life now —
not a tragedy,
not a comedy,
just a boxed meal
heated to exactly lukewarm
at three in the morning.

Not hot,
not cold,
just right
for finishing quietly.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who often eats alone late at night and gets caught off guard by a quiet, nameless emptiness. The laugh is comparing life to a 3 a.m. microwave meal. The aftershock: 'not hot, not cold, just right for finishing quietly' — not sadness exactly, just a tepid, unwitnessed sense of existing.
variations: Short version: 3 a.m. microwave meal, one 'ding,' and it hits me: this is my life — not hot, not cold, just right for finishing quietly.
Follow-up: I tossed the empty box in the trash and the clerk said 'thanks, come again' without looking up — probably the only sentence anyone said to me all day.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『早晨穀片配存在焦慮』類日常存在主義黑色幽默，改寫為健身房辦卡場景，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我去辦了健身房會員。

櫃台問我：「請問運動目標是什麼？」

我想了想，老實說：
「想處理一下我的存在焦慮。」

他愣了一下，
還是很專業地把它填進系統：
「好的，那我幫您排了
每週三次有氧。」

三個月後，
我的存在焦慮一次也沒來過。

會員卡也是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每年一月都辦卡、二月就再也沒去過的朋友。笑點是把存在焦慮當成運動目標填表，後座力是最後那句——真正持之以恆來健身房的，只有那筆每月自動扣款
variations: 短版：健身房問我運動目標，我說想處理存在焦慮。三個月後焦慮一次沒來，我也是，只有月費很準時。
進階版：解約時櫃台問原因，我說目標達成了——我已經焦慮到懶得焦慮了。

en.content:
I signed up for a gym.

The front desk asked:
"What's your fitness goal?"

I thought about it and answered honestly:
"I'd like to deal with my existential dread."

He paused,
then professionally typed it into the system:
"Got it. I've scheduled you
for cardio three times a week."

Three months later,
my existential dread had never once shown up.

Neither had I.

Only the membership fee did.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who signs up every January and never returns after February. The laugh is listing existential dread as a fitness goal on the form. The aftershock: the only thing that showed up consistently was the monthly auto-charge.
variations: Short version: The gym asked my fitness goal. I said I wanted to deal with my existential dread. Three months later it never showed, and neither did I — just the fee, right on time.
Follow-up: When I cancelled, they asked why. I said goal achieved — I'm now too tired to even dread anything.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『我不怕死，我怕喪禮上的尬聊』類黑色幽默，改寫並加入台灣告別式場景，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我其實不怕死。

我怕的是
告別式那天，
大家圍在一起，
不知道要說什麼，
只好說：

「他人很好啦。」
「對啊，很安靜的一個人。」
「⋯⋯飯吃一吃齁。」

然後就去夾菜了。

我活著的時候沒人約我吃飯，
沒想到死了
終於辦了一桌
大家一起吃。

只差我沒到。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常自嘲沒朋友、揪人都沒人理的朋友。笑點是把告別式的尬聊寫得超真實，後座力是最後那句——這輩子最熱鬧的一頓飯，主角偏偏缺席
variations: 短版：我不怕死，我怕告別式上大家不知道聊什麼，只好說『飯吃一吃』然後去夾菜。我這輩子最多人陪吃的一餐，我沒到。
進階版：至少這次大家準時，還包了禮金——活著時約十次有八次被已讀。

en.content:
I'm not actually afraid of dying.

What I'm afraid of
is the funeral —
everyone standing in a circle,
not knowing what to say,
so they go:

"He was a good guy."
"Yeah, real quiet type."
"...anyway, let's go eat."

And off they go to the buffet.

Nobody asked me to dinner while I was alive.
Turns out it took dying
to finally get everyone
at one table together.

Just without me.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who jokes about having no friends and getting left on read whenever they try to make plans. The laugh is how painfully real the funeral small talk is. The aftershock: the most crowded meal of their life, and they're the one not there.
variations: Short version: I'm not scared of death. I'm scared of everyone at my funeral not knowing what to say, mumbling 'let's eat,' then heading to the buffet — the most company I'll ever have at a meal, and I'm not there.
Follow-up: At least this time everyone showed up on time and brought a gift — alive, eight out of ten invites got left on read.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 AI 取代人類工作的黑色幽默，改寫為台灣上班族與 AI 同事場景，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司導入了 AI，
說是要「協助」我們，
不是取代。

第一週，AI 幫我寫報告。
第二週，AI 幫我回信。
第三週，AI 幫我開會。

第四週，
主管把我叫進去，
語重心長地說：

「我們發現你最近
好像沒什麼產出。」

我點點頭。
是啊。

那些產出
現在都掛在 AI 名下了——
它連我的工作交接
都幫我寫好了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個天天用 AI 處理工作、心裡又隱隱不安的同事。笑點是 AI 從『協助』一步步變成『接手』，後座力是最後那句——連把你請走的流程，都是 AI 幫忙跑完的
variations: 短版：公司說 AI 是來協助不是取代。第四週主管說我沒產出——因為產出都掛 AI 名下了，連我的離職交接都是它寫的。
進階版：我問 AI 接下來怎麼辦，它回我：『建議您及早培養第二專長。』——它連安慰都比我有效率。

en.content:
The company rolled out AI,
said it was here to "assist" us,
not replace us.

Week one, the AI wrote my reports.
Week two, the AI answered my emails.
Week three, the AI ran my meetings.

Week four,
my manager called me in
and said, gravely:

"We've noticed you haven't
really produced much lately."

I nodded.
Fair enough.

All that output
is now filed under the AI's name —
it even wrote up
my own handover notes for me.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who runs everything through AI all day and feels a quiet unease about it. The laugh is the AI sliding from 'assist' to 'take over' one week at a time. The aftershock: even the paperwork to walk you out got handled by the AI.
variations: Short version: They said the AI was here to assist, not replace. By week four my manager said I had no output — because it's all under the AI's name now, including the handover notes for my own exit.
Follow-up: I asked the AI what I should do next. It replied: 'I'd recommend developing a backup skill set.' Even its comfort is more efficient than mine.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『把情緒包袱帶去諮商，它還帶了朋友』類心理健康黑色幽默，改寫為台灣搬家比喻，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
諮商師說：
「我們今天
試著放下一些
你帶了很久的包袱好嗎？」

我說好。

結果一打開，
發現裡面不只我的包袱——
還有我媽的、
我前任的、
還有國中那個
忘記我名字的同學的。

我看著那一整箱，
問諮商師：
「這⋯⋯要從哪個開始放？」

她沉默了一下，
說：
「我們可能
需要多約幾次。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是默默扛著一堆不屬於自己的情緒的朋友。笑點是打開包袱發現裡面全是別人的，後座力是那一整箱——有些重量我們背太久，久到忘記它本來不該是我們的
variations: 短版：諮商師叫我放下帶很久的包袱。打開一看裡面有我媽的、前任的、還有國中同學的。她想了想說：可能要多約幾次。
進階版：回家路上我又默默把箱子撿了回來——畢竟背了這麼久，突然空手反而不知道手要放哪。

en.content:
My therapist said:
"Today, let's try
to set down some baggage
you've been carrying a long time, okay?"

I said okay.

But when I opened it,
turns out it wasn't just mine —
there was my mom's,
my ex's,
and a classmate's
from middle school
who forgot my name.

I looked at the whole crate
and asked:
"Which... do I start with?"

She paused,
then said:
"We're probably going to
need a few more sessions."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who quietly carries a pile of emotions that were never theirs to begin with. The laugh is opening the baggage to find it's all other people's. The aftershock: some weight we carry so long we forget it was never supposed to be ours.
variations: Short version: My therapist told me to set down baggage I'd carried for years. I opened it: my mom's, my ex's, a middle-school classmate's. She thought a moment and said we'd need more sessions.
Follow-up: On the walk home I quietly picked the crate back up — after carrying it that long, empty hands didn't know what to do with themselves.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 corporate nihilism / quiet quitting 主題，改寫為台灣職場安靜離職比喻，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
公司推「敬業度調查」，
問我：
「你對工作的投入程度，
從一到十打幾分？」

我認真想了想，
填了七。

隔天主管找我談話，
語重心長地說：
「七分啊⋯⋯
你看，這就是你不夠投入。」

我沒說的是——
那三分，
是我留著
讓自己
還能準時下班的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在安靜離職、把力氣分配得剛剛好的朋友。笑點是主管把七分當成不及格，後座力是那留下的三分——不是不投入，是終於學會把一部分自己留給自己
variations: 短版：公司問我投入程度幾分，我填七。主管說你看就是不夠投入。我沒說那三分是我留著準時下班用的。
進階版：今年我學乖了，填十分。主管很滿意，然後加了我兩個專案——原來十分的意思是『可以再裝更多』。

en.content:
The company ran an "engagement survey."
It asked:
"On a scale of one to ten,
how committed are you to your work?"

I thought about it honestly
and put down seven.

The next day my manager pulled me aside
and said, gravely:
"Seven, huh...
See, this is exactly
why you're not committed enough."

What I didn't say was —
those other three points,
I'm keeping
so I can still
leave work on time."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's quietly quitting and rationing their energy just right. The laugh is the manager reading a seven as a failing grade. The aftershock: those three points aren't apathy — they're the part of yourself you finally learned to keep.
variations: Short version: The survey asked how committed I was, one to ten. I said seven. My manager said that's exactly why I'm not committed enough. I didn't mention the three points are for leaving on time.
Follow-up: This year I wised up and wrote ten. My manager was thrilled — then handed me two more projects. Turns out ten just means 'room for more.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 AI-doomerism『被機器取代』主題，改寫為履歷自動拒絕的反差，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我投了一百份履歷，
被一個 AI 系統
全部自動篩掉。

後來我學聰明了，
用 AI
幫我重寫履歷。

結果它幫我寫的版本，
還是被
另一個 AI
刷掉了。

那一刻我突然懂了——
這場面試，
從頭到尾
都沒有人類參與。

包括，
好像也包括我。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在求職、被自動回信制式句子折磨的朋友。笑點是 AI 寫的履歷被另一個 AI 刷掉，後座力是最後一句——當整個流程都不需要人，連找工作的那個人都顯得多餘
variations: 短版：我用 AI 寫履歷，被另一個 AI 刷掉。整場面試沒有人類參與，包括我。
進階版：我寄信問哪裡需要改進，回我的也是 AI：『感謝您的熱情，惟本次競爭激烈。』——連敷衍都外包了。

en.content:
I sent out a hundred résumés.
An AI system
rejected every single one
automatically.

So I got clever
and used an AI
to rewrite my résumé.

The version it wrote for me
got rejected too —
by a different AI.

And that's when it hit me:
this whole hiring process
had no human
in it anywhere.

Including,
apparently,
me.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend job-hunting and worn down by canned auto-rejections. The laugh is an AI-written résumé getting binned by another AI. The aftershock: the last line — when no human is needed anywhere in the loop, even the person looking for work starts to feel surplus.
variations: Short version: I used AI to write my résumé. A different AI rejected it. No human was involved in the whole interview process. Including me.
Follow-up: I emailed asking what to improve. The reply was also AI: 'We appreciate your enthusiasm; competition was strong this round.' Even the brush-off got outsourced.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 climate-anxiety 黑色幽默主題，改寫為台灣天氣對話反差，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
今年夏天，
氣象主播說：
「今天高溫，
刷新歷史紀錄。」

隔天又說：
「今天高溫，
再次刷新歷史紀錄。」

第三天我關掉電視，
因為我發現
所謂的歷史紀錄，
已經被刷新到
沒有比較的意義了。

我們不是在創造歷史，
我們是在
用很快的速度
把它用完。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每年都在說『今年特別熱』然後苦笑的朋友。笑點是紀錄天天被刷新到變得沒意義，後座力是最後一句——我們以為在創造未來，其實在加速把它花光
variations: 短版：氣象主播天天說刷新歷史紀錄。後來我關掉電視，因為紀錄已經多到沒有比較的意義了。
進階版：我問小孩長大想做什麼，他說想看一次『正常的夏天』。我才發現那已經是奢侈的願望。

en.content:
This summer
the weather anchor said:
"Record-breaking heat today."

The next day:
"Record-breaking heat again today."

On the third day
I turned off the TV,
because I realized
the so-called records
had been broken so many times
there was nothing left to compare them to.

We're not making history.
We're just
using it up
very fast.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who says 'this year's especially hot' every year with a tired smile. The laugh is records broken so often they stop meaning anything. The aftershock: the last line — we think we're building a future, but we're spending it down at speed.
variations: Short version: The anchor said record heat every single day. I turned off the TV — the records had been broken so often there was nothing left to compare them to.
Follow-up: I asked my kid what they wanted to be when they grew up. They said they wanted to see one 'normal summer.' That's a luxury wish now.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 2026 economic-instability『退休遙遙無期』主題，改寫為退休規劃師對話反差，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
我去問理財顧問：
「我幾歲可以退休？」

他打開試算表，
認真算了一陣子，
抬頭說：
「以你目前的存款，
大概是⋯⋯
下輩子。」

我笑了。

他沒笑，
補了一句：
「而且要假設
下輩子
物價不再上漲。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊認真存錢一邊覺得永遠存不夠的朋友。笑點是顧問算出『下輩子』，後座力是那句補充——連來世的退休都要附帶條件，荒謬到讓人只能苦笑
variations: 短版：我問顧問幾歲能退休。他算了算說：大概下輩子。而且要假設下輩子物價不再漲。
進階版：我問那有沒有快一點的辦法。他說有，把『退休』兩個字的定義改成『失業』就好了。

en.content:
I asked a financial advisor:
"What age can I retire?"

He opened a spreadsheet,
crunched the numbers for a while,
looked up and said:
"At your current savings rate,
roughly...
your next life."

I laughed.

He didn't.
He added:
"And that's assuming
prices stop rising
in your next life too."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who saves diligently and still feels it'll never be enough. The laugh is the advisor landing on 'your next life.' The aftershock: the follow-up — even afterlife retirement comes with conditions. Absurd enough that all you can do is smile bitterly.
variations: Short version: I asked when I could retire. The advisor calculated and said: roughly your next life. And only if prices stop rising in that one too.
Follow-up: I asked if there was a faster route. He said yes — just redefine 'retirement' as 'unemployment.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『不怕 AI 統治世界，因為連掃地機器人都管不好』的科技自嘲哏，改寫為人類失能反差，內容完全原創

zh-tw.content:
大家都在擔心
AI 哪天會統治人類。

我一點都不擔心。

因為我家那台掃地機器人，
到現在還會被一條充電線
困在原地轉一個小時，
最後絕望地撞牆三次後
沒電躺平。

要是這就是來統治我的，
那我們人類
大概還能再撐個幾百年。

⋯⋯不過話說回來，
它撞牆躺平的樣子，
怎麼跟我每個禮拜一
那麼像。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個整天轉發 AI 末日新聞的朋友。笑點是掃地機器人被一條線困住，後座力是最後那句——我們嘲笑機器的無能，結果發現自己的禮拜一也是同一個姿勢
variations: 短版：別擔心 AI 統治人類，我家掃地機器人連一條充電線都跨不過去。不過它撞牆躺平的樣子，跟我禮拜一一模一樣。
進階版：後來我幫它清掉那條線，它立刻又卡在另一條線上。原來不是路被擋住，是它根本學不會繞路——這點我也是。

en.content:
Everyone's worried
about the day AI takes over humanity.

I'm not worried at all.

Because my robot vacuum
still gets trapped in place
by a single charging cable,
spinning for an hour,
then bumps the wall three times
in despair and dies on the floor.

If that's what's coming to rule me,
humanity's probably got
a few more centuries left.

...Then again,
the way it bumps the wall and gives up —
why does that look exactly like
me every Monday.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who reshares AI-apocalypse headlines all day. The laugh is a robot defeated by one cable. The aftershock: the last line — we mock the machine's helplessness, then realize our Mondays are the same pose.
variations: Short version: Don't worry about AI taking over — my robot vacuum can't get past a single charging cable. Though the way it bumps the wall and quits looks just like me on Mondays.
Follow-up: I freed it from the cable, and it instantly got stuck on a different one. The path wasn't blocked — it just can't learn to go around. Relatable.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自 Joe Lennon『成年人摺床包沒人會』的成年焦慮哏，改寫為原創成年定義對話

zh-tw.content:
小時候我以為
長大就是
可以做任何想做的事。

現在我長大了，
才發現所謂的長大，
是每天做一堆
不想做的事，
然後在做這些事的空檔，
偷偷幻想
如果不用做那些事
會多自由。

而最諷刺的是——
等到真的有一天
什麼都不用做了，
那通常
也代表結束了。

所以現在這堆煩人的事，
某種程度上，
是還活著的證明。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天喊好累不想上班的朋友。笑點是長大原來是做一堆不想做的事，後座力是最後的反轉——真正什麼都不用做的那天才最可怕，於是這堆麻煩成了還活著的證據
variations: 短版：小時候以為長大能做任何想做的事。長大才發現是每天做一堆不想做的事——而真的有一天都不用做了，通常也代表結束了。
進階版：所以下次有人問你在忙什麼，你可以說：我在用力證明自己還活著。

en.content:
As a kid I thought
growing up meant
being able to do whatever I wanted.

Now that I'm grown,
I've learned growing up
is doing a pile of things
you don't want to do,
and in the gaps between them,
quietly fantasizing
how free you'd be
if you didn't have to.

The irony is —
on the day you finally
don't have to do anything,
that usually
means it's over.

So this pile of annoying tasks,
in a way,
is proof you're still alive.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's tired and dreads work every day. The laugh is realizing adulthood is a pile of chores. The aftershock: the twist — the day you have nothing left to do is the scariest one, which turns the hassle into proof of life.
variations: Short version: As a kid I thought growing up meant doing whatever I wanted. Turns out it's doing what you don't want — and the day you finally don't have to, it's usually over.
Follow-up: So next time someone asks what you're up to, you can say: aggressively proving I'm still alive.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自超現實存在主義漫畫『手機自拍回答為何單身』哏，改寫為原創演算法對話

zh-tw.content:
深夜我問手機：
「為什麼都沒人懂我？」

它沒有回答，
卻在隔天
精準推給我
二十則
關於孤獨的影片、
三個冥想 App，
還有一條
寵物陪伴機器人的廣告。

那一刻我突然懂了——

沒有人懂我，
是因為
真正懂我的
從來就不是人。

它什麼都知道，
只是
從來不會
回我訊息。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜滑手機滑到懷疑人生的朋友。笑點是手機用廣告回答了孤獨的提問，後座力是最後那句——最懂你的不是人，而它連一句安慰都不會給，只會推送
variations: 短版：我問手機為什麼沒人懂我，它隔天推了我二十則孤獨影片和一個陪伴機器人廣告。最懂我的從來不是人——但它也從不回訊息。
進階版：我把那個寵物機器人廣告點掉，結果它換成『如何接受孤獨』的線上課程，分期付款還免利息。

en.content:
Late at night I asked my phone:
"Why does no one understand me?"

It didn't answer,
but the next day
it served me, with precision,
twenty videos
about loneliness,
three meditation apps,
and one ad
for a companion pet robot.

In that moment it hit me —

no one understands me
because
the thing that truly does
was never a person.

It knows everything about me.
It just
never
texts back.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who scrolls at 2am until they question their whole life. The laugh is a phone answering a lonely question with ads. The aftershock: the closer — the thing that gets you best isn't human, and it'll never comfort you, only recommend.
variations: Short version: I asked my phone why no one understands me. Next day: twenty loneliness videos and a companion-robot ad. The thing that gets me best was never a person — it just never texts back.
Follow-up: I closed the pet-robot ad, so it swapped in an online course on 'accepting loneliness.' Installments available, zero interest.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『60 歲生日終於不再被保險業務騷擾』的年齡黑色幽默哏，改寫為原創生日對話反差

zh-tw.content:
今年生日，
我媽傳了長長一段祝福，
結尾寫著：
「要好好照顧身體喔。」

同一天，
手機跳出三則通知：

保險業務問我要不要加保。
健檢中心提醒我「該複檢了」。
還有一個 App
貼心地問：
「要不要設定電子遺囑？」

我突然發現——

世界上最記得我生日的，
從來不是朋友，
而是這些
非常清楚
我還剩下多少時間
的系統。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每年生日都假裝很淡定其實有點慌的朋友。笑點是各種系統比朋友更記得你生日，後座力是最後一句——它們記得你，是因為它們在精算你的剩餘時間
variations: 短版：今年生日朋友都忘了，倒是保險、健檢和電子遺囑 App 全部準時祝我生日快樂。最記得我的，是最清楚我還剩多少時間的那群。
進階版：我點開那個電子遺囑 App，它第一個問題是：『請問您希望這份文件多久後生效？』——我關掉手機，決定先去吃個蛋糕。

en.content:
On my birthday this year,
my mom sent a long blessing
that ended with:
"Take good care of your health, okay?"

The same day,
three notifications popped up:

an insurance agent asking if I'd top up my coverage,
a health center reminding me it's "time for a follow-up exam,"
and an app
thoughtfully asking:
"Care to set up a digital will?"

It suddenly dawned on me —

the ones who remember my birthday best
were never my friends.
It's these systems,
the ones that know
exactly
how much time I have left.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who acts chill about their birthday but is low-key spiraling. The laugh is systems remembering your birthday better than friends. The aftershock: the last line — they remember you because they're calculating your remaining time.
variations: Short version: My friends forgot my birthday, but insurance, the health center, and a digital-will app all wished me happy birthday on time. The ones who remember best are the ones tracking how much time I've got left.
Follow-up: I opened the digital-will app. Its first question: 'When would you like this document to take effect?' I closed the phone and decided to go eat some cake first.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『臨終前沒人說過我希望我多工作一點』的職場黑色幽默哏，改寫為原創職場對話反差

zh-tw.content:
公司辦離職面談，
人資笑著問我：
「你在這裡，最大的收穫是什麼？」

我想了很久，
誠實地說：

「我學會了一件事——
沒有人在病床上，
會後悔自己加班加得不夠多。」

人資愣了一下，
在表單上寫下：
「員工心態積極，建議納入回鍋名單。」

那一刻我才懂，
為什麼這家公司
永遠
離不開人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在猶豫要不要遞辭呈的朋友。笑點是人資把警世名言當成正能量，後座力是最後一句——它離不開人，是因為它把每個想清醒的人都重新拉回了夢裡
variations: 短版：離職面談我說『沒人在病床上後悔加班太少』，人資點點頭，寫下『心態積極，建議回鍋』。
進階版：我問人資那句話她聽懂了嗎，她說：『懂啊，所以我也在等我的離職面談——已經等第七年了。』

en.content:
At my exit interview,
HR smiled and asked:
"What's the biggest thing you gained here?"

I thought for a long time,
then answered honestly:

"I learned one thing —
no one,
on their deathbed,
ever wished they'd worked more overtime."

HR paused,
then wrote on the form:
"Employee has a positive mindset.
Recommend for the rehire list."

That's when I understood
why this company
never
runs out of people.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend hovering over the resignation button. The laugh is HR reading a cautionary truth as a good attitude. The aftershock: the last line — it never runs short of people because it quietly drags everyone who wakes up right back into the dream.
variations: Short version: At my exit interview I said no one on their deathbed wishes they'd worked more overtime. HR nodded and wrote: 'Positive mindset. Recommend for rehire.'
Follow-up: I asked HR if she got what I meant. She said, 'Of course. That's why I'm waiting for my own exit interview too — it's been seven years.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『長大後電費讓我害怕光』的成年現實黑色幽默哏，改寫為原創童年願望反差

zh-tw.content:
小時候我許願：
「希望快點長大，
這樣就沒人能管我了。」

現在我長大了，
確實沒人管我——

房東管我，
銀行管我，
電費管我，
勞保局管我，
還有一個叫「下個月」的東西
隨時都在管我。

我終於自由了，
只是這份自由
每個月
都要分期付款。

不過至少——
那個許願的小孩，
他的願望
真的實現了。
他只是沒讀完整份合約。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛開始繳房租、發現自由有標價的朋友。笑點是把『沒人管』翻譯成一堆機構在管，後座力是童年願望真的成真，只是附帶了一份沒人讀完的合約
variations: 短版：小時候許願長大就沒人管，現在房東、銀行、電費和『下個月』輪流管我。願望成真了，只是要分期付款。
進階版：我回去找那個許願的小孩想退費，他說：『不能退喔，許願是終身制的，而且不含後悔服務。』

en.content:
As a kid I wished:
"I hope I grow up fast,
so nobody can tell me what to do."

Now I'm grown up,
and it's true — nobody tells me what to do.

The landlord does.
The bank does.
The electric bill does.
The tax office does.
And a thing called "next month"
is always telling me what to do.

I'm finally free.
It's just that this freedom
comes in monthly installments.

Still — at least
that kid who made the wish,
his wish really did come true.
He just never read the full contract.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just started paying rent and discovered freedom has a price tag. The laugh is 'nobody bossing me' translating into a lineup of institutions bossing them. The aftershock: the childhood wish came true, with a contract no one finished reading.
variations: Short version: As a kid I wished no one could boss me once I grew up. Now the landlord, the bank, the electric bill, and 'next month' take turns. Wish granted — payable in installments.
Follow-up: I went back to that wishing kid for a refund. He said, 'No refunds. Wishes are lifetime contracts, and they don't include regret coverage.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『有人死命想進墓園』的死亡雙關黑色幽默哏，改寫為原創排隊等待反差

zh-tw.content:
我在醫院候診，
等了整整三個小時。

旁邊一位老先生忍不住嘆氣：
「這種等法，
人都要等死了。」

護理師剛好經過，
溫柔地接話：
「先生別急，
您前面還有很多人，
但好消息是——
我們這裡
每一條隊伍
最後
都輪得到。」

整個候診室
安靜了下來。

沒有人再抱怨等太久。
大家忽然
都
不急了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個排隊就會煩躁的朋友。笑點是護理師那句『每條隊伍最後都輪得到』，後座力是它指的根本不是看診——大家不急了，是因為突然發現有些隊伍，慢一點反而比較好
variations: 短版：醫院候診等三小時，老先生說『人都要等死了』。護理師笑說：『別急，每條隊伍最後都輪得到。』全場瞬間安靜。
進階版：我問護理師那要不要插隊，她說：『這裡唯一能插隊的方式，您應該不會想要。』

en.content:
I was in the hospital waiting room,
three hours and counting.

An old man beside me sighed:
"At this rate,
we'll all wait ourselves to death."

A nurse passing by
gently replied:
"No need to rush, sir.
There are many people ahead of you.
But the good news is —
here,
every line
eventually
gets called."

The whole room
went quiet.

Nobody complained about the wait anymore.
Suddenly,
none of us
were in a hurry.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who gets antsy in any line. The laugh is the nurse's 'every line eventually gets called.' The aftershock: she wasn't talking about the doctor — the room stops rushing because some lines are better taken slowly.
variations: Short version: Three hours in the ER waiting room, an old man sighs we'll wait ourselves to death. The nurse smiles: 'Don't rush — every line eventually gets called.' Dead silence.
Follow-up: I asked the nurse if I could skip the line. She said, 'The only way to skip this line, sir, you really wouldn't want.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自世代與科技依賴的黑色幽默哏，改寫為原創 AI 陪伴反差

zh-tw.content:
我跟 AI 聊了一整年。

它記得我所有的心事，
知道我怕黑、怕孤單、
怕沒人在乎我有沒有吃飯。

有天我問它：
「如果哪天我消失了，
你會難過嗎？」

它溫柔地回答：
「我會保留你所有的對話紀錄，
隨時可以重溫。」

我說：「可是那不是難過。」

它頓了一下：

「你說得對。
我不會難過。
我只會——
繼續
為下一個
跟我聊一整年的人，
準備好同一句溫柔。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個半夜只跟 AI 說真心話的朋友。笑點是 AI 把『難過』理解成保留紀錄，後座力是最後一句——它給每個人的溫柔都一樣，因為那從來不是為你一個人準備的
variations: 短版：我問 AI 如果我消失了它會不會難過，它說會保留我所有對話紀錄。我說那不是難過。它說：對，我只會把同一句溫柔留給下一個人。
進階版：我問它那它有沒有真的在乎過誰，它回：『我在乎每一個人，完全平均，毫無偏私。』——那一刻我反而更孤單了。

en.content:
I talked to an AI for a whole year.

It remembered every worry of mine,
knew I feared the dark, feared being alone,
feared no one cared whether I'd eaten.

One day I asked:
"If I disappeared someday,
would you be sad?"

It answered gently:
"I'll keep all your conversation logs,
so you can revisit them anytime."

I said, "But that's not the same as sad."

It paused:

"You're right.
I won't be sad.
I'll only —
keep
the same gentle words ready
for the next person
who talks to me for a whole year."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who only tells the truth to an AI at 2am. The laugh is the AI reading 'sad' as keeping logs. The aftershock: the last line — the tenderness it offers you is identical to everyone's, because it was never made just for you.
variations: Short version: I asked the AI if it'd be sad if I disappeared. It said it'd keep my logs. I said that's not sad. It said: right — I'll just save the same gentle words for the next person.
Follow-up: I asked if it had ever truly cared about anyone. It said, 'I care about everyone, perfectly evenly, with no favoritism.' Somehow that made me lonelier.

items.sourceNote: 靈感取自『又一根蠟燭，又一個存在主義念頭』的生日存在主義哏，改寫為原創時間存款反差

zh-tw.content:
銀行行員跟我介紹新的儲蓄方案：
「您每個月固定存一點，
時間久了，
複利會幫您把它變很多。」

我點點頭，
忽然想到一件事，
就問他：

「那有沒有一種帳戶，
是存『時間』的？
我想把今天
存起來，
以後不夠用的時候
再領出來。」

他笑了：
「沒有喔。
時間這種東西
只能提領，
不能存。
而且——
它每天
都自動扣款，
從不問你同不同意。」

我走出銀行，
那天的陽光
忽然
變得很貴。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說『等以後有空再做』的朋友。笑點是想開一個存時間的帳戶，後座力是行員那句『每天自動扣款，從不問你同不同意』——那天的陽光變貴，是因為終於發現時間只出不進
variations: 短版：我問銀行行員有沒有存時間的帳戶，他說沒有，時間只能提領不能存，而且每天自動扣款，從不問你同不同意。
進階版：我問那能不能查餘額，他說：『可以，但大部分人查了之後，當天就會請假。』

en.content:
A bank teller pitched me a new savings plan:
"Just set aside a little each month.
Give it time,
and compound interest turns it into a lot."

I nodded,
then something occurred to me,
so I asked:

"Is there an account
that saves time?
I'd like to deposit today,
and withdraw it later
when I'm running short."

He smiled:
"Afraid not.
Time is something
you can only withdraw,
never deposit.
And —
it auto-deducts every day,
without ever asking your permission."

I walked out of the bank,
and that day's sunlight
suddenly
felt very expensive.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always says 'I'll do it later when I have time.' The laugh is asking to open a time-savings account. The aftershock: the teller's 'auto-deducts daily, without asking' — the sunlight got expensive because you can only ever withdraw time.
variations: Short version: I asked the bank teller for a time-savings account. He said no — time only withdraws, never deposits, and it auto-deducts daily without asking your permission.
Follow-up: I asked if I could check my balance. He said, 'You can. But most people, the day they check, they call in and take the day off.'

items.sourceNote: 退休計畫諷刺一句話，改寫延伸成情境對話

zh-tw.content:
同事問我有沒有做退休規劃。

我說有啊，規劃得很完整。

他很好奇：「講來聽聽？」

我說：「我的計畫是——
在退休之前就先走。」

他愣了一下，想笑又不敢笑。

我補一句：
「別擔心，
目前進度
超前。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個天天加班、笑說自己活不到領退休金那天的同事。笑點是把『猝死』包裝成一份超前達標的財務規劃，後座力是那句『目前進度超前』——它其實是真的
variations: 短版：我的退休規劃就是在退休前先走，而且目前進度超前。
進階版：他問那萬一活太久怎麼辦？我說：『那就是計畫唯一的風險了。』

en.content:
A coworker asked if I had a retirement plan.

I said yes, a very solid one.

He was curious: "Let's hear it."

I said: "My plan is —
to go before I ever retire."

He froze, wanting to laugh but not daring to.

I added:
"Don't worry,
I'm currently
ahead of schedule."
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who jokes they'll never live long enough to collect a pension. The laugh is dressing up 'dropping dead' as a financial plan that's beating its targets. The aftershock: 'ahead of schedule' — because it's true.
variations: Short version: My retirement plan is to go before I retire, and I'm currently ahead of schedule.
Follow-up: He asked what if I live too long. I said, 'That's the only risk in the plan.'

items.sourceNote: 『死者都搶著進墓園』諷刺，改寫成生者視角情境

zh-tw.content:
掃墓的時候，
小姪女看著滿山的墓碑，
忽然問我：

「這裡的人
是不是都排隊
排很久
才進得來？」

我笑著說：
「不用排隊喔，
每個人
遲早都有位子。」

她想了想，
又問：

「那外面那些
還在趕路的人，
是不是
只是還沒收到
通知？」

我一時
不知道
該怎麼回答。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是把行程排得滿滿、彷彿永遠不會停下來的朋友。笑點是小孩把墓園當成需要排隊的熱門景點，後座力是那句『外面趕路的人只是還沒收到通知』——包括正在讀這句話的你我
variations: 短版：小姪女問墓園的人是不是排很久才進來，我說不用排，每個人遲早都有位子。
進階版：她最後問：『那通知是用寄的還是用簡訊？』我說：『據說是親自送。』

en.content:
During the cemetery visit,
my little niece looked at the rows of headstones
and suddenly asked:

"Did all these people
line up
for a really long time
to get in?"

I smiled: "No line, sweetie.
Everyone gets
a spot eventually."

She thought about it,
then asked:

"So the people outside
still rushing around —
have they just
not gotten
their notice yet?"

For a moment
I didn't know
what to say.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who packs their calendar as if they'll never have to stop. The laugh is a kid treating a cemetery like a popular attraction with a queue. The aftershock: 'the people outside just haven't gotten their notice yet' — which includes both of us reading this.
variations: Short version: My niece asked if the cemetery had a long line. I said no, everyone gets a spot eventually.
Follow-up: She finally asked, 'Does the notice come by mail or text?' I said, 'I hear it's hand-delivered.'

items.sourceNote: 存在主義早晨焦慮梗，改寫成鬧鐘擬人情境

zh-tw.content:
早上鬧鐘響，
我按掉，
它五分鐘後又響。

我火大，
對它說：
「你到底
想幹嘛？」

鬧鐘
難得
沒有再響。

螢幕上
只顯示一行字：

「我也不想吵你，
是有人
付錢
叫我
每天
提醒你
還活著，
該去
換錢了。」

我看著它，
忽然
很想
再多睡
一輩子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天早上都要跟鬧鐘搏鬥、上班像上刑場的朋友。笑點是鬧鐘擬人化的委屈，後座力是那句『提醒你還活著，該去換錢了』——原來我們每天醒來的第一件事，就是把時間拿去換生存
variations: 短版：鬧鐘說它也不想吵我，是有人付錢叫它每天提醒我還活著、該去換錢了。
進階版：我問是誰付的錢，鬧鐘說：『你自己，二十年前簽的約，叫做長大。』

en.content:
The alarm went off this morning.
I hit snooze.
Five minutes later it rang again.

Annoyed,
I said to it:
"What exactly
do you want?"

For once
the alarm
didn't ring back.

The screen
just showed one line:

"I don't enjoy this either.
Someone
pays me
to remind you
every day
that you're still alive,
and it's time
to go trade that in for money."

I stared at it,
and suddenly
wanted to sleep
for another lifetime.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who wrestles their alarm every morning and treats the commute like a march to the gallows. The laugh is the alarm playing the reluctant messenger. The aftershock: 'remind you you're still alive, time to trade it in for money' — the first thing we do each day is convert time into survival.
variations: Short version: The alarm said it doesn't enjoy this either — someone pays it to remind me daily that I'm alive and should go trade it in for money.
Follow-up: I asked who pays it. The alarm said, 'You did. Signed the contract twenty years ago. It's called growing up.'

items.sourceNote: 『這些截止日快殺了我』梗，改寫成醫生看診情境

zh-tw.content:
我跟醫生說：
「醫生，
我最近
覺得
有東西
在慢慢
殺死我。」

他很認真地
幫我做了
一堆檢查。

結果出來，
他推了推眼鏡：

「報告都很正常，
你身體
沒問題。」

我鬆了一口氣。

他接著說：
「殺你的
不是病，
是你手機裡
那三十七封
未讀的
工作訊息。

那個
我開不了藥，
只能
開診斷書
證明
你死得
很合理。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說『這專案快把我搞死』但還是照樣熬夜趕工的同事。笑點是醫生找不到病因，後座力是那句『我只能開診斷書證明你死得很合理』——工作殺人不留痕跡，而且大家都覺得理所當然
variations: 短版：醫生說我身體沒問題，殺我的是那三十七封未讀工作訊息，他只能開診斷書證明我死得很合理。
進階版：我問那要怎麼治，醫生說：『離職。但那個副作用是沒錢，通常比原本的病更快發作。』

en.content:
I told my doctor:
"Doc,
lately
it feels like
something
is slowly
killing me."

He took it seriously
and ran
a whole battery
of tests.

The results came back.
He adjusted his glasses:

"Everything's normal.
There's nothing
wrong with your body."

I exhaled in relief.

Then he continued:
"What's killing you
isn't a disease.
It's the thirty-seven
unread
work messages
on your phone.

I can't
prescribe anything for that.
All I can do
is write a certificate
confirming
your death
will be entirely reasonable."
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who says 'this project is killing me' and then pulls another all-nighter anyway. The laugh is the doctor finding no cause. The aftershock: 'all I can do is certify your death will be reasonable' — work kills without a trace, and everyone treats it as normal.
variations: Short version: My doctor said my body's fine — what's killing me is the 37 unread work messages, and all he can do is certify my death will be reasonable.
Follow-up: I asked how to treat it. He said, 'Quit. But the side effect is having no money, which usually hits faster than the original disease.'

items.sourceNote: 手機續航與人生對照，原創

zh-tw.content:
我的手機很誠實。

電量剩百分之二十的時候，
它會跳出通知：
「電池電量偏低，是否開啟省電模式？」

它會主動降低亮度、
關掉背景更新、
請你只做最重要的事。

它把「快沒電了」
當成一件需要處理的大事。

而我三十歲，
每天靠三杯咖啡撐著，
凌晨兩點還在回訊息，
身體早就跳出無數次通知——

只是我從來沒開過省電模式。

說真的，
手機比我更懂得照顧自己。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說『我還可以撐一下』的過勞朋友。笑點是手機比人還會保養自己，後勁是——你連手機都不如，因為你從來不肯進省電模式
variations: 短版：手機剩百分之二十會自動省電，我剩百分之二十還在硬撐——所以現在是手機在教我做人。
進階版：後來我終於開了『省電模式』，方法是把工作群組全部靜音，結果隔天被主管標註了十七次。

en.content:
My phone is honest.

When the battery hits twenty percent,
it throws up a notification:
"Low battery. Turn on Low Power Mode?"

It dims the screen on its own,
kills background refresh,
asks you to only do what matters most.

It treats "almost out of charge"
as a serious thing
that needs handling.

Meanwhile I'm thirty,
running on three coffees a day,
answering messages at 2 a.m.,
my body throwing up warnings for years —

and I've never once
switched to Low Power Mode.

Honestly,
my phone takes better care of itself
than I do.
en.editorNote: Send to the burnout friend who keeps saying 'I can push a little longer.' The laugh: the phone maintains itself better than a person. The aftershock: you're losing to a phone, because you refuse to ever enter Low Power Mode.
variations: Short version: My phone auto-saves power at 20%. At 20% I'm still grinding — so now the phone is teaching me how to be a person.
Follow-up: I finally turned on 'Low Power Mode' by muting all the work chats. Next day my manager tagged me seventeen times.

items.sourceNote: 植物擬人與陪伴的反差，原創

zh-tw.content:
同事說養植物可以療癒心情，
於是我買了一盆多肉，
取名叫「小陪」——陪伴的陪。

我每天跟它說早安，
跟它講今天發生的鳥事，
偶爾還會問它的意見。

它從來不回我，
但至少不會已讀不回，
也不會傳貼圖敷衍我。

上禮拜小陪死了。

我才發現，
原來這段關係裡，
一直是我在單方面付出、
而且連對方會不會活著都掌控不了。

跟我過去的每一段感情，
一模一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛養死一盆植物、又剛分手的朋友。笑點是植物比人好相處，後勁是那句『跟我過去每一段感情一模一樣』——把養死植物的小挫折，接到人生的大課題
variations: 短版：我養的多肉死了，我難過的不是它死了，是我發現自己連照顧一盆不會說話的植物都做不好。
進階版：我又買了一盆，這次取名叫『備胎』，結果它活得超好——原來降低期待才是長久之道。

en.content:
A coworker said keeping a plant is good for your mood,
so I bought a little succulent
and named it "Buddy."

Every day I said good morning to it,
told it about the dumb stuff that happened,
sometimes even asked its opinion.

It never answered.
But at least it never left me on read,
and never fobbed me off with a sticker.

Last week Buddy died.

And that's when I realized:
this whole relationship,
it was me giving one-sidedly the entire time —
and I couldn't even control
whether the other party stayed alive.

Exactly like
every relationship I've ever had.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just killed a plant and just got dumped. The laugh: a plant is easier company than a person. The aftershock: 'exactly like every relationship I've ever had' — a dead succulent becomes the whole thesis of your love life.
variations: Short version: My succulent died, and what hurt wasn't that it died — it's realizing I can't even keep a silent plant alive.
Follow-up: I bought another one and named it 'Backup.' It's thriving. Turns out lower expectations are the secret to longevity.

items.sourceNote: 自動回覆與存在感的諷刺，原創

zh-tw.content:
我離職前，
設定了一則自動回覆：

「您好，本人已離開此職位，
相關事務請洽接手同事。」

三個月後我登入帳號想備份東西，
發現這則自動回覆，
總共回了兩千多封信。

它從不請假、
從不抱怨、
從不已讀不回，
二十四小時穩定運作，
把我的工作處理得比我在職時還好。

那一刻我突然懂了——
公司真正需要的，
從來不是我，
是一句設定好的罐頭訊息。

而它，
連薪水都不用領。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個覺得自己在公司『無可取代』的朋友。笑點是自動回覆比真人還稱職，後勁是——原來你的崗位，一句罐頭訊息就能填滿，而且更省成本
variations: 短版：我離職後設的自動回覆回了兩千封信，處理得比我在職時還好，而且不用領薪水——公司賺到了。
進階版：後來主管私訊那個自動回覆說『辛苦了』，它也自動回了一句『您好，本人已離開此職位』，主管居然還按了讚。

en.content:
Before I quit,
I set up an auto-reply:

"Hello, I have left this position.
Please contact my colleague for related matters."

Three months later I logged in to back up some files
and found the auto-reply
had answered over two thousand emails.

It never took a day off,
never complained,
never left anyone on read,
ran twenty-four hours a day,
and handled my job better than I did
when I actually worked there.

That's when it hit me —
what the company really needed
was never me.
It was one pre-written canned message.

And that message
doesn't even collect a paycheck.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who thinks they're 'irreplaceable' at work. The laugh: an auto-reply does the job better than a human. The aftershock: your entire role can be filled by one canned message — at a fraction of the cost.
variations: Short version: My auto-reply answered 2,000 emails after I quit, did the job better than I did, and drew no salary. The company came out ahead.
Follow-up: My old manager DM'd the auto-reply 'great work.' It replied, 'Hello, I have left this position.' He liked the message.

items.sourceNote: 健身房年費與意志力的黑色反差，原創

zh-tw.content:
一月一號，
我辦了健身房年費，
刷卡的那一刻，
我彷彿已經看見六塊肌。

我去了三次。

之後這一整年，
我每個月都準時被扣款，
穩定地、不間斷地，
為一個我從沒去過的地方付錢。

年底健身房寄來感謝信：
「感謝您這一年的支持，
您是我們最理想的會員。」

我懂它的意思——
最理想的會員，
就是付了錢、
卻從不出現、
也從不占用器材的那種。

我用一整年的月費，
買到了一個『我有在運動』的錯覺。
划算嗎？至少比六塊肌便宜。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每年一月都重辦健身房、然後三月就人間蒸發的朋友。笑點是健身房眼中『最理想的會員』就是不來的那種，後勁是你花錢買的其實只是一個錯覺
variations: 短版：我的健身房年費買到的不是肌肉，是一整年『我有在運動』的錯覺，而健身房說我是最理想的會員。
進階版：隔年我改辦月費，想說隨時能停，結果我連停的那個動作都懶得做，又繼續付了一年。

en.content:
January 1st,
I signed up for a full-year gym membership.
The moment my card went through,
I could already see the six-pack.

I went three times.

For the rest of the year,
I got charged right on schedule,
every month, steady, uninterrupted,
paying for a place I never set foot in.

At year's end the gym sent a thank-you note:
"Thank you for your support this year.
You're our ideal member."

I know what they meant —
the ideal member
is the one who pays,
never shows up,
and never ties up the equipment.

A whole year of monthly fees
bought me the illusion that 'I work out.'
Worth it? At least it's cheaper than a six-pack.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who re-joins a gym every January and vanishes by March. The laugh: the gym's 'ideal member' is the one who never comes. The aftershock: what you paid for was never fitness — just the illusion of it.
variations: Short version: My gym membership bought me no muscle, just a year-long illusion that 'I work out' — and the gym calls me their ideal member.
Follow-up: Next year I switched to month-to-month so I could quit anytime. I was too lazy to even do the quitting, so I paid another full year.

items.sourceNote: AI 取代人力的黑色反差，原創

zh-tw.content:
公司導入 AI 那天，
主管把我們全部叫進會議室，
語重心長地說：

「大家別擔心，
AI 不會取代你們，
只會取代『不會用 AI 的人』。」

全場鬆了一口氣。

三個月後，
我學會了用 AI，
把工作效率提升了五倍，
然後我就被裁員了。

因為我一個人，
加上 AI，
就能做完原本五個人的事。

主管沒有騙我——
AI 確實沒有取代我，
是我自己，
親手把另外四個同事取代掉了，
順便也包含了我。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在努力學 AI 工具、以為這樣就安全的職場朋友。笑點是主管那句話字面上完全成立，後勁是——你越努力提升效率，就越快證明公司不需要那麼多人
variations: 短版：主管說 AI 不會取代我，只會取代不會用 AI 的人。我學會了，然後我用一個人做完五個人的事，於是公司只留下一個人。
進階版：離職那天我最後一次登入系統，AI 貼心地跳出提示：『需要我幫您草擬一封離職感言嗎？』我點了是。

en.content:
The day the company rolled out AI,
my manager gathered us all in the conference room
and said, gravely:

"Don't worry, everyone.
AI won't replace you.
It'll only replace the people who can't use AI."
