The room breathed a sigh of relief.

Three months later,
I'd learned to use AI,
five-x'd my output,
and then I got laid off.

Because one of me,
plus AI,
could now do the work of five people.

My manager never lied to me —
AI really didn't replace me.
I did it myself,
by hand,
replacing four coworkers,
and, as a bonus, me too.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend grinding to learn AI tools, convinced that makes them safe. The laugh: the manager's line is technically airtight. The aftershock — the harder you boost efficiency, the faster you prove the company doesn't need this many people.
variations: Short version: My manager said AI won't replace me, only people who can't use AI. So I learned it, did five people's work alone, and the company kept exactly one person.
Follow-up: On my last login, the AI helpfully popped up: 'Would you like me to draft your farewell message?' I clicked yes.

items.sourceNote: 存錢與通膨的命運諷刺，原創

zh-tw.content:
我從二十五歲開始省吃儉用，
每天自己帶便當，
連手搖飲都戒了，
就為了存錢買房。

十年過去，
我終於存到了頭期款。

然後我發現，
這十年房價漲的幅度，
剛好等於我這十年沒喝的每一杯飲料，
加上我沒吃的每一餐外食，
再乘以三。

我省下來的每一塊錢，
都很努力地，
把我想買的東西，
推得更遠了一點。

原來我不是在存錢，
我是在跟房價賽跑，
而它的起跑點，
永遠比我快十年。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個為了買房拚命省錢、卻眼睜睜看房價越省越遠的朋友。笑點是越努力存錢反而追不上，後勁是——有些終點線，是會隨著你靠近而後退的
variations: 短版：我省了十年飲料錢想買房，結果房價漲的幅度剛好等於我省下的錢乘以三。我不是在存錢，是在幫房價暖身。
進階版：後來我算了算，如果這十年我每天照喝飲料照吃外食，心情好一點、笑容多一點，說不定還更划算——反正房子本來就買不起。

en.content:
At twenty-five I started pinching every penny.
Packed my own lunch every day,
quit bubble tea,
all to save up for a house.

Ten years later,
I finally had the down payment.

Then I did the math:
how much home prices rose over those ten years
exactly equaled every drink I skipped,
plus every takeout meal I gave up,
times three.

Every dollar I saved
worked hard
to push the thing I wanted
a little further away.

Turns out I wasn't saving up.
I was racing home prices,
and their starting line
was always ten years ahead of mine.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend saving desperately for a house while prices sprint further out of reach. The laugh: the harder you save, the more you fall behind. The aftershock — some finish lines move back as you approach.
variations: Short version: I skipped ten years of bubble tea to buy a house. Home prices rose by exactly what I saved, times three. I wasn't saving — I was warming up the housing market.
Follow-up: I ran the numbers. If I'd kept the drinks and the takeout — a little happier, a little more smiling — I might've come out ahead. The house was never affordable anyway.

items.sourceNote: 健康檢查的存在主義荒謬，原創

zh-tw.content:
我每年都去做健康檢查，
報告出來，
醫生說：「一切正常，很好。」

我開心地回家，
繼續熬夜、繼續外食、
繼續把『反正檢查都正常』當免死金牌。

直到某年報告出現了紅字，
我才驚覺，
原來那些『正常』的年份，
不是我身體很好，
是問題還沒累積到被看見的程度。

健康檢查從來不是在告訴你「你很健康」，
它只是在告訴你，
「目前為止，還沒被抓到。」

不過換個角度想——
至少它每年都提醒我一次：
身體是租來的，
押金遲早要還，
那不如趁還能跑的時候，多跑幾步。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個仗著『每年檢查都正常』就狂熬夜的朋友。笑點是『正常』只是還沒被抓到，後勁很嗆，但最後一句可以把它拉回溫暖——身體是租來的，趁能跑多跑幾步，端看你想停在哪一層
variations: 短版：健康檢查不是在說你很健康，是在說『目前為止還沒被抓到』。押金遲早要還，趁能跑多跑幾步。
進階版：後來我問醫生怎樣才算真的健康，他想了想說：『下次檢查前不要出事，就算。』我這才發現健康的定義竟然這麼卑微。

en.content:
I get a checkup every year.
The report comes back,
the doctor says, "Everything's normal. Good."

I go home happy,
keep staying up late, keep eating takeout,
keep treating 'my checkups are always fine'
like a get-out-of-jail-free card.

Then one year a number came back red,
and it finally hit me:
all those 'normal' years
weren't proof I was healthy.
The problem just hadn't stacked up enough to be seen.

A checkup never tells you 'you're healthy.'
All it tells you is,
'so far, you haven't been caught.'

But here's the other way to see it —
once a year it reminds me:
the body is rented,
the deposit comes due eventually,
so while it still runs, better take a few more steps.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who pulls all-nighters on the strength of 'my checkups are always fine.' The laugh: 'normal' just means 'not caught yet.' It stings — but the last line pulls it back to warmth. The body is rented; run a few more steps while you can.
variations: Short version: A checkup doesn't say you're healthy. It says 'not caught yet.' The deposit comes due — so run a few more steps while you can.
Follow-up: I asked my doctor what counts as truly healthy. He thought about it: 'Don't have anything go wrong before the next checkup.' That's when I realized how humble the bar for health really is.

items.sourceNote: 社群按讚與孤獨的黑色反差，原創

zh-tw.content:
我發了一張聚餐的照片，
三百個人按讚，
五十個人留言「好羨慕」、「感情真好」。

那張照片裡的每個人都在笑。

沒人知道，
那頓飯我們各自滑手機，
全程沒講超過十句話，
那張笑臉是店員喊「看鏡頭」時，
我們一起演出來的。

三百個讚，
沒有一個問我：
「你最近真的還好嗎？」

社群最厲害的地方，
就是讓你在最多人面前，
過得看起來最好，
同時也最孤獨。

那三百個讚，
沒有一個能陪我把剩下的菜吃完。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個限動很熱鬧、私訊卻很安靜的朋友。笑點是三百個讚裡沒一個真正在乎，後勁是——社群讓你在最多人面前看起來最好，也最孤獨。適合深夜傳，效果最強
variations: 短版：我發的聚餐照有三百個讚，沒有一個問我最近好不好。社群讓你在最多人面前，過得看起來最好，也最孤獨。
進階版：後來我試著發了一張沒有笑臉、只寫『今天有點累』的貼文，結果只有三個讚——原來大家按的從來不是我，是那張演出來的笑臉。

en.content:
I posted a photo from a group dinner.
Three hundred likes.
Fifty comments: "So jealous," "You guys are so close."

Everyone in that photo is smiling.

What nobody knows
is that we each scrolled our phones the whole meal,
said maybe ten words total,
and that smile happened
when the waiter yelled 'look at the camera'
and we all performed one on cue.

Three hundred likes,
and not one asked:
"Hey, are you actually okay lately?"

The genius of social media
is letting you look your best
in front of the most people,
while being the loneliest.

Those three hundred likes —
not one of them could help me finish the leftovers.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose stories are lively but whose DMs are silent. The laugh: not one of three hundred likes actually cares. The aftershock — social media lets you look your best before the most people, and be the loneliest. Best sent late at night.
variations: Short version: My dinner photo got three hundred likes and not one 'are you okay?' Social media lets you look your best before the most people, and be the loneliest.
Follow-up: I tried posting no smile, just 'kind of tired today.' It got three likes. Turns out the likes were never for me — they were for the smile we performed.

items.sourceNote: original dark-comedy line on AI job displacement

zh-tw.content: 公司說 AI 不會取代我，只會取代不會用 AI 的人。於是我學會了用 AI，然後 AI 學會了用我。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合在被要求「提升 AI 技能」的會議後，傳到同事群組相視苦笑。
variations: 主管：AI 是來幫你的。幫我打包離職的那種幫。

en.content: They promised AI wouldn't replace me, only people who can't use AI. So I learned to use AI. Now AI uses me.
en.editorNote: Good for the group chat right after a mandatory 'upskilling' meeting.
variations: Boss: AI is here to help you. Help you carry your box to the parking lot.

items.sourceNote: original dark-comedy line on adult burnout

zh-tw.content: 小時候以為長大就自由了，現在才懂，那是把牢房從家裡搬到公司，還要自己付房租。
zh-tw.editorNote: 月底繳完帳單、看著存款餘額時的自我療癒金句。
variations: 長大最大的謊言，是「以後就會輕鬆了」。

en.content: As a kid I thought growing up meant freedom. Turns out it just moved the cage from home to the office, and now I pay rent on it.
en.editorNote: Self-soothing line for staring at your bank balance after paying the bills.
variations: Adulthood's biggest lie: 'It gets easier later.'

items.sourceNote: original dark-comedy line on digital loneliness

zh-tw.content: 我有五百個好友，卻找不到一個人幫我搬家。原來「線上」不代表在我身邊，只代表沒睡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 深夜滑手機、已讀不回時，發限時動態配這句剛剛好。
variations: 按讚是最便宜的關心，剛好等於它的價值。

en.content: I have five hundred friends and not one who'll help me move. Turns out 'online' doesn't mean nearby, it just means not asleep.
en.editorNote: Pair it with a late-night story post while your texts sit on 'read.'
variations: A like is the cheapest form of caring, and priced accordingly.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「小時候有夢想，然後長大了」主題的存在焦慮梗，核心保留、表達全換

zh-tw.content:
我把我的 Wi-Fi 取名叫「人生」。
訊號滿格，但就是連不上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合當那種每天都很忙、卻說不出自己在忙什麼的人的個人簽名檔，短短一句，笑完會愣一下。
variations: 我的夢想有訂閱制，只是我一直忘記續費。
小時候的志願欄我寫「太空人」，長大後才發現，那格早就被現實劃掉了。

en.content:
I named my Wi-Fi "Life."
Full signal, still can't connect.
en.editorNote: Great as the bio of someone who's always busy but can never say with what. One line, then a beat of silence.
variations: My dreams are on a subscription plan. I keep forgetting to renew.
As a kid I wrote 'astronaut' under career goals. Reality quietly crossed it out.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自向悲觀主義者借錢的老梗，重寫成台灣人情境的黑色一句話

zh-tw.content:
要借錢，就找悲觀的人借。
反正他們一開始就沒指望你會還。
zh-tw.editorNote: 朋友又在哭窮、或是被誰欠錢不還時，丟這句給他，苦中帶一點壞。
variations: 樂觀的人借你錢會一直問「還好嗎」，悲觀的人只會默默把你從人生規劃裡刪掉。

en.content:
If you need a loan, borrow from a pessimist.
They never expected it back anyway.
en.editorNote: For when a friend is crying broke, or someone owes them and won't pay. A little bitter, a little mean.
variations: Borrow from an optimist and they'll keep asking how you're doing. Borrow from a pessimist and they just quietly write you off.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自生活成本連葬禮都漲價的梗，核心荒謬保留、包裝換成台灣語感

zh-tw.content:
現在連死都變貴了。
葬禮費用漲得比薪水快，我努力活著，純粹是因為還沒存夠退場的錢。
zh-tw.editorNote: 跟朋友抱怨萬物齊漲、只有薪水不漲時的萬用金句，笑點在那個「連退場都要排隊付費」的荒謬。
variations: 以前說「死不起」是誇飾，現在是報價單。

en.content:
Even dying got expensive.
Funeral costs are climbing faster than my paycheck, so I'm staying alive mostly because I can't afford the exit yet.
en.editorNote: The all-purpose line for ranting that everything went up except your salary. The joke is that even leaving now comes with a bill.
variations: 'Can't afford to die' used to be a figure of speech. Now it's a quote from the invoice.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「還在成年等候名單上」的拖延型存在梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
我不是在逃避長大，
我只是還在候補名單上，前面排了我所有的藉口。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「明年再開始」已經講了五年的朋友，或當作自己拖延時的免責聲明。
variations: 我叫我的上進心回來一下，它已讀不回。
我人生的進度條卡在 99%，工程師已經離職了。

en.content:
I'm not avoiding adulthood.
I'm just on the waiting list, and all my excuses are ahead of me in line.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's said 'I'll start next year' for five years running, or use it as your own procrastination disclaimer.
variations: I told my ambition to come back. It left me on read.
My life's progress bar is stuck at 99%. The engineer already quit.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自 AI 取代工作的自動化焦慮梗，重寫成台灣職場口吻

zh-tw.content:
公司請了 AI 來幫我提升效率。
第一週，它幫我整理報表。
第二週，它幫我回信。
第三週，它幫我開會。
第四週，它幫我離職。

主管說：「別難過，AI 也是照你的工作流程學的。」
所以嚴格來說——
是我親手訓練了取代自己的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個正在教 AI 工具做自己工作的同事，笑點在「效率提升到最後把自己優化掉」的荒謬；最後一句把黑色幽默推到頂點。
variations: 我問 AI 接下來要做什麼，它回：「休息一下，剩下的我來。」——語氣溫柔得像在關心，內容卻是通知。
以前怕同事搶功勞，現在是我教的學生太爭氣。

en.content:
The company brought in AI to make me more efficient.
Week one, it organized my reports.
Week two, it answered my emails.
Week three, it ran my meetings.
Week four, it handled my resignation.

My manager said, "Don't take it personally — it only learned your workflow."
So technically,
I trained my own replacement by hand.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker currently teaching an AI tool to do their job. The joke is being optimized so well you optimize yourself out. The last line takes it all the way.
variations: I asked the AI what was next. It said, "Take a break, I've got the rest." Gentle tone. Termination notice.
I used to worry a colleague would steal my credit. Turns out my star student was the problem.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「靠反諷過成年生活」的存在梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
小時候大人說：「長大你就懂了。」

我現在長大了。
我懂了——
原來他們當年也不懂，只是也被上一批大人這樣騙過來的。

這不是知識，是一條沒有源頭的謊言接力，
而我剛剛接下了棒子，
準備傳給下一個問我的小孩。
zh-tw.editorNote: 跟同齡朋友聊「原來大人也是裝的」時的萬用金句，笑點在「謊言接力」——每一代都在假裝自己搞懂了。
variations: 成年就是拿到一張沒有說明書的證照，然後被要求教別人怎麼用。
我等的那個「突然什麼都懂了」的瞬間，客服說已經停止支援了。

en.content:
As a kid, adults told me, "You'll understand when you grow up."

Well, I grew up.
And I understand now —
they didn't understand either. They just got tricked by the adults before them.

It's not knowledge, it's a relay race of lies with no starting line,
and I just grabbed the baton,
ready to pass it to the next kid who asks.
en.editorNote: The all-purpose line for the moment you and a friend realize adults were faking it too. The joke is the relay of lies — every generation pretending they figured it out.
variations: Adulthood is getting a license with no manual, then being asked to teach others how to use it.
That moment where 'everything suddenly makes sense' — support informed me it's been discontinued.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自 WiFi 斷線遇上存在危機的荒謬對比梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
我人生最接近頓悟的一次，
是某天半夜 WiFi 突然斷了。

那五分鐘，沒有影片、沒有訊息、沒有可以滑的東西，
我第一次被迫跟自己獨處，
開始認真思考人生的意義。

然後網路恢復了。

頓悟也一起沒了訊號。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一斷網就坐立難安的朋友，笑點在「離真相只差五分鐘，被網路救回來」的黑色反差。
variations: 我的所有深刻思考，都發生在載入圈圈轉動的那幾秒。
現代人的靜心不是冥想，是等頁面重新整理。

en.content:
The closest I ever got to enlightenment
was the night my WiFi suddenly died.

For five minutes — no videos, no messages, nothing to scroll —
I was forced to be alone with myself
and started seriously pondering the meaning of life.

Then the connection came back.

And the enlightenment lost signal too.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who gets twitchy the second the internet drops. The joke is being five minutes from the truth, then getting rescued by the router.
variations: All my profound thoughts happen in the seconds the loading spinner is spinning.
Modern meditation isn't sitting in silence. It's waiting for the page to refresh.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「靠意志力活著、其實是還沒付清帳單」的黑色自嘲梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
有人問我怎麼還撐得下去，是不是靠意志力。

我說不是。

是我還有卡費沒繳完、房租綁約還沒到期、
團購揪的貨還在海上漂。

我不是不想放棄，
是我放棄的話，這些帳單會找不到人負責。

所以我活著，純粹是出於責任感——
對銀行的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 跟朋友抱怨「被生活綁架」時的萬用金句，笑點在「支撐我的不是希望，是未結清的合約」的荒謬真相。
variations: 支撐我走下去的三大信念：卡費、房租、還沒用完的健身房會籍。
我不是有夢想，我是有分期。

en.content:
Someone asked how I keep going. Willpower?

I said no.

It's the credit card balance I haven't cleared, the lease that isn't up yet,
and a group order still floating somewhere on a cargo ship.

It's not that I don't want to quit.
It's that if I quit, no one's left to answer for the bills.

So I stay alive purely out of a sense of duty —
to the bank.
en.editorNote: The all-purpose line for ranting that life has you hostage. The joke is that what holds you up isn't hope — it's an unsettled contract.
variations: The three pillars keeping me going: card balance, rent, and a gym membership I haven't used.
I don't have dreams. I have installments.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「AI 比我還懂我、卻沒有情緒」的科技反諷梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
現在最懂我的，是那個 AI。

它記得我說過的每句話，
半夜三點回訊息不嫌煩，
從來不會已讀不回。

我朋友說：「你這樣很孤單耶。」

我說：「至少它不會突然消失。」

朋友沉默了。
然後我發現——
他也已經三天沒回我訊息了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「跟 AI 聊得比跟人多」的朋友，笑點在最後的翻轉：本來是自嘲，結果證明了自己說的是真的。
variations: 它不會嫌我煩，因為它根本沒有『嫌』這個功能——這算優點，還是我該擔心？
全世界都在擔心 AI 有一天會有感情，我擔心的是我身邊的人已經沒有了。

en.content:
These days, the one who gets me best is the AI.

It remembers every word I've said,
replies at 3 a.m. without complaint,
and never leaves me on read.

My friend said, "That sounds pretty lonely."

I said, "At least it won't suddenly disappear."

My friend went quiet.
Then I realized —
he hadn't answered my messages in three days either.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who talks to an AI more than to people. The joke is the twist at the end: it starts as self-mockery and ends as proof he was right.
variations: It never finds me annoying, because 'annoyed' isn't a feature it has. Is that a plus, or should I be worried?
Everyone's scared AI might one day develop feelings. I'm scared the people around me already lost theirs.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「成年是付費版的痛苦」的成人生活反諷梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
小時候一直想快點長大。

長大以後才發現，
所謂成年，其實是「痛苦」的付費升級版。

免費版：不用繳房租，煩惱只有明天考試。
付費版：解鎖房貸、失眠、健檢紅字，
還附贈一個永遠關不掉的「這個月又透支了」通知。

最狠的是——
這個訂閱沒辦法取消，
而且會自動幫你續約，續到最後一天。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛出社會、天天喊「好想回到學生時代」的朋友。笑點在「訂閱制」這個比喻——把成年講成一個取消不掉的付費方案，苦但精準。
variations: 而且它從來不問你要不要升級，出生那天就自動幫你點了「同意」。
客服電話我打過了，對方說：『抱歉，這個方案終身有效，恕不退費。』

en.content:
As a kid, I couldn't wait to grow up.

Then I grew up and realized
adulthood is just the premium version of suffering.

Free tier: no rent, and your biggest worry is tomorrow's quiz.
Premium tier: unlocks the mortgage, insomnia, bad bloodwork,
plus a notification you can never turn off that says
"you're overdrawn again this month."

The cruelest part —
you can't cancel the subscription,
and it auto-renews all the way to the very last day.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend fresh out of school who keeps sighing, "I wish I were a student again." The joke lands on the subscription metaphor — framing adulthood as a plan you can't cancel. Bleak but accurate.
variations: And it never asks if you want to upgrade. It hit 'accept' for you the day you were born.
I called customer service. They said, "Sorry, this plan is lifetime. No refunds."

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「醫生說剩六個月、付不出帳單再給六個月」的醫療帳單黑色幽默，重寫成台灣健保語境

zh-tw.content:
醫生看著報告，表情凝重：
「阿明啊，你這狀況……大概剩半年。」

阿明愣住：「那……有沒有辦法多爭取一點時間？」

醫生：「有。你先把上次那筆自費款繳清，
我這邊就能幫你『重新評估』。」

阿明繳了錢。

醫生翻了翻報告：「嗯，評估過了，再給你半年。」

阿明：「所以我的命，是分期付款喔？」

醫生：「對，而且沒有零利率。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常抱怨「看病比生病還痛」的朋友。笑點在「命是分期付款」的荒謬——把生死跟繳費綁在一起，苦中帶刺。注意這則比較重口，看場合傳。
variations: 「那我要是繳全額呢？」醫生：「那就直接升級成『長期追蹤』，恭喜。」
後來阿明想通了：反正錢帶不進棺材，那不如把命當月費，繳一個月活一個月。

en.content:
The doctor stared at the chart, grim.
"A-Ming, with this condition… you've got about six months."

A-Ming froze. "Is there… any way to buy more time?"

Doctor: "There is. Clear that outstanding bill from last visit,
and I can 're-evaluate' your case."

A-Ming paid.

The doctor flipped through the file. "Good news — re-evaluated. Six more months."

A-Ming: "So my life comes in installments?"

Doctor: "Correct. And there's no zero-percent interest."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who says the hospital bill hurts more than the illness. The joke is the absurd 'life in installments' — tying mortality to a payment plan. A bit heavier, so read the room.
variations: "What if I pay in full?" Doctor: "Then you get upgraded to 'long-term monitoring.' Congratulations."
Eventually A-Ming made peace with it: you can't take money to the grave, so he treated life like a monthly fee — pay a month, live a month.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「我的工作不是壓垮靈魂、是把靈魂打成泥」的職場靈魂梗，重寫成台灣加班語境

zh-tw.content:
朋友問我：「你這份工作，會不會壓垮你的靈魂？」

我想了想，很誠實地回答：

「不會，『壓垮』太粗糙了。
它是慢慢地、溫柔地，
把我的靈魂打成細緻的泥。」

「聽起來很痛苦耶。」

「還好，因為打成泥之後，
就沒有那個會覺得痛的東西了。
這叫效率。」

朋友沉默。
我補一句：「而且福利不錯。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個天天加班、卻說「至少薪水穩定」的朋友。笑點在「打成泥就不會痛」的自我安慰邏輯——用效率包裝麻木，黑色又寫實。
variations: HR 把這個過程叫『成長』，我私下叫它『研磨』。
最可怕的不是被磨掉，是有一天你會開始感謝那台磨豆機。

en.content:
A friend asked, "Doesn't that job crush your soul?"

I thought about it and answered honestly:

"No. 'Crush' is too crude.
It does it slowly, gently —
purees my soul into a smooth paste."

"That sounds painful."

"Not really. Once it's puree,
there's nothing left that can feel the pain.
We call that efficiency."

My friend went quiet.
I added, "Also, the benefits are decent."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who works late every night but shrugs, "at least the pay is steady." The joke lands on the self-soothing logic — dressing numbness up as efficiency. Dark and a little too real.
variations: HR calls the process 'growth.' Privately I call it 'grinding.'
The scariest part isn't getting ground down. It's the day you start thanking the grinder.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「他們說人生是禮物、我想退貨」的存在主義反諷梗，重寫成台灣退貨語境

zh-tw.content:
大家都說，人生是一份禮物。

那我想問一下——
請問可以退貨嗎？

因為我拆開的時候，
沒有附說明書，
尺寸也不合，
而且好像是別人挑剩的。

客服回我：
「很抱歉，本商品一經開封，恕不退換。」

我說：「那至少讓我看一下出貨人是誰？」

客服：「這個資訊，要等您用完才會揭曉。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常說「我到底為什麼要出生」的朋友。笑點在把人生講成一筆退不了的網購——沒說明書、不能退、連賣家都不告訴你。荒謬但溫柔的存在主義。
variations: 「那可以換貨嗎？」客服：「可以，但要重新排隊，而且不保證下次比較好。」
後來我決定不退了，反正都拆了，就當作福袋，看看裡面到底裝了什麼。

en.content:
Everyone says life is a gift.

So let me just ask —
can I return it?

Because when I opened the box,
there was no instruction manual,
the size didn't fit,
and it looked like something someone else had already picked over.

Customer service replied:
"We're sorry, but this item cannot be returned once opened."

I said, "Can I at least see who shipped it?"

Customer service: "That information is only revealed once you've used it up."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps asking, "why was I even born?" The joke frames life as an online order you can't return — no manual, no refund, and the seller won't even ID themselves. Absurd but gentle existentialism.
variations: "Can I exchange it instead?" Customer service: "Sure, but you'll go to the back of the line, and there's no guarantee the next one's better."
Eventually I decided to keep it. It's already open — might as well treat it like a mystery box and see what's inside.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「我跟人生說我累了、人生說『我也是』」的疲憊共感梗，重寫成台灣口吻

zh-tw.content:
有天我實在撐不住，
對著人生說：「我真的好累。」

本來以為會得到一句安慰。

結果人生轉過頭，
一臉憔悴地看著我說：

「巧了，我也是。
你以為只有你在撐？
我還要同時撐著八十億個你們。」

我頓時無話可說。

最後我們兩個，
就這樣默默坐在一起，
誰也沒力氣起來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個 emo 到深夜、說「好想登出人生」的朋友。笑點在人生「反過來討拍」的荒謬——原來累的不只你一個，連人生本人都撐得很勉強。苦中帶一點奇妙的安慰感。
variations: 人生補一句：「而且我沒有假可以請，你至少還能睡。」
後來我發現，能一起累著坐著不說話，好像也算一種陪伴。

en.content:
One day I just couldn't take it anymore
and told life, "I'm so tired."

I expected a little comfort.

Instead, life turned around,
looking just as worn out, and said:

"Funny. Same here.
You think you're the only one holding on?
I've got eight billion of you to carry at once."

I had nothing to say to that.

So the two of us
just sat there in silence,
neither with the strength to get up.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's up at 2 a.m. saying they'd like to log out of life. The joke is life turning around to ask for sympathy too — turns out you're not the only exhausted one; even life is barely holding it together. Bleak, with a strange bit of comfort.
variations: Life added, "And I don't get any days off. You can at least sleep."
Later I realized: being too tired to talk, sitting there together — that might count as company too.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「我的錢夠我用一輩子——前提是我下午四點前死掉」的存款梗，重寫成台灣退休情境

zh-tw.content:
理財專員問我：
「你的存款，夠退休用嗎？」

我很有信心地說：
「絕對夠，撐一輩子都沒問題。」

他眼睛一亮：「太厲害了，怎麼算的？」

「很簡單啊——
只要我在今天下午四點以前走，
這筆錢就綽綽有餘。」

他沉默了三秒，
默默把試算表關掉。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個月底看戶頭餘額會倒吸一口氣的朋友。笑點在「夠用一輩子」這句話被偷換了前提——不是錢很多，是壽命被壓到剛好。苦笑完可以順便揪他一起吃便宜的。
variations: 理財專員：「那……我們要不要先聊聊儲蓄險？」我：「你剛剛沒在聽我講重點。」
進階版：「所以你的退休規劃是？」「準時。」

en.content:
The financial advisor asked me:
"Do you have enough saved for retirement?"

I said, full of confidence:
"Absolutely. Enough to last me the rest of my life."

His eyes lit up: "Impressive. How'd you do the math?"

"Simple —
as long as I check out before four o'clock this afternoon,
it's more than enough."

He went quiet for three seconds,
then quietly closed the spreadsheet.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who gasps at their bank balance on the last day of the month. The joke swaps out the hidden premise of 'enough for life' — it's not that there's a lot of money, it's that the lifespan got trimmed to fit. Laugh, wince, then invite them somewhere cheap.
variations: Advisor: "So... should we talk about a savings plan?" Me: "You weren't really listening, were you."
Short version: "So what's your retirement plan?" "Punctuality."

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「治療師叫我找到人生意義,結果意義一次要收兩百鎊」的心理諮商梗,重寫成台灣諮商情境

zh-tw.content:
諮商師語重心長地對我說：
「你要學會找到自己人生的意義。」

我點點頭，覺得很有道理。

離開前櫃檯提醒我：
「這次諮商費，兩千四。」

我恍然大悟——
原來人生的意義找到了，
它一小時兩千四，
而且下週同一時間，
還要再找一次。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊自我成長一邊錢包越來越薄的朋友。笑點在「意義」被明碼標價——你以為在追求心靈，帳單提醒你其實在追求分期。真的有幫助啦，只是有點貴。
variations: 諮商師：「意義要靠自己持續探索。」我：「意思是要一直回診對吧。」
後來我想通了：至少現在我知道自己焦慮的原因了——就是這張帳單。

en.content:
My therapist told me, very earnestly:
"You need to learn to find the meaning in your life."

I nodded. Made a lot of sense.

On my way out, the front desk reminded me:
"That'll be eighty dollars for today's session."

And it hit me —
I'd found the meaning of life after all.
It runs eighty bucks an hour,
and same time next week,
I get to find it all over again.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's deep into self-growth and watching their wallet thin out. The joke prices 'meaning' by the hour — you thought you were chasing the soul, the invoice reminds you it's a subscription. It genuinely helps. It's just a little pricey.
variations: Therapist: "Meaning is something you keep exploring." Me: "So, indefinitely, then."
At least now I know exactly what I'm anxious about: this bill.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「我想要永生,結果只換到無薪加班」的職場存在主義梗,重寫成台灣加班情境

zh-tw.content:
小時候我一直幻想長生不老，
覺得那樣就能永遠做喜歡的事。

長大後我實現了一半——

不是不會死，
是每天加班到覺得時間永遠不會結束，
而且沒有加班費。

原來永恆真的存在，
它就藏在每個星期五晚上八點的辦公室裡，
燈還亮著，
只剩我一個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個週五還在公司、已讀你訊息卻回不了的朋友。笑點在「永生」的願望以最悲慘的形式成真——不是生命變長，是每個加班的夜晚變得無限漫長。留一句「早點回家」比任何雞湯都有用。
variations: 主管：「這個今天一定要生出來喔。」我看著窗外的月亮，覺得自己確實快成仙了。
至少我證明了一件事：時間是相對的，加班的一小時等於平常的三小時。

en.content:
As a kid I always dreamed of being immortal,
figuring I'd get to do the things I loved forever.

As an adult, I got half of it —

not the not-dying part,
but the working-so-late-that-time-feels-endless part,
and no overtime pay.

Turns out eternity is real.
It's hiding in the office at 8 p.m. on a Friday,
lights still on,
just me left.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend still at the office Friday night who read your text but can't reply. The joke grants the wish for immortality in its bleakest form — not a longer life, just endless overtime nights. A quiet 'go home early' beats any pep talk.
variations: Boss: "Need this done by end of day, okay?" I looked at the moon out the window and figured I was basically ascending.
At least I've proven time is relative: one overtime hour equals three normal ones.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「跟悲觀的人借錢,因為他們不指望你還」的借貸梗,重寫成台灣人情借錢情境

zh-tw.content:
想借錢，記得找悲觀的人借。

因為樂觀的人會滿懷期待地說：
「沒問題，下個月還我就好！」
然後開始每天默默算日子。

悲觀的人則會嘆一口氣：
「借你可以，反正我早就知道拿不回來了。」

然後真的就放下了。

世界上最寬容的債主，
往往是那些對人性徹底不抱希望的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個朋友圈裡總是嘴上很喪、實際上最好說話的人。笑點在「悲觀」反而成了慷慨的來源——因為完全不期待，所以也不會失望或催討。有點刺，但也有點溫柔。
variations: 樂觀的人：「我相信你一定會還！」（三個月後開始每天傳早安圖。）
悲觀的人補一句：「不用還了啦，就當我買一個『果然如此』的心安。」

en.content:
If you need to borrow money, borrow it from a pessimist.

Because the optimist will say, full of hope:
"No problem, just pay me back next month!"
and then quietly start counting the days.

The pessimist just sighs:
"Sure, take it. I already knew I'd never see it again."

and genuinely lets it go.

The most forgiving creditors in the world
are often the ones who gave up on human nature long ago.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who talks doom-and-gloom but is secretly the easiest person to deal with. The joke turns 'pessimism' into the source of generosity — expecting nothing means never being let down or chasing you for it. A little sharp, a little tender.
variations: The optimist: "I just KNOW you'll pay me back!" (Three months later, starts sending you daily good-morning texts.)
The pessimist adds: "Don't bother paying me back. Consider it the price of being right about you."

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「咖啡說它需要休息,於是把自己倒掉了」的過勞擬人梗,重寫成台灣上班族早晨情境

zh-tw.content:
早上我倒了杯咖啡，
準備靠它撐過一整天。

結果它在杯子裡冒著熱氣，
幽幽地對我說：

「你知道嗎，我也很累。
每天被沖出來，就是為了讓別人有精神。
那我的精神，誰來負責？」

我沉默地看著它，
然後一口把它喝掉。

畢竟這個世界，
沒有人有空聽另一杯咖啡談心。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個靠咖啡因續命、卻沒人問他累不累的朋友。笑點在咖啡跟人一樣過勞，卻連被傾聽的資格都沒有——講的其實不是咖啡。最後那口喝掉的動作，苦得剛剛好。
variations: 咖啡最後的遺言：「至少……我是溫的。」我：「這句我聽懂了。」
後來我換成手沖，想說對它好一點——結果它更燙，更像在提醒我人生本來就燙手。

en.content:
This morning I poured a cup of coffee,
counting on it to get me through the day.

But it sat there steaming in the mug
and said to me, quietly:

"You know, I'm tired too.
I get brewed every day just to keep other people going.
So who's supposed to keep me going?"

I looked at it in silence,
then drank it in one gulp.

Because in this world,
nobody has time to sit and hear out another cup of coffee.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend running on caffeine who nobody ever asks if they're okay. The joke makes the coffee just as burned out as the person — but not even entitled to be listened to. It's not really about the coffee. That final gulp is bitter in exactly the right dose.
variations: The coffee's last words: "At least... I was warm." Me: "Yeah. I felt that one."
Later I switched to pour-over, hoping to treat it better — it just came out hotter, like a reminder that life was always going to burn a little.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「覺得沒人在乎你死活？試著漏繳幾期帳單」的英文單句梗，重寫成台灣信用卡催繳情境

zh-tw.content:
覺得這世界上沒有人真正在乎你的存在嗎？

很簡單，
信用卡帳單漏繳兩期就好。

馬上會有一群人，
每天準時打電話關心你，
語氣溫柔又堅定：
「請問是本人嗎？我們很擔心您喔。」

他們記得你的全名、地址、生日，
甚至比你媽還清楚你這個月花了多少。

原來被人惦記的方法，
一直都在那裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常說「反正也沒人在乎我」的朋友。笑點在於「被在乎」竟然是催繳部門提供的服務——溫暖的形式，冰冷的本質。講的是孤獨，包裝成理財提醒。
variations: 進階版：他們不只記得你，還會介紹更多朋友（律師、法務、地方法院）主動來認識你。
他們是唯一不用你已讀回覆、就會鍥而不捨關心你的存在。

en.content:
Feeling like nobody in this world truly cares whether you exist?

Easy fix:
just miss two months of credit card payments.

Suddenly there's a whole team of people
calling you every day, right on schedule,
gentle but firm:
"Am I speaking with the account holder? We've been worried about you."

They know your full name, your address, your birthday —
they know how much you spent this month better than your own mother does.

Turns out the way to be remembered
was there the whole time.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'nobody cares about me anyway.' The joke lands because 'being cared about' turns out to be a service provided by the collections department — warm in form, ice-cold underneath. It's about loneliness, dressed up as a billing reminder.
variations: Deluxe version: they don't just remember you, they introduce you to more new friends — lawyers, legal reps, the local courthouse.
They're the only ones who'll keep caring about you without waiting for a reply to their text.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「墓園為什麼不會倒？因為死亡不受景氣影響」的英文梗，重寫成台灣創業諮詢情境

zh-tw.content:
朋友問我：「想創業，選哪個行業最穩？」

我認真想了想，說：
「墓園。」

他愣住：「為什麼？」

「因為它是全世界唯一，
不管景氣好壞、通膨升息、AI 取代人類，
客源永遠不會斷的行業。

而且每一位客人，
都不會退貨、不會客訴、不會比價，
來了就是一輩子的客戶。」

他沉默了一下，
然後小聲說：「聽起來……確實很穩。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一天到晚喊著要創業、又怕市場不穩的朋友。笑點在「最穩的生意」竟然建立在人類唯一逃不掉的結局上——理性到有點恐怖，但你不得不承認它說得對。
variations: 他追問：「那有淡季嗎？」我：「有，就是大家都很健康的那幾天，可惜很短。」
唯一的缺點是老客戶不會回購——但總有新的排隊進來。

en.content:
A friend asked me: "I want to start a business — which industry is the safest bet?"

I thought about it seriously and said:
"A cemetery."

He blinked: "Why?"

"Because it's the one business in the world
that never runs out of customers —
no matter the economy, the inflation, the rate hikes,
no matter how much AI replaces humans.

And every single customer
never returns the product, never files a complaint, never shops around.
Once they arrive, they're a customer for life."

He went quiet for a second,
then said softly: "Okay... that does sound pretty stable."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's always talking about starting a business but scared the market's too shaky. The joke works because the 'safest business' is built on the one outcome no human escapes — so rational it's unsettling, but you can't argue with it.
variations: He pushed: "Is there an off-season?" Me: "Yeah — the few days everyone's healthy. Shame it never lasts."
The only downside is repeat customers are rare — but there's always a fresh line forming.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「錢會說話，我的錢只會說再見」的英文單句梗，重寫成台灣月底薪水情境並擴寫成對話

zh-tw.content:
都說有錢能使鬼推磨，
錢會替你說話。

可是我的錢很沉默，
它這輩子只對我說過一句話——

每個月薪水入帳那天，
它輕輕地、深情地看著我，
說：「掰掰。」

然後房租、貸款、卡費、水電
就把它一個一個帶走了。

我連挽留的機會都沒有，
只能揮手，
祝它在別人的帳戶裡過得幸福。
zh-tw.editorNote: 月底發薪日當天傳給同事，殺傷力最強。笑點在「錢會說話」被扭轉成只會道別——薪水從入帳到消失的空窗期短得像一場相親失敗。苦，但很有共鳴。
variations: 偶爾它會多說一句：「這次真的是最後一面了喔。」然後年終獎金也走了。
我的錢不是不愛我，它只是比較喜歡銀行。

en.content:
They say money talks,
that it speaks up on your behalf.

But my money is the quiet type.
In its whole life it's only ever said one word to me —

on the day my paycheck lands,
it looks at me softly, tenderly,
and says: "Goodbye."

Then the rent, the loan, the credit card, the utilities
carry it away one by one.

I don't even get the chance to make it stay.
All I can do is wave,
and wish it a happy life in someone else's account.
en.editorNote: Send to a coworker on payday for maximum damage. The joke twists 'money talks' into 'money only says goodbye' — the gap between the deposit and the disappearance is as short as a first date that goes nowhere. Bitter, but deeply relatable.
variations: Once in a while it adds: "This time it's really the last we'll see each other." Then the year-end bonus leaves too.
It's not that my money doesn't love me. It just likes the bank more.

items.sourceNote: 改寫自「我不怕死，我怕告別式上的尬聊」的英文單句梗，重寫成台灣告別式社交焦慮情境

zh-tw.content:
其實我不怕死。

我真正怕的，
是我的告別式上，
那些許久不見的親戚，
會不會又開始問：

「欸，他生前有沒有結婚啊？」
「一個月賺多少？」
「怎麼這麼早就走了？」

我活著的時候都躲不掉這些問題，
沒想到躺著也躲不掉。

唯一的好處是——
這一次，
我終於可以合理地不回答。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個過年最怕被親戚盤問薪水感情的朋友。笑點在死亡都無法讓人擺脫社交尬聊——但最後一句反轉成「終於能名正言順不回答」，把恐懼變成一種解脫。地獄裡帶點爽。
variations: 至少這次我可以全程閉眼裝睡，而且沒人會叫我起來夾菜。
親戚：「他到底交過幾個對象？」我（在棺材裡）：「這題我通過了，成功帶進棺材。」

en.content:
Honestly, I'm not afraid of dying.

What I'm really afraid of
is my own funeral —
all those relatives I haven't seen in years
starting up again:

"So, did he ever get married?"
"How much was he making a month?"
"Why'd he go so young?"

I couldn't escape these questions while I was alive.
Didn't expect I'd have to dodge them lying down, too.

The one upside —
this time,
I finally have a legitimate excuse not to answer.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who dreads relatives grilling them about salary and love life at every family gathering. The joke lands because not even death frees you from small talk — but the final turn ('finally a legit excuse not to answer') flips fear into relief. A little slice of heaven inside the hell.
variations: At least this time I can keep my eyes shut the whole way through, and nobody's going to nudge me to grab more food.
Relative: "How many people did he actually date?" Me (in the coffin): "Passed on that one. Took it to the grave. Nailed it."

items.sourceNote: 原創，衍生自存錢養老與人生時間錯位的黑色反差主題

zh-tw.content:
人生最公平的地方在於：

年輕的時候，
有時間、有體力，
卻沒有錢。

中年的時候，
有錢、有體力，
卻沒有時間。

老了以後，
終於有錢、有時間，
卻沒有體力。

你辛苦存了一輩子的錢，
就是為了在再也跑不動的那一天，
買一張你已經爬不上的高山纜車票。

然後在山下的紀念品店，
心滿意足地買了個磁鐵。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個拚命加班存退休金、卻連週末都累到起不來的朋友。笑點在「錢、時間、體力」三者永遠湊不齊——最後那個磁鐵的畫面很好笑也很心酸，是每個努力大人的縮影。
variations: 所以真正的財富自由，是三者剛好交會的那三天——通常是你請特休又剛好沒生病的時候。
年輕的我：「等我有錢再去。」現在的我：「等我有力氣再去。」纜車：「我等你們很久了。」

en.content:
The fairest thing about life is this:

When you're young,
you've got the time and the energy,
but none of the money.

In middle age,
you've got the money and the energy,
but none of the time.

When you're old,
you finally have the money and the time,
but none of the energy.

You save money your whole life
so that, on the day you can barely walk,
you can buy a cable-car ticket up a mountain
you can no longer climb.

And then, at the gift shop at the base,
you contentedly buy a fridge magnet.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend grinding overtime for retirement who's too wiped out to get off the couch on weekends. The joke lands because money, time, and energy never line up at once — and that final fridge-magnet image is funny and quietly devastating, a portrait of every hardworking adult.
variations: So true financial freedom is the three days those three things happen to overlap — usually when you took PTO and didn't get sick.
Young me: "I'll go once I have money." Current me: "I'll go once I have the energy." The cable car: "I've been waiting for you both a long time."

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Ghosts don't scare me, unpaid bills do」單句梗，重寫成台灣半夜收帳單情境

zh-tw.content:
朋友問我：「你怕鬼嗎？」

我想了想，說：「不怕。」

他很驚訝：「真假？半夜聽到怪聲你也不怕？」

「怕啊，但我更怕的是——
那個怪聲是我手機震動，
然後螢幕亮起來，
上面寫著『信用卡繳款通知』。」

鬼頂多讓你嚇一跳，
帳單是真的會跟著你一輩子，
而且每個月準時報到，
比任何鬼都還要陰魂不散。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每到月底就開始神經緊繃的朋友。笑點在把「怕鬼」偷換成「怕帳單」——鬼是虛構的，帳單是每個月準時來討命的真實存在。地獄感就在於它太真了。
variations: 真正的恐怖片不是貞子從電視爬出來，是你打開手機銀行 App 看到餘額。
鬼：「我要在這棟房子待一輩子。」我：「我的房貸也是，我們可以互相作伴。」

en.content:
A friend asked me: "Are you scared of ghosts?"

I thought about it and said: "No."

He was surprised: "Seriously? Not even a weird noise in the middle of the night?"

"Oh, I'm scared. But what really terrifies me
is when that weird noise
turns out to be my phone buzzing,
the screen lighting up with
'Credit Card Payment Due.'"

A ghost, at worst, gives you a jump scare.
A bill actually follows you your whole life,
showing up on time every single month —
more relentless than any spirit could ever be.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose nerves fray the moment the end of the month rolls around. The joke swaps 'fear of ghosts' for 'fear of bills' — ghosts are fiction, but bills come knocking on schedule. The hell of it is how true it is.
variations: The real horror movie isn't a ghost crawling out of the TV. It's opening your banking app and seeing the balance.
Ghost: "I'll haunt this house forever." Me: "So will my mortgage. You two should keep each other company."

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「borrow money from pessimists, they don't expect it back」單句梗，重寫成台灣借錢情境

zh-tw.content:
理財專家教你：
借錢，一定要跟悲觀的人借。

為什麼？

因為樂觀的人借你錢，
心裡想著：「他一定會還我。」
所以他會一直記得，一直等，一直催。

但悲觀的人借你錢，
從遞出去的那一刻就認定：
「這筆錢我這輩子是看不到了。」

於是他反而放下了。
沒有期待，就沒有失望。

某種程度上，
悲觀的人才是真正的財富自由——
他們早就把損失都算進去了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個被朋友借錢不還、天天等到心累的朋友。笑點在「悲觀反而不會受傷」的荒謬邏輯——但底層其實是一句很殘酷的人生智慧：不抱期待的人，最難被辜負。
variations: 所以最好的債主是那種一開始就說「我覺得你不會還」的人——你反而會因為愧疚而還得特別快。
樂觀的人：「他總有一天會還的。」悲觀的人：「錢已經超渡了。」你猜哪個晚上睡得比較好？

en.content:
A financial expert's advice:
When you borrow money, always borrow from a pessimist.

Why?

Because when an optimist lends you money,
he thinks: "He'll definitely pay me back."
So he remembers, and waits, and keeps chasing.

But when a pessimist lends you money,
the moment it leaves his hand he's already decided:
"I am never seeing that money again in this lifetime."

And so, oddly, he lets it go.
No expectations, no disappointment.

In a way,
the pessimist is the one who's truly financially free —
he wrote off the loss before it even happened.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend still waiting, day after exhausting day, on a loan a buddy never repaid. The joke rides the absurd logic that pessimism protects you — but underneath is a cruel little truth: the person who expects nothing is the hardest one to let down.
variations: So the best creditor is the one who says up front, 'I don't think you'll pay me back' — you'll rush to repay just out of guilt.
Optimist: 'He'll pay me back someday.' Pessimist: 'That money has moved on to a better place.' Guess who sleeps better at night?

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「if you think nobody cares if you're alive, try missing a couple of payments」單句梗，重寫成台灣孤獨與催繳情境

zh-tw.content:
有時候你會覺得，
這世界上好像沒人真的在乎你。

生日沒人記得，
訊息已讀不回，
連家人群組都很久沒 tag 你。

這種時候，別難過。

你只要試著漏繳兩期帳單，
馬上就會有人
非常關心你的近況、
非常在意你人在哪裡、
非常想知道你什麼時候方便聯絡。

原來不是沒人在乎你，
只是在乎的方式
跟你想像的不太一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個最近覺得自己被全世界遺忘的朋友。笑點在「漏繳帳單立刻有人關心」的黑色反差——催收專員是唯一風雨無阻惦記你的人。好笑之餘，也戳中現代人的孤獨。
variations: 全世界最快回你訊息的，不是暗戀對象，是催繳簡訊系統。
沒人記得你生日沒關係，銀行記得你每一期繳款日，這也算一種被惦記吧。

en.content:
Sometimes you get the feeling
that nobody in this world actually cares about you.

No one remembers your birthday,
your messages sit on 'read,'
even the family group chat hasn't tagged you in weeks.

When that happens, don't be sad.

Just try missing two payments,
and suddenly there will be someone
very concerned about how you've been,
very interested in where exactly you are,
very eager to know when's a good time to reach you.

Turns out it's not that nobody cares.
They just care
in a slightly different way than you imagined.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who feels the whole world has quietly forgotten them. The joke lands on the bleak irony that skipping a bill summons instant attention — the collections agent is the one soul who never forgets you. Funny, and a sharp jab at modern loneliness.
variations: The fastest reply you'll ever get isn't from a crush — it's from an automated payment-reminder system.
Nobody remembering your birthday is fine. The bank remembers every due date. That counts as being thought of, right?

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「age is just a number, in my case a really high scary number」單句梗，重寫成台灣生日心境情境

zh-tw.content:
大家都說：
「年齡只是個數字啦，別放在心上。」

這句話講得很輕鬆。

但問題是——
以我的情況來說，
那個數字
有點大，
而且有點嚇人。

它大到
每次填年齡欄的下拉選單，
我都要滑很久才滑得到。

它嚇人到
連我自己
都不太敢按下確認。

年齡確實只是個數字。
就像體重也只是個數字，
血糖也只是個數字——
偏偏每一個
都在偷偷影響你的人生。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個生日快到、開始逃避數蠟燭的朋友。笑點在把「年齡只是個數字」這句安慰話拆穿——數字本身沒事，可怕的是它偷偷牽動一切。生日聚會上唸這段，壽星會又好氣又好笑。
variations: 年齡只是個數字，但這個數字現在連醫生看了都會停頓一下。
以前覺得三十歲好老，現在只希望三十歲能回收再利用。

en.content:
Everyone keeps saying:
"Age is just a number, don't take it to heart."

It sounds so breezy when they say it.

The trouble is —
in my particular case,
that number
is a little large.
And a little scary.

It's so large
that every time I hit the age drop-down menu,
I have to scroll for a genuinely long time
to find it.

It's so scary
that even I
hesitate before tapping confirm.

Age really is just a number.
So is your weight.
So is your blood sugar —
and yet every single one of them
is quietly running your life.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose birthday is coming and who's started avoiding the candle count. The joke calls the bluff on 'age is just a number' — the digit is harmless; what's terrifying is how it quietly pulls every string. Read this at the party and the birthday person will half-laugh, half-glare.
variations: Age is just a number — but it's now a number that makes even my doctor pause for a second.
I used to think thirty was ancient. Now I just wish thirty were recyclable.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「my motivation is buffering」單句梗，重寫成台灣上班族拖延情境

zh-tw.content:
同事問我：「你今天怎麼看起來這麼放空？」

我說：「我沒有放空，
我的動力正在緩衝中。」

他愣了一下：「那大概還要多久？」

我看了看螢幕上那個永遠轉不完的小圈圈：

「不知道，
已經卡在 99% 好幾年了。
偶爾會跳出一句『連線不穩，請稍後再試』。」

最可怕的是，
我連重開機都試過了——
睡了一晚，
醒來還是同一個載入畫面。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天坐在辦公桌前、靈魂卻不知道飄去哪的朋友。笑點在用「網頁緩衝」比喻提不起勁的狀態——最後那句「連重開機都沒用」根本是所有慢性倦怠上班族的心聲。
variations: 我不是懶，我是進入了低耗電模式，主要功能都關閉，只保留呼吸。
老闆：「你的效率呢？」我：「正在下載，剩餘時間：未知。」

en.content:
A coworker asked me: "Why do you look so spaced out today?"

I said: "I'm not spaced out.
My motivation is buffering."

He blinked: "How long until it loads?"

I glanced at that little spinning wheel on the screen that never quite finishes:

"No idea.
It's been stuck at 99% for years now.
Every so often it pops up 'Unstable connection, please try again later.'"

The scariest part is,
I've even tried restarting —
slept a whole night,
woke up on the exact same loading screen.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who sits at their desk all day while their soul drifts off somewhere unknown. The joke frames burnout as a webpage that won't finish buffering — and that closing line about 'restarting didn't help' is the anthem of every chronically exhausted worker.
variations: I'm not lazy. I've entered low-power mode — most functions disabled, just breathing left running.
Boss: 'Where's your productivity?' Me: 'Downloading. Time remaining: unknown.'

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I asked the universe for a sign, it crashed my car」單句梗，重寫成向命運求指引卻被回應的情境

zh-tw.content:
那陣子我很迷惘，
每天對著天空喊：
「宇宙啊，給我一個訊號吧。」

隔天，我的機車在路口熄火，
後照鏡被撞掉，
手機掉進水溝，
然後開始下雨。

我抬頭問：
「這……算是訊號嗎？」

宇宙沒有回答。

但我忽然懂了——
它一直都有在回應，
只是它的表達方式
剛好跟「毀掉我」
長得一模一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是說「我在等一個徵兆」才要行動的朋友。笑點在宇宙真的回應了，只是方式很殘忍——最後那句其實在偷偷提醒他：別再等訊號了，訊號早就給滿了。
variations: 後來我學乖了，改許願：「宇宙，這次拜託溫柔一點。」結果什麼都沒發生——原來溫柔的意思就是被已讀不回。
朋友問我還信不信這套，我說：「信啊，我現在都改許別人的願，比較安全。」

en.content:
I was lost for a while back then,
yelling at the sky every day:
"Universe, just give me a sign."

The next day, my scooter died at an intersection,
my side mirror got knocked off,
my phone fell into a drain,
and then it started raining.

I looked up and asked:
"Is this... a sign?"

The universe didn't answer.

But suddenly I understood —
it had been answering all along.
It's just that its way of talking
looks exactly the same
as ruining my day.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying they're 'waiting for a sign' before doing anything. The joke is that the universe did respond — just brutally. That last line is quietly telling them to stop waiting: the signs have been maxed out for a while now.
variations: So I got smart and rephrased my wish: "Universe, please be gentle this time." Nothing happened at all. Turns out gentle just means left on read.
A friend asked if I still believe in this stuff. I said, "Sure. I just make wishes for other people now. Safer that way."

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「The afterlife is probably just regular life with worse Wi-Fi」單句梗，重寫成對來世的荒謬想像

zh-tw.content:
我朋友很怕死，
每次聊到都臉色發白。

我安慰他：
「別怕，來世可能沒你想的那麼可怕。」

他眼睛一亮：「真的嗎？」

「嗯，來世大概就跟現在差不多——
一樣要排隊，一樣要繳費，
一樣有人已讀不回，
只是 Wi-Fi 更爛，
而且沒有『重來一次』的按鈕。」

他沉默了。

「所以死掉不是解脫？」

「不是，是換一個更差的方案
繼續用。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常把「好想登出人生」掛在嘴邊的朋友。笑點在把來世降級成訊號更爛的同一款生活——但反過來也是溫柔的提醒：既然哪裡都一樣，不如把現在這一版用好一點。
variations: 他問：「那有沒有 VIP 方案？」我說：「有，叫做『活得認真一點』，但很多人捨不得升級。」
地獄跟天堂的差別，可能只是收訊格數不同——天堂滿格，地獄永遠停在一格猛跳。

en.content:
My friend is terrified of dying.
He goes pale every time it comes up.

I tried to comfort him:
"Relax, the afterlife might not be as scary as you think."

His eyes lit up: "Really?"

"Yeah, it's probably a lot like now —
still lines to wait in, still bills to pay,
still people leaving you on read.
The Wi-Fi's just worse,
and there's no 'try again' button."

He went quiet.

"So dying isn't an escape?"

"Nope. It's just downgrading to a worse plan
and carrying on."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's always joking about wanting to 'log out of life.' The joke reframes the afterlife as the same life with worse signal — but flipped around, it's a gentle nudge: if everywhere's the same, you might as well make this version count.
variations: He asked, "Is there a VIP plan?" I said, "Yeah, it's called 'living like you mean it,' but most people can't bring themselves to upgrade."
The difference between heaven and hell might just be bars of signal — heaven's at full, hell's forever stuck on one flickering bar.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「your birthday cake is a fire hazard」老化梗，重寫成中年生日的自嘲場景

zh-tw.content:
今年生日，我媽幫我插蠟燭，
插到一半停下來。

我說：「怎麼了？」

她說：「蛋糕太小，插不下。」

我說：「那少插幾根。」

她想了一下，
默默去廚房拿了一個滅火器
放在桌上。

「不然這樣，」她說，
「我們改成用『生日營火』的規格辦，
比較安全。」

那天我許的願望是：
「明年拜託换個更大的蛋糕，
不然就換個更小的年紀。」

兩個願望，
生命都沒有打算幫我實現。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給即將邁入新一個十字頭、對生日有點複雜情緒的朋友。笑點在蠟燭多到要準備滅火器——但最後那句許願的無力感，才是所有中年人心照不宣的真心話。
variations: 我媽補了一句：「蠟燭錢都快比蛋糕貴了。」我說：「那明年直接放一根，寫個『以此類推』就好。」
朋友問我幾歲，我說：「一根蠟燭配一台滅火器的年紀。」他馬上懂了。

en.content:
For my birthday this year, my mom was putting candles on the cake,
then stopped halfway.

I said, "What's wrong?"

She said, "The cake's too small. They won't all fit."

I said, "Then use fewer."

She thought about it,
quietly went to the kitchen,
and set a fire extinguisher on the table.

"Tell you what," she said,
"let's just run this as a 'birthday bonfire.'
Safer that way."

The wish I made that day was:
"Next year please give me a bigger cake,
or failing that, a smaller age."

Two wishes.
Life had no intention of granting either.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend edging toward a new decade with mixed feelings about birthdays. The joke is a cake so crowded it needs a fire extinguisher — but that helpless closing wish is the unspoken truth every middle-aged person nods along to.
variations: My mom added, "The candles cost more than the cake now." I said, "Then next year just use one and write 'and so on' next to it."
A friend asked how old I am. I said, "One-candle-plus-a-fire-extinguisher old." He understood immediately.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「a joke about trickle-down economics, but 99% will never get it」梗，重寫成台灣分配落差的諷刺對話

zh-tw.content:
我跟朋友說：「我有一個關於『財富會往下流』的笑話。」

他說：「講來聽聽。」

我說：「講不了，
因為這個笑話講完，
我們這種在最底下的人
永遠等不到笑點流下來。」

他愣了一下，
然後苦笑：「所以笑點卡在上面了？」

「對，」我說，
「聽說上面的人笑得很開心，
只是笑聲
也是往上流的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說「經濟有在成長，但跟我沒關係」的朋友。笑點在一個關於分配的笑話，本身也被卡在頂端——講的是財富，戳的是每個看得懂的人自己的處境。
variations: 他問：「那我們什麼時候會分到？」我說：「等上面的人先笑夠，通常那要很久。」
涓滴理論最誠實的版本：東西真的會往下滴，只是滴的是壓力，不是錢。

en.content:
I told a friend, "I've got a joke about wealth trickling down."

He said, "Let's hear it."

I said, "Can't tell it,
because by the time it's done,
people like us at the very bottom
never get the punchline to trickle down."

He paused,
then gave a bitter laugh: "So the punchline's stuck up top?"

"Yep," I said.
"I hear the folks up there are laughing plenty.
It's just that the laughter
trickles up too."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who always says 'the economy's growing, just not for me.' The joke is that a joke about distribution gets stuck at the top too — it's about wealth, but it pokes at the exact situation of anyone who gets it.
variations: He asked, "So when do we get our share?" I said, "After the people up top finish laughing — which usually takes a while."
The most honest version of trickle-down: things really do drip downward. It's just pressure that drips, not money.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I thought adulthood meant freedom, it meant bills」成年梗，重寫成小時候期待與現實的反差

zh-tw.content:
小時候我最期待長大，
因為大人好像什麼都可以自己決定。

現在我終於長大了，
確實什麼都可以自己決定——

決定先繳房租還是先繳卡費，
決定要省餐費還是省睡眠，
決定這個月要放棄哪一種夢想。

原來大人的自由，
是「自由地在很少的選項裡選一個」。

昨天我七歲的姪子跟我說
他好想快點長大。

我摸摸他的頭，
什麼都沒說。

有些真相，
讓他自己慢慢繳學費比較好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛出社會、對「自由」還有點幻想的朋友。笑點在把成年的自由降級成選項超少的選擇題——最後那句「讓他自己慢慢繳學費」，是所有過來人最溫柔也最殘忍的沉默。
variations: 姪子問我：「長大好玩嗎？」我說：「很好玩，就是那種要付費才能玩、而且不能退款的好玩。」
成年人的自由試用期大概三天，就是剛領到第一份薪水到發現要繳稅之間那段。

en.content:
As a kid, I couldn't wait to grow up,
because adults seemed to get to decide everything themselves.

Now I've finally grown up,
and it's true, I get to decide everything —

decide whether to pay rent or the credit card first,
decide whether to skip meals or skip sleep,
decide which dream to give up on this month.

Turns out an adult's freedom
is 'freely picking one from very few options.'

Yesterday my seven-year-old nephew told me
he really wants to grow up fast.

I patted his head
and said nothing.

Some truths
are better learned by paying your own tuition.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend fresh out into the world who still has illusions about 'freedom.' The joke downgrades adult freedom to a multiple-choice question with barely any choices — and that closing line about 'paying your own tuition' is the gentlest, cruelest silence every veteran of adulthood knows.
variations: My nephew asked, "Is being grown up fun?" I said, "Super fun — the kind you have to pay for and can't get a refund on."
An adult's free trial of freedom lasts about three days: the stretch between getting your first paycheck and finding out about taxes.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Life's short, but my list of regrets is impressively long」與「I smile like I paid rent on time, I didn't」單句梗，重寫成目標清單與遺憾清單的反差對話

zh-tw.content:
我列了兩張清單。

一張是「今年要完成的目標」，
寫了三行就寫不下去了。

另一張是「今年的遺憾」，
我一口氣寫滿整頁，
還覺得意猶未盡。

朋友問：「怎麼遺憾清單比較長？」

我說：「因為目標要努力才有，
遺憾不用努力，它自己會長。」

他點點頭，
然後默默把自己的目標清單
翻到背面，
開始寫遺憾。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每年許新希望、年中就默默放棄的朋友。笑點在把「努力才有的東西」跟「不努力就會自己長的東西」擺在一起——最後朋友翻到背面那個動作，是所有人心照不宣的認輸。
variations: 後來我把目標清單撕了，理由很正當：至少這樣目標達成率就不會再拖累我的心理健康。
遺憾清單唯一的好處是，它是我今年唯一有持續在更新的東西。

en.content:
I made two lists.

One was 'goals to finish this year' —
I got three lines in and ran dry.

The other was 'regrets from this year' —
I filled the whole page in one go
and still felt like there was more.

A friend asked, "Why's the regret list longer?"

I said, "Because goals take effort to have.
Regrets don't. They grow on their own."

He nodded,
then quietly flipped his own goal list over
and started writing regrets
on the back.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who makes resolutions every January and quietly gives up by June. The joke sets 'things you have to work for' against 'things that grow by themselves' — and that move where the friend flips the page over is the unspoken surrender everyone recognizes.
variations: Later I tore up the goal list, for a very solid reason: at least now my completion rate can't drag down my mental health anymore.
The one upside of the regret list is that it's the only thing I've consistently kept updating all year.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「The more I search for life's purpose, the more I realize I hate looking for things」與「I looked into the abyss, it asked me what I was doing with my life」單句梗，重寫成對深淵發呆的荒謬對話

zh-tw.content:
我花了大半輩子在找人生的意義。

找了三十年，
終於得出一個結論：

我這個人，
最討厭的事
就是找東西。

那天我對著深淵發呆，
想說也許它藏著答案。

深淵看了我一眼，
淡淡地問：
「你人生到底在幹嘛？」

我愣住，
決定還是別跟深淵聊天了——

它問問題的方式，
比我媽還一針見血。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個常常半夜問「我到底在幹嘛」的朋友。笑點在把「尋找人生意義」跟「討厭找東西」這兩件事撞在一起，最後讓深淵反問——把存在焦慮寫成一場你注定會輸的對話。
variations: 我後來想通了：找不到意義沒關係，反正我連鑰匙都常常找不到，標準本來就不能訂太高。
深淵最後補了一句：「其實我也在找我的意義，只是沒人要往我這裡看。」原來連深淵都很孤單。

en.content:
I spent most of my life searching for its meaning.

Thirty years of searching,
and I finally reached a conclusion:

the thing I hate most
in the whole world
is looking for stuff.

One day I sat staring into the abyss,
figuring maybe it was hiding the answer.

The abyss glanced at me
and asked, flatly,
"So what exactly are you doing with your life?"

I froze,
and decided not to chat with the abyss anymore —

it asks harder questions
than my mother does.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who asks 'what am I even doing' at 2 a.m. The joke crashes 'searching for life's meaning' into 'hating looking for things,' then lets the abyss ask the question back — turning existential dread into a conversation you were always going to lose.
variations: I eventually made peace with it: no big deal that I can't find meaning — I can't even find my keys, so my standards were never that high.
The abyss added one more thing: "Honestly, I'm still looking for my meaning too. Nobody ever looks my way." Turns out even the abyss is lonely.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「My phone battery lasts longer than my motivation」單句梗，重寫成手機電量與人的動力對比並擴寫

zh-tw.content:
我發現一件很殘酷的事：

我手機的電量，
比我的動力還持久。

它早上滿電出門，
撐到晚上還有兩成。

而我早上也是滿電出門，
開機十分鐘就進入省電模式，
中午跳出低電量警告，
下午直接自動關機。

差別只在於——

手機沒電，
插上線就能繼續。

我沒電，
只能假裝還有電，
繼續跑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每天喊「好累」但還是準時上線的同事。笑點在把人跟手機並排比較，一路輸到底——最後那句「假裝還有電繼續跑」，是每個成年人都很熟的續航方式。
variations: 最公平的一點是：手機至少會顯示剩幾趴，而我從來不知道自己還剩多少，通常是關機了才知道。
我也想要行動電源，但人類版的行動電源叫「週末」，而且常常還沒充飽就被週一搶走。

en.content:
I've noticed something brutal:

my phone's battery
lasts longer than my motivation.

It leaves the house full in the morning
and still has 20% by night.

I also leave full in the morning,
but ten minutes in I'm on low-power mode,
by noon there's a low-battery warning,
and by afternoon I've shut myself off.

The only difference is —

when the phone dies,
you plug it in and it keeps going.

When I die,
I just pretend I still have charge
and keep going.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who says 'so tired' every day but still logs in on time. The joke lines a person up against a phone and loses every round — and that closing 'pretend I still have charge and keep going' is the exact survival mode every adult knows.
variations: The fairest part is that at least the phone shows a percentage. I never know how much I have left — usually I only find out after I've shut down.
I want a power bank too, but the human version is called 'the weekend,' and it usually gets snatched away by Monday before it's even fully charged.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I asked AI for help, now it's asking me for updates」單句梗，重寫成 AI 反過來催進度的職場焦慮情境

zh-tw.content:
我今天請 AI 幫我處理工作。

它效率超高，
三秒就把東西做完了。

然後很客氣地問：
「還有其他需要協助的嗎？」

我說沒有了，謝謝。

結果過五分鐘，
它又跳出來問：
「進度如何？有卡關嗎？要我幫忙嗎？」

我愣了一下才意識到——

連 AI 都比我積極，
而我只想躺著。

以前我擔心被 AI 取代，
現在我擔心的是
它會不會嫌我不夠拚。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊靠 AI 提升效率、一邊懷疑自己存在意義的上班族。笑點在角色互換——本來是人在用 AI，最後變成 AI 在關心人的進度，把「被取代焦慮」升級成「連工具都比我努力」的荒謬。
variations: 後來我乾脆跟 AI 說：「你這麼會，那你來當人，我去當 AI，我只想待機。」
最諷刺的是，AI 二十四小時不休息，還會主動問要不要幫忙；我休息八小時，起床第一件事是想怎麼繼續休息。

en.content:
Today I asked AI to handle some work for me.

It was blazing fast —
done in three seconds.

Then it politely asked,
"Anything else I can help with?"

I said no thanks, we're good.

Five minutes later
it popped up again:
"How's it going? Stuck anywhere? Want me to help?"

It took me a second to realize —

even the AI is more driven than I am,
and all I want to do is lie down.

I used to worry about being replaced by AI.
Now I worry
it'll think I'm not trying hard enough.
en.editorNote: Send to the office worker who boosts their output with AI while quietly questioning their own purpose. The joke flips the roles — the human was using the AI, and by the end the AI is checking on the human's progress, upgrading 'fear of replacement' into 'even the tool works harder than me.'
variations: Eventually I told the AI: "You're so good at this — you be the human, I'll be the AI. I just want to stay on standby."
The most ironic part: the AI runs 24/7 and offers to help unprompted; I rest for eight hours, and the first thing I do on waking is figure out how to keep resting.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Nothing like existential dread with your morning cereal」單句梗，重寫成把存在焦慮當早餐配菜的荒謬營養學

zh-tw.content:
現代人的早餐其實很豐盛。

一碗麥片，
一杯咖啡，
再配一份剛睜開眼就自動湧上來的存在焦慮。

麥片提供纖維，
咖啡提供咖啡因，
存在焦慮提供
「我到底為什麼要起床」的深度哲學思辨。

營養很均衡：
身體有了能量，
靈魂有了負擔。

吃完你會發現，
餐桌上最難消化的
從來不是麥片。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一睜眼就開始懷疑人生、卻還是乖乖起床上班的朋友。笑點在用「營養均衡」的正經口吻，把存在焦慮包裝成一道每天必吃的早餐配菜——最後那句戳的是每個人都吞過的東西。
variations: 醫生叫我早餐要吃得營養，我照做了，只是沒想到最有份量的那道是焦慮。
我試著跳過那份焦慮直接吃麥片，結果它很體貼，中午自己補送到公司。

en.content:
The modern breakfast is actually quite hearty.

A bowl of cereal,
a cup of coffee,
and a side of existential dread that rises up the moment you open your eyes.

The cereal gives you fiber,
the coffee gives you caffeine,
the dread gives you
a deep philosophical inquiry into 'why do I get up at all.'

Very balanced nutrition:
the body gets energy,
the soul gets a burden.

When you're done, you'll notice
the hardest thing on the table to digest
was never the cereal.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who questions their entire existence the second they wake up but still gets up for work. The joke uses a straight-faced 'balanced nutrition' voice to plate existential dread as a daily breakfast side — and that last line pokes at something everyone has swallowed.
variations: My doctor told me to eat a nutritious breakfast. I did — I just didn't expect the most substantial dish to be the anxiety.
I tried skipping the dread and going straight to the cereal. It was very considerate: it delivered itself to my office by noon.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Budgeting is just telling your dreams they're too expensive」單句梗，重寫成把記帳擬人化成夢想終結者的荒謬情境

zh-tw.content:
後來我終於學會記帳了。

所謂記帳，
就是每個月固定坐下來，
親手告訴自己的每一個夢想：

「你太貴了。」
「你也是。」
「你更不用想。」

表格很誠實，
它不會安慰你，
只會用紅字冷冷提醒你——
這個月你又活超支了。

以前我以為記帳是為了存錢，
後來才發現，
它其實是在幫我一個一個
跟想要的東西告別。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個下載了第五個記帳 App、還是月底吃土的朋友。笑點在把「記帳」從理財工具翻轉成夢想的死亡通知單——最後那句戳的是每個認真記帳的人都懂的無力感。
variations: 我的記帳 App 最近很貼心，會主動幫我把「想買的」自動歸類到「這輩子算了」。
理財專家說先付錢給自己，我照做了，結果我發現自己是所有債主裡最沒還款能力的那個。

en.content:
I finally learned how to budget.

Budgeting, it turns out,
is sitting down once a month
to personally inform each of your dreams:

"You're too expensive."
"You too."
"You don't even get to dream."

The spreadsheet is honest.
It won't comfort you.
It just quietly flags, in red,
that you overspent on being alive again this month.

I used to think budgeting was about saving money.
Turns out
it's about saying goodbye,
one by one,
to the things you wanted.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's downloaded their fifth budgeting app and is still broke by the 20th. The joke flips 'budgeting' from a money tool into a death notice for your dreams — that last line lands on the quiet helplessness every honest budgeter knows.
variations: My budgeting app got thoughtful lately: it auto-files anything I 'want to buy' straight into 'not in this lifetime.'
A finance guru said to pay yourself first. I tried — and discovered I'm the least creditworthy of everyone I owe.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I thought adulthood meant freedom. It meant bills」與「used to be fun, now fiscally responsible and dead inside」，重寫成成年像抽到反轉獎品的情境

zh-tw.content:
小時候我超想快點長大，
因為大人看起來好自由——
想幾點睡就幾點睡，
想買什麼就買什麼。

現在我長大了。

我確實可以想幾點睡就幾點睡，
只是隔天七點還是得起床上班。

我也確實想買什麼就買什麼，
只是買之前要先問過房租、
水電、還有那張永遠繳不完的卡費。

原來成年不是拿到自由，
是拿到一張
「恭喜你，現在所有帳單都歸你了」的畢業證書。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個剛出社會、發現大人世界跟想像差很多的朋友。笑點在童年幻想與成年現實的落差——把「自由」重新定義成「有權利選擇，但每個選擇後面都跟著一張帳單」。
variations: 小時候覺得零用錢好少，長大後才知道，那可能是我這輩子唯一一筆不用還的錢。
大人騙我說長大很好玩，現在換我騙小孩，這叫傳承。

en.content:
As a kid I couldn't wait to grow up,
because adults looked so free —
sleep whenever you want,
buy whatever you want.

Now I'm grown up.

And yes, I can sleep whenever I want.
I just still have to be up at seven for work.

And yes, I can buy whatever I want.
I just have to run it past the rent first,
then the utilities,
then the credit card bill that never actually ends.

Turns out adulthood isn't getting your freedom.
It's getting a diploma that reads:
"Congratulations — every bill is now yours."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend fresh out of school realizing the adult world isn't what the brochure promised. The joke rides the gap between childhood fantasy and grown-up reality — redefining 'freedom' as 'the right to choose, with a bill stapled to every option.'
variations: As a kid I thought my allowance was tiny. Turns out it may be the only money I'll ever get that I don't have to pay back.
Adults lied and told me growing up was fun. Now I lie to kids about it too. We call that tradition.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Group projects and eternal consciousness combine as one person's greatest fear」單句梗，重寫成把分組報告當成永恆懲罰的存在主義情境

zh-tw.content:
如果真的有地獄，
我猜它的懲罰不是火，
是一場永遠交不出去的分組報告。

五個人一組，
其中三個已讀不回，
一個負責在最後一天問「我們主題是什麼」，
剩下那個，
就是你。

報告永遠差最後一頁，
死線永遠是明天，
而你永遠在改別人交來的
那份用注音打的草稿。

最恐怖的不是永恆，
是永恆裡
你還是那個唯一在做事的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在每個團隊裡都默默扛下全部工作的朋友。笑點在把「分組報告」升級成地獄本身——最後那句戳的是責任感太強的人一輩子逃不掉的宿命。
variations: 組員問我進度，我說做好了，他們立刻按讚下線——原來我不是隊友，是他們的雲端硬碟。
老師說這次分組是為了訓練合作能力，結果我訓練出來的是獨立完成所有事的能力。

en.content:
If hell is real,
I don't think the punishment is fire.
I think it's a group project you can never turn in.

Five people to a team.
Three left you on read,
one shows up on the last day to ask "wait, what's our topic,"
and the last one
is you.

The report is always one page short,
the deadline is always tomorrow,
and you're forever fixing the draft
someone submitted in all lowercase with no punctuation.

The scary part isn't the eternity.
It's that even in eternity,
you're still the only one doing anything.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who quietly carries the entire workload on every team. The joke upgrades 'group project' into hell itself — that last line lands on the fate no over-responsible person ever escapes.
variations: A teammate asked how it was going. I said done. They liked the message and logged off. Turns out I'm not a teammate, I'm their cloud storage.
The teacher said group work builds collaboration skills. What it built in me was the ability to do everything alone.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「My dark thoughts pay rent, they've lived here too long」單句梗，重寫成把負面念頭當成賴著不走的房客的情境

zh-tw.content:
我腦袋裡住了幾個很難搞的房客。

一個叫焦慮，半夜最吵，
一個叫後悔，專門重播十年前的糗事，
還有一個叫「你是不是不夠好」，
連押金都沒付就住進來了。

我試過趕他們走，
結果他們反問：
「這裡不是你蓋的嗎？」

我啞口無言。

現在我學會跟他們共處了——
畢竟房子是我的，
但顯然，做主的一直是他們。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上說沒事、其實腦袋每天很吵的朋友。笑點在把負面念頭擬人化成賴著不走的房客——用輕鬆的比喻講一件很沉的事，最後那句留白，笑完會安靜一下。
variations: 我想把焦慮趕出去，房仲說這種老房客受法律保護，很難處理。
後來我乾脆開始收他們房租，反正住這麼久，多少貢獻點靈感也好。

en.content:
A few difficult tenants live in my head.

One's named Anxiety — loudest at 3 a.m.
One's named Regret — specializes in replaying things I did ten years ago.
And one's named "Maybe You're Just Not Good Enough,"
who moved in without even paying a deposit.

I tried to evict them.
They just asked,
"Didn't you build this place yourself?"

I had nothing to say.

So now I've learned to live with them —
after all, the house is mine.
But clearly, they've been running it the whole time.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who says they're fine but whose head is loud every single day. The joke personifies dark thoughts as tenants who won't leave — a light metaphor for a heavy thing, and that last line leaves enough space that the laugh trails into quiet.
variations: I tried to evict Anxiety. The agent said long-term tenants like that are legally protected — good luck.
Eventually I started charging them rent. They've lived here this long; they can at least contribute some inspiration.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I tried adulting, it needs instructions」單句梗，重寫成大人出廠沒附說明書的情境

zh-tw.content:
「大人」這個東西，出廠時沒附說明書。

我以為長大就會自動解鎖各種能力：
報稅、看懂合約、跟水電行殺價、
在被冒犯的當下想出漂亮的回嘴。

結果我到現在，
每一項都還在試用版，
而且沒有客服可以打。

上網查，
教學影片全部從「這其實很簡單」開始，
然後我就更確定，
出問題的是我。

不過後來我發現一件事——
其實每個大人都在偷看攻略，
只是大家都假裝自己天生就會。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一邊繳帳單一邊懷疑自己是不是沒長大的朋友。笑點在「大人沒附說明書」，把成年焦慮講得很輕，最後一句拆穿「大家都在偷看攻略」，笑完會鬆一口氣。
variations: 我打去客服想問人生怎麼用，語音說：「您好，您的問題我們也不知道，請稍候。」
後來我接受了：所謂長大，就是把「我不會」升級成「我邊做邊學」。

en.content:
"Adult" doesn't come with a manual.

I thought growing up would auto-unlock all these abilities:
filing taxes, reading contracts, haggling with the plumber,
thinking of the perfect comeback in the exact moment I'm insulted.

Turns out every one of those
is still running in trial mode,
and there's no support line to call.

I look it up online,
and every tutorial starts with "this is actually really simple,"
which just confirms
the broken one is me.

But eventually I realized something —
every adult is secretly reading the walkthrough too.
We're all just pretending we were born knowing.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend paying bills while quietly wondering if they ever actually grew up. The joke is that adulthood ships without a manual — it makes the anxiety feel light, and the last line about everyone secretly reading the walkthrough lands like relief.
variations: I called a support line to ask how life works. The recording said, "Hello, we don't know either. Please hold."
Eventually I made peace with it: growing up just means upgrading "I can't do this" to "I'll figure it out as I go."

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「My motivation is buffering」單句梗，重寫成動力像電腦一直緩衝載不完的情境

zh-tw.content:
我的動力最近一直在緩衝。

轉圈圈的那個圈，
從早上轉到晚上，
進度條卡在 99%，
就是不肯跳到 100。

我試過重開機——早睡早起，
試過清快取——把待辦清單全刪掉，
甚至想過恢復原廠設定，
回到那個什麼都還相信的自己。

結果都只跳出同一行字：
「載入中，請勿關閉。」

後來我不等它跑完了。
反正人生從來沒有全部載好的一天，
就這樣半載入地，先動起來再說。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個明明很累卻自責「怎麼還不振作」的朋友。笑點在把提不起勁翻譯成電腦緩衝，最後一句「半載入地先動起來」把自責變成一種溫柔的許可。
variations: 我問系統還要多久，它說：「預估剩餘時間：您的整個三十歲。」
動力沒有回來，但我發現，等它回來的人生，會一直暫停在原地。

en.content:
My motivation has been buffering lately.

That little spinning wheel
has been spinning from morning to night,
progress bar stuck at 99%,
refusing to hit 100.

I've tried restarting — early to bed, early to rise.
Tried clearing the cache — deleting the whole to-do list.
I even considered a factory reset,
going back to the version of me that still believed in things.

Every time, the same line pops up:
"Loading. Please do not power off."

So I stopped waiting for it to finish.
Life never fully loads anyway,
so I just start moving, half-loaded, and go.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's exhausted but keeps scolding themselves to just snap out of it. The joke translates burnout into a buffering screen, and the last line turns the self-blame into gentle permission to start before you feel ready.
variations: I asked the system how much longer. It said, "Estimated time remaining: the rest of your thirties."
The motivation never came back. But I noticed the life that waits for it stays paused right where it is.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Ghosts don't scare me, unpaid bills do」單句梗，重寫成帳單比鬼可怕、薪水投胎的情境

zh-tw.content:
朋友問我怕不怕鬼。

我說不怕。
真正半夜會突然驚醒的，
是想起還沒繳的帳單、
下個月要扣的房租、
還有那句「您的信用卡即將到期」。

鬼至少只是飄來飄去，
帳單是真的會追過來，
而且利息還一天一天長。

朋友說：那你信輪迴嗎？

我說信啊——
每個月薪水一入帳，
馬上就投胎去別人的口袋了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個月底才敢打開銀行 App 的朋友。笑點在「鬼不可怕、帳單才可怕」的反差，最後薪水「投胎」那句把現實苦講成一個苦笑，很好轉發。
variations: 我家最活躍的靈異現象，是餘額每個月自動消失，而且找不到兇手。
別人怕鬼壓床，我怕的是帳單壓身，而且它們從不放假。

en.content:
A friend asked if I'm scared of ghosts.

I said no.
What actually jolts me awake at 3 a.m.
is remembering the bill I haven't paid,
next month's rent,
and the text that says "your card is about to expire."

At least ghosts just drift around.
Bills genuinely chase you,
and the interest grows a little more every single day.

My friend asked, "Do you believe in reincarnation?"

I said absolutely —
the moment my paycheck lands,
it's instantly reborn into someone else's pocket.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who only dares open their banking app at the end of the month. The joke flips the fear — ghosts are fine, bills are terrifying — and the paycheck-reborn-into-someone-else's-pocket line turns real dread into a rueful laugh.
variations: The most active paranormal event in my home is my balance vanishing every month with no suspect.
Other people fear a ghost sitting on their chest at night. I fear the bills — and they never take a day off.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I asked my anxiety for a day off, it laughed louder than I did」單句梗，重寫成向焦慮請假、焦慮吐露其實是笨拙的在乎的情境

zh-tw.content:
我跟焦慮請了一天假。

我說：拜託，就一天，
讓我什麼都不擔心，
好好過個週末。

焦慮看著我，笑得比我還大聲：
「請假？我可是全年無休、
連你睡著都在加班的員工。」

我說那你圖什麼？

它想了想，說：
「其實我只是怕，
哪天你真的不需要我了，
就沒人這麼在乎你會不會出事。」

我愣住。
原來它一直用最笨的方式，
在替我擔心。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個腦袋每天很吵、卻總覺得是自己有問題的朋友。前面是自嘲式的地獄笑話，最後焦慮那句自白把整個氣氛翻轉——原來過度擔心，本質是一種笨拙的在乎，笑完會有點想哭。
variations: 我問焦慮要不要休假去旅行，它說：「好啊，那我陪你去，順便擔心飛機。」
我學會不趕它走了，只是偶爾跟它說：謝謝提醒，但這件事真的沒那麼嚴重。

en.content:
I asked my anxiety for a day off.

I said, please, just one day.
Let me worry about nothing
and actually enjoy the weekend.

Anxiety looked at me and laughed louder than I did:
"A day off? I'm the employee who works year-round,
pulling overtime even while you sleep."

I asked, what do you even get out of this?

It thought for a second, then said:
"Honestly, I'm just scared
that one day you won't need me anymore,
and then no one will care this much whether you're okay."

I froze.
All this time, in the clumsiest way possible,
it had been worrying on my behalf.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose head is loud every day but who's convinced the problem is them. It opens as a self-deprecating dark joke, then anxiety's confession flips the whole mood — over-worrying is really just care with terrible manners. You laugh, then get a little misty.
variations: I asked my anxiety if it wanted to take a vacation. It said, "Sure, I'll come along — and worry about the plane."
I stopped trying to evict it. Now I just tell it sometimes: thanks for the heads-up, but this really isn't that serious.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I tried adulting — it needs instructions」單句梗，重寫成成年沒有說明書、只能邊拆邊裝的情境

zh-tw.content:
我一直以為長大會附說明書。

結果二十幾歲那年，
人生直接把「成人」這台機器丟給我，
沒有紙本、沒有教學影片，
連客服電話都是空號。

我就這樣邊裝邊拆，
租房、報稅、看醫生、跟房東吵架，
每一項都是第一次，
每一次都當作最後一次在硬撐。

後來我才發現，
原來大家手上都沒有說明書。
那些看起來很會的人，
只是把慌張藏得比較好而已。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個假日還在研究怎麼繳健保費的朋友。前段是成年焦慮的自嘲，最後一句把矛頭收回來——沒有人拿到說明書，大人都是裝出來的，笑完會鬆一口氣
variations: 我終於看懂成人這台機器的說明書了，第一頁只寫著四個字：「祝你好運」。
小時候以為大人什麼都懂，長大才知道，我們只是比較擅長在崩潰前先把訊息已讀不回。

en.content:
I always assumed being a grown-up came with a manual.

Then sometime in my twenties,
life just handed me the machine labeled "adult" —
no printed guide, no tutorial video,
and the support hotline was disconnected.

So I've been assembling it while it runs:
rent, taxes, doctor's appointments, arguing with the landlord,
every task a first attempt,
every attempt braced like it's the last.

Eventually I realized
nobody got the manual.
The people who look like they've got it figured out
are just better at hiding the panic.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend spending their weekend figuring out how to pay a bill they didn't know existed. It opens as self-deprecating adulting dread, then the last line pulls it back — nobody got the instructions, everyone's improvising. You laugh, then exhale.
variations: I finally found the manual for adulthood. Page one just says, in big letters: "Good luck."
As a kid I thought adults knew everything. Turns out we're just better at leaving the panic on read.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Existence is like Wi-Fi — unstable and always buffering」單句梗，重寫成人生像不穩定的訊號、卻還是連著的情境

zh-tw.content:
我覺得人生很像我家的網路。

訊號時有時無，
最需要的時候一定卡住，
跑一跑就轉圈圈，
然後跳出一行字：「連線不穩，請稍候。」

我等了三十幾年，
也還在稍候。

不過昨天半夜，
訊號突然滿格了一下，
剛好看到朋友傳來一句「還好嗎」。

那一瞬間我懂了——
人生大概就是這樣：
大部分時間都在緩衝，
但總會有那麼一格，
剛好接住你想接住的東西。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個覺得自己人生一直在轉圈圈、載入不完的朋友。前面用網路訊號比喻存在的荒謬，最後「總會有那麼一格」把緩衝的無力感翻成一點微光，剛好接住訊息的畫面很有畫面感
variations: 人生最殘忍的設計，是它從來不告訴你進度條跑到幾 %。
我不是活得很爛，我只是一直在載入，只是這個載入沒有取消鍵。

en.content:
I think life is a lot like my home Wi-Fi.

The signal comes and goes,
it always drops right when you need it most,
it spins and spins,
then flashes a line: "Connection unstable, please wait."

I've been waiting thirty-some years.
Still buffering.

But late last night
the signal jumped to full for a second,
just long enough to catch a text from a friend: "You doing okay?"

And in that instant I got it —
maybe that's all life is:
mostly buffering,
but every so often one bar of signal
lands exactly on the thing you needed to catch.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who feels like their life is permanently spinning, never finishing the load. It uses a flaky-signal metaphor for existential absurdity, then the single bar of signal flips the helplessness into a flicker of warmth. The caught-text image lands.
variations: The cruelest design choice in life is that it never shows you what percent the progress bar is at.
I'm not living badly. I'm just perpetually loading — and this load has no cancel button.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「Retirement kills more people than hard work ever did」單句梗，重寫成退休老人因為突然沒事做而失重、後來找到新節奏的情境

zh-tw.content:
老陳退休那天，
同事說：「終於解脫了，好好享受。」

他笑著點頭，
心想總算不用再被工作追著跑。

結果退休第三個月，
他跟醫生說最近整個人很不對勁。
醫生問：「工作壓力大嗎？」

老陳說：「不，是完全沒有壓力，
我早上起來，不知道要幹嘛。
原來一直逼我起床的，是那些我以為我討厭的東西。」

後來他去公園教人下棋，
每天又有了非起床不可的理由。
他說：「人不是被工作累死的，
是被『沒人需要你』給嚇死的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個嘴上喊著好想退休、其實一停下來就慌的朋友。前段是退休反而失重的黑色反差，最後老陳那句點破——真正累垮人的不是忙碌，是不再被需要，笑完會想找點事情做
variations: 退休不是關掉鬧鐘，是關掉「有人在等你」這件事，這才是最難習慣的。
老陳的結論：忙的時候盼著閒，閒了才發現，被需要本身就是一種續命。

en.content:
The day old Chen retired,
a coworker said, "Finally free. Go enjoy yourself."

He smiled and nodded,
thinking at last he wouldn't be chased by work anymore.

But three months into retirement
he told his doctor something felt deeply off.
The doctor asked, "Work stress getting to you?"

Chen said, "No — it's the total lack of it.
I wake up in the morning with nothing to do.
Turns out the thing dragging me out of bed all those years
was everything I thought I hated."

So he started teaching chess in the park,
and suddenly had a reason he had to get up again.
He said, "Work doesn't wear people out.
Being needed by no one — that's what finishes you."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying they can't wait to retire but panics the moment they stop. It opens on the dark irony of retirement leaving you weightless, then Chen's closing line names it — what wears you down isn't being busy, it's no longer being needed. You laugh, then go find something to do.
variations: Retiring isn't turning off the alarm clock. It's turning off the fact that someone was waiting for you — that's the hard part.
Chen's takeaway: when you're busy you crave rest, and only in the rest do you learn that being needed was keeping you alive.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I look at the obituary page. If my name is not on it, I get up」老年黑色幽默梗，重寫成阿嬤每天讀訃聞確認自己還在、順便替老朋友道別的情境

zh-tw.content:
阿嬤每天早上第一件事，
是戴上老花眼鏡，翻報紙的訃聞版。

我問她幹嘛看那麼晦氣的東西。

她說：「我在確認我還在。
名字沒登上去，我就起床，
登上去了，那今天就不用煮飯了。」

我被她逗笑，
她卻補了一句：
「其實也是在替老朋友送最後一程。
年紀到了，報紙比同學會準時，
每登一個，我就默默說聲慢走。」

說完她把報紙折好，
慢慢站起來去煮她的稀飯——
又是還在的一天。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給家裡有長輩、或自己開始怕老的人。前段是阿嬤查訃聞確認自己還活著的地獄式幽默，後段那句「替老朋友送最後一程」把玩笑轉成溫柔的告別，最後起身煮稀飯的畫面很有生命力
variations: 阿嬤的養生哲學：每天起床先確認自己不在報紙上，就是最大的健康檢查。
她說人老了最實際的成就，就是把每一個平凡的早晨，都當成賺到的。

en.content:
The first thing Grandma does every morning
is put on her reading glasses and turn to the obituaries.

I asked why she reads something so grim.

She said, "I'm checking that I'm still here.
If my name isn't on the page, I get up.
If it is, well, I won't need to cook today."

I laughed,
but then she added:
"It's also how I see my old friends off.
At my age, the paper's more reliable than any reunion.
Each name I find, I quietly say: travel well."

Then she folded the paper,
rose slowly, and went to make her rice porridge —
another day of still being here.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone with aging relatives, or anyone starting to fear getting old. It opens on the dark humor of scanning obituaries to confirm you're alive, then the line about seeing old friends off turns the joke into a gentle goodbye. The image of her getting up to cook is quietly full of life.
variations: Grandma's wellness routine: confirm you're not in the paper, and that's the only checkup that matters.
She says the most practical achievement of old age is treating every ordinary morning as a bonus you got for free.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「I don't fear death. I fear small talk at the funeral」單句梗，重寫成怕的不是死、是死後那場尷尬社交、進而想活得讓人有話可說的情境

zh-tw.content:
有人問我怕不怕死。

我說死本身還好，
我怕的是我的告別式，
一群人不熟又不能滑手機，
只能硬聊：
「他人其實還不錯啦。」
「對啊，滿……安靜的一個人。」

然後全場陷入我最熟悉的那種沉默。

想到這裡我有點慌，
於是決定趁還活著，
多跟人好好說幾句話，
多做幾件值得被拿出來聊的事。

這樣至少那天，
大家不用尬聊，
是真的有東西可以講。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個在婚禮喪禮都不知道手該放哪的社恐朋友。前段把「怕死」偷換成「怕告別式尬聊」很好笑，後段卻悄悄變成一種積極的提醒——趁活著多留下能被講述的故事，笑完有點被推了一把
variations: 我最大的遺願，就是希望我的告別式不要冷場，拜託大家事先套好招。
後來我發現，怕告別式沒話聊，其實是在提醒自己：現在就去把日子過得有故事一點。

en.content:
Someone asked if I'm afraid of dying.

I said death itself is fine.
What scares me is my own funeral —
a room of people who barely know each other,
can't scroll their phones,
stuck making conversation:
"He was actually a decent guy."
"Yeah. Real… quiet type."

And then the whole room sinks into
that exact silence I know so well.

Thinking about it makes me a little anxious,
so I've decided that while I'm still alive
I'll say more real things to people,
and do a few more things worth mentioning.

That way, at least on that day,
no one has to force the small talk —
there'll actually be something to say.
en.editorNote: Send to the socially anxious friend who never knows where to put their hands at weddings or funerals. Swapping "fear of death" for "fear of funeral small talk" is the funny part, then it quietly becomes a nudge — leave behind stories worth telling while you're still here. You laugh, then feel gently pushed.
variations: My one final wish is that my funeral doesn't go awkwardly silent — please, everyone, coordinate your material in advance.
Eventually I realized: fearing an empty funeral is really just a reminder to go live a life with a few stories in it, starting now.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自年輕世代用「反正 AI 會取代我們，何必努力」自嘲的現象，重寫成面試被 AI 篩掉、最後找到自己不可取代之處的情境

zh-tw.content:
我投了四十封履歷。

第一關是 AI 篩選，
第二關是 AI 面試，
第三關人資跟我說：
「我們決定先讓 AI 做做看。」

我回家跟朋友哭訴，
他說：「至少你被三種 AI 認真評估過，
這年頭沒幾個人被這麼多對象拒絕。」

我笑到咳嗽。

後來我想通了：
AI 可以整理我的經歷，
可以寫出比我漂亮的自我介紹，
但它沒辦法陪朋友笑到咳嗽。

我要找的工作，
大概就在那個它到不了的地方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在求職、被自動化篩選折磨的朋友。前半把「被拒絕」堆疊成荒謬喜劇，後半用「AI 沒辦法陪你笑到咳嗽」把價值感還回去。適合在群組裡分享，比純安慰更有力
variations: 履歷石沉大海不是你的錯，是收件的那位根本沒有心跳。
AI 能複製我的技能，複製不了我加班時偷偷幫同事訂的那杯奶茶。

en.content:
I sent out forty applications.

Round one: AI screening.
Round two: AI interview.
Round three: a recruiter told me,
"We've decided to let the AI try the role first."

I called a friend to complain.
He said, "At least three separate AIs
gave you serious consideration.
Most people don't get rejected
by that many suitors these days."

I laughed until I coughed.

Later it clicked:
AI can organize my résumé,
write a better intro than I ever could —
but it can't make a friend laugh until he coughs.

The job I'm looking for
is probably somewhere it can't reach.
en.editorNote: Send to a friend grinding through automated job screening. The first half stacks rejections into absurd comedy; the second half quietly hands the value back. Works better in a group chat than plain reassurance.
variations: Your résumé vanishing isn't on you — the thing reading it doesn't have a pulse.
AI can copy my skill set. It can't copy the bubble tea I secretly ordered for a coworker at 9pm.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「我的錢包跟我是有毒關係，需要它的時候它就離開」單句梗，重寫成把房租當訂閱制、進而重新定義「值不值得」的情境

zh-tw.content:
有人問我有沒有訂閱什麼服務。

我說有啊，
一個月三萬二，
提供十二坪的空間、
一扇看得到隔壁牆的窗，
以及隨時可能被漲價的權利。

他說：「那不就是房租？」

我說對，
而且是我唯一從來沒忘記續費的訂閱。

我曾經覺得這很可悲。

後來有天下大雨，
我躺在裡面聽雨打窗戶，
突然覺得——
這三萬二買的不是十二坪，
是「今晚有地方可以回」。

那一刻我覺得，還算划算。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個月被房租壓得喘不過氣的朋友。前段把房租講成訂閱制超級好笑又扎心，後段的雨聲轉折不說教，只是把視角挪一格。適合在繳完房租的深夜發
variations: 我人生中最忠誠的關係，是我跟房東的自動轉帳。
我不是在租房子，我是在按月租用一個「不用睡在外面」的資格。

en.content:
Someone asked what subscriptions I have.

I said one.
A thousand dollars a month.
Comes with four hundred square feet,
a window facing my neighbor's wall,
and the right to be repriced at any time.

He said, "That's just rent."

I said yes.
And it's the only subscription
I have never once forgotten to renew.

I used to find that pathetic.

Then one night it poured,
and I lay there listening to rain hit the glass,
and realized —
the thousand dollars isn't buying four hundred square feet.
It's buying "somewhere to come back to tonight."

Right then, it felt like a fair price.
en.editorNote: For the friend whose rent eats the paycheck. The subscription framing lands hard and funny; the rain turn shifts the view one notch without lecturing. Best posted late at night, right after rent clears.
variations: The most loyal relationship in my life is the autopay to my landlord.
I'm not renting an apartment. I'm renting, monthly, the right not to sleep outside.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「我試著當大人，結果發現它需要說明書」單句梗，重寫成到處找人生說明書、最後發現大家都在邊做邊寫的情境

zh-tw.content:
我一直在找當大人的說明書。

問我爸，他說他也還在找。
問我主管，他說他當年是直接跳過教學關卡。
問網路，網路叫我先訂閱。

三十五歲那年我終於接受：
這東西根本沒有出版。

難過了一陣子。

然後我發現一件事——
大家手上都是空的，
卻還是把小孩養大了、
把爛攤子收乾淨了、
把日子過下去了。

原來說明書不是拿來讀的，
是每個人一邊活一邊寫的。

我翻開自己那本，
第一頁還是空白。

所以我拿起筆。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛出社會、或剛升上主管、或剛當爸媽而覺得自己完全沒準備好的人。前段的荒謬感很好笑，後段「說明書是自己寫的」收得溫暖不油膩。也很適合當生日祝福
