variations: 當大人最恐怖的不是責任變重，是你發現原本以為的大人也在裝。
我沒有說明書，但我有橡皮擦——這大概就是重點。

en.content:
I've been looking for the instruction manual for being an adult.

Asked my dad — he's still looking too.
Asked my boss — he says he skipped the tutorial.
Asked the internet — the internet asked me to subscribe.

At thirty-five I finally accepted it:
the thing was never published.

That stung for a while.

Then I noticed something.
Everyone's hands are empty too,
and they still raised the kids,
still cleaned up the disasters,
still kept the days moving.

Turns out the manual isn't something you read.
It's something everyone writes
while they're living.

I opened mine.
Page one is still blank.

So I picked up a pen.
en.editorNote: For anyone newly out of school, newly promoted, or newly a parent and quietly convinced they're unqualified. The absurd search is the laugh; "you write the manual yourself" is the landing, warm without being sappy. Doubles as a birthday message.
variations: The scariest part of adulthood isn't the responsibility. It's realizing the adults you looked up to were improvising too.
I don't have a manual, but I do have an eraser. That's probably the whole point.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「我的動力還在緩衝中」單句梗，重寫成把人生比喻成載入條、最後發現緩衝也是進度的情境

zh-tw.content:
我的動力一直在緩衝。

轉圈圈轉了大概三年，
進度條卡在百分之九十九，
每次我以為要好了，
它就從頭開始。

我朋友說：「你要不要重開機？」
我說我試過，
重開的是我的人生，
不是那個進度條。

有天我盯著那個圈圈看很久，
突然想到——
會轉，代表還在連線。

真正壞掉的東西，
是連圈圈都不轉的。

所以我決定不再逼它快一點，
就讓它慢慢跑。
反正它一直都在跑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個明明沒有停下來、卻一直覺得自己毫無進展的人。前段的載入條比喻很有現代感也很好笑，後段「會轉代表還在連線」是很溫柔的一句安慰。適合週日晚上發給正在內耗的朋友
variations: 我不是不努力，我是頻寬不足。
百分之九十九也是九十九，只是這一段特別長而已。

en.content:
My motivation has been buffering.

The little wheel has been spinning
for about three years now.
Progress bar stuck at 99%.
Every time I think it's about to finish,
it starts over.

A friend said, "Have you tried restarting?"
I said I did.
I restarted my life.
The bar kept spinning.

Then one day I stared at that wheel a long time
and it hit me —
if it's spinning, it's still connected.

The things that are actually broken
don't spin at all.

So I stopped demanding it go faster.
I let it run.
It has been running the whole time.
en.editorNote: For the person who hasn't stopped moving but is convinced they've gotten nowhere. The loading-bar image is funny and very of-this-moment; "if it's spinning, it's still connected" is the gentle landing. Good Sunday-night message.
variations: I'm not lazy. I'm bandwidth-limited.
99% is still 99%. This stretch is just a long one.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自導航「recalculating」永遠不責怪你走錯路的黑色幽默觀察，重寫成把人生繞遠路和導航重新規劃路線並置的情境，場景與對白原創

zh-tw.content:
我開車開錯路，導航說：「路線重新規劃中。」

我下錯交流道，它說：「路線重新規劃中。」

我整個人生走偏了，它還是那句：「路線重新規劃中。」

有天我忍不住問它：
「你不會生氣嗎？我一直走錯。」

它沉默了一秒，說：
「我沒有『你走錯了』這個功能。
我只有『從你現在的位置，重新出發』。」

那天我在紅燈前坐了很久。
原來這世界上，
真的有東西不在乎你走了多少冤枉路，
只在乎你現在要去哪。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一直在懊悔自己選錯路的人。前面很好笑，後面導航那句「我沒有你走錯了這個功能」是整段最溫柔的地方。適合對正在轉職、重來、或覺得人生繞遠路的人說
variations: 導航從來不會說「你怎麼又錯了」，它只會說「請掉頭」。人生也可以掉頭，只是我們自己捨不得。
「重新規劃路線」聽起來像失敗，其實只是換一條到得了的路。

en.content:
I took a wrong turn. The GPS said, "Recalculating."

I got off at the wrong exit. "Recalculating."

My whole life went sideways. Same voice, same word: "Recalculating."

One day I finally asked it,
"Doesn't it bug you? I keep getting it wrong."

It paused for a second and said,
"I don't have a setting for 'you messed up.'
I only have 'from where you are now, let's go again.'"

I sat at that red light for a long time.
Turns out there really is something in this world
that doesn't care how many wrong turns you took,
only where you're trying to go now.
en.editorNote: For the person stuck replaying every wrong turn they've made. The joke lands early; "I don't have a setting for 'you messed up'" is the quiet, warm part. Good for anyone changing careers, starting over, or convinced they took the long way.
variations: GPS never says 'why did you mess up again.' It just says 'make a U-turn.' You're allowed to U-turn too.
'Recalculating' sounds like failure. It's just a different road that still gets you there.

items.sourceNote: 原創，衍生自「連盆栽都養不活代表我照顧不好自己」的自我否定，反轉成把水都留給別人的黑色幽默療癒角度

zh-tw.content:
我連一盆多肉都養不活。

它其實不用澆很多水，
網路上說它「很好養、新手友善」，
結果它在我手上撐了兩個月，
默默地乾成一團標本。

我很自責，覺得我連照顧一株植物的資格都沒有，
那我到底憑什麼照顧自己。

我朋友聽完，說：
「等等，那盆多肉沒人澆水就會死。
你這幾年沒人特別澆你，
還不是活到現在，還會難過、還會自責。」

「你不是不會照顧生命。
你只是把水，都留給自己以外的東西了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種很會照顧別人、卻總覺得自己一無是處的人。多肉的部分很好笑很有共鳴，最後那句「你把水都留給自己以外的東西了」是想讓他把注意力轉回自己身上
variations: 植物養不活不代表你不會愛，只代表你需要有人提醒你也要喝水。
我養死的每一盆植物，都是我忙著顧別人的證據。

en.content:
I can't keep a single succulent alive.

It barely needs water.
The internet swore it was 'beginner-friendly, impossible to kill.'
It lasted two months in my care
and quietly dried into a little museum piece.

I felt terrible. If I can't keep a plant alive,
what business do I have keeping myself alive?

My friend heard all this and said,
"Hold on. That succulent dies if nobody waters it.
You've gone years without anyone watering you,
and you're still here — still sad, still trying, still hard on yourself."

"You're not bad at caring for things.
You just poured all the water on everything that wasn't you."
en.editorNote: For the person who takes care of everyone and still thinks they're useless. The dead-succulent bit is the relatable laugh; "you poured all the water on everything that wasn't you" is meant to turn their attention back toward themselves.
variations: Killing plants doesn't mean you can't love. It means someone needs to remind you to drink water too.
Every plant I've killed is just proof of how busy I was keeping someone else alive.

items.sourceNote: 原創，把「忘記密碼／重設密碼」的登入流程比喻成找不回自己，安全問題「你最快樂時在做什麼」作為轉折的黑色幽默療癒情境

zh-tw.content:
我想登入我的人生，
系統說：「密碼錯誤。」

我試了我以為的夢想，錯誤。
我試了別人期待的樣子，錯誤。
我試了小時候的志願，帳號已停用。

最後我按了「忘記密碼」。

它問我：「請回答安全問題——
你最快樂的時候，在做什麼？」

我愣住了。
我發現我根本不記得答案。

然後我慢慢地、一個字一個字打上去。
打完才發現，
原來重設密碼的第一步，
是先想起自己到底喜歡什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個活得很用力、卻忘記自己為什麼出發的人。前面登入失敗的排比很好笑，最後「重設密碼的第一步是想起自己喜歡什麼」是希望他停下來問問自己。適合深夜內耗的朋友
variations: 人生沒有「記住我」這個選項，每一天都要重新登入一次。
我不是迷路，我只是忘記密碼，而安全問題是我一直逃避回答的那個。

en.content:
I tried to log into my life.
The system said, "Incorrect password."

I tried the dream I thought was mine. Wrong.
I tried the version other people expected. Wrong.
I tried my childhood ambition. Account deactivated.

Finally I hit "Forgot password."

It asked, "Security question:
What were you doing when you were happiest?"

I froze.
I realized I couldn't remember the answer.

So I typed it out slowly, one letter at a time.
And only when I finished did I understand —
the first step to resetting the password
is remembering what you actually love.
en.editorNote: For the person running hard through life who forgot why they started. The failed-login rhythm is the joke; "the first step to resetting the password is remembering what you love" is the ask to stop and check in. Good for a late-night spiraling friend.
variations: Life has no 'remember me' box. You have to log back in every single day.
I'm not lost. I just forgot the password, and the security question is the one I keep dodging.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「人生沒有退貨」的黑色幽默，重寫成想把爛感情、選錯科系、浪費的時間退貨、客服卻只給店家積點（經驗值）的情境，人物與對白原創

zh-tw.content:
我想把我這幾年退貨。

那段很爛的感情，我想退。
那個選錯的科系，我想退。
那些浪費掉的時間，我想全額退款。

人生客服冷冷地說：
「不好意思，本店一律不接受退貨。」

我快哭了。

它頓了一下，補上一句：
「但我們可以給你店家積點。
你退不掉的每一件事，
都會換算成經驗值，存進你的帳戶。
下次消費，你會比較知道自己要什麼。」

原來人生不能退貨，
不是因為它無情，
是因為它把你每一次踩雷，
都偷偷幫你存起來了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一直後悔過去、想砍掉重練的人。前面「想退貨」的排比很有共鳴，最後「踩雷都幫你存成積點」是想讓他知道那些爛經歷沒有白費。適合陪一個正在懊悔的人
variations: 人生沒有七天鑑賞期，但有無限期的「下次不要這樣」。
退不掉的過去不是損失，是還沒用掉的點數。

en.content:
I'd like to return the last few years.

That awful relationship — returning it.
The major I picked wrong — returning it.
All that wasted time — I'd like a full refund.

Life's customer service said flatly,
"Sorry. We have a strict no-returns policy."

I almost cried.

Then it paused and added,
"But we can offer you store credit.
Everything you can't return
gets converted into experience points on your account.
Next time, you'll know a little better what you actually want."

Turns out life won't let you return anything
not because it's heartless,
but because it's been quietly banking
every mistake you ever made.
en.editorNote: For the person who keeps wishing they could undo the past. The 'returns' list is the relatable part; 'every mistake gets banked as store credit' is meant to tell them the bad chapters weren't wasted. Good company for someone deep in regret.
variations: Life has no 14-day return window, but it does have an unlimited 'let's not do that again.'
The past you can't return isn't a loss. It's credit you haven't spent yet.

items.sourceNote: 原創，把「我把自己搞丟了」的失落感設定成走進失物招領處報案，櫃檯阿姨說這種很多、而且它會自己回來排隊的黑色幽默療癒情境

zh-tw.content:
我走進失物招領處，說：
「我要報案，我把自己搞丟了。」

櫃檯的阿姨頭也沒抬：
「特徵？」

我說：「呃……大概三年前還很有熱情，
笑起來眼睛會亮，
對很多事情都還有感覺。」

她想了一下，說：
「這種的我們這裡很多喔，
每天都有人來報。」

我問：「那……找得回來嗎？」

她終於抬起頭：
「找得回來啊。
不過通常不是我們找到的，
是你哪天突然又對某件事眼睛一亮，
它自己就回來排隊了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個覺得自己「不見了」、找不回以前那個有熱情的自己的人。失物招領的設定很好笑，最後「它自己會回來排隊」是很溫柔的一句——你不用拼命找，只要還願意被某件事打動就好
variations: 我沒有壞掉，我只是被歸類在「無人認領」太久了。
有些東西不是弄丟了，是暫時放在你想不起來的地方。

en.content:
I walked into the lost and found and said,
"I'd like to file a report. I lost myself."

The woman at the counter didn't look up.
"Description?"

I said, "Um… about three years ago he still had a lot of energy.
Eyes lit up when he smiled.
Still felt things."

She thought for a moment.
"We get a lot of those in here.
Somebody files one every single day."

I asked, "So… can it be found?"

She finally looked up.
"Sure it can.
Usually we're not the ones who find it, though.
One day something makes your eyes light up again,
and it just walks back and gets in line on its own."
en.editorNote: For anyone who feels like they've gone missing — like the version of them that used to care is nowhere to be found. The lost-and-found setup is the laugh; "it walks back in on its own" is the gentle part: you don't have to hunt for it, you just have to stay open to being moved by something.
variations: I'm not broken. I've just been filed under 'unclaimed' for too long.
Some things aren't lost. They're just in a place you can't remember putting them.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「my reflection and I are no longer on speaking terms, it was a toxic mirror relationship」與鏡子照出老化的梗，重寫成把浴室鏡子當成從不說謊的老朋友、每天報告壞消息卻也是唯一天天出席的存在，情境與對白原創

zh-tw.content:
我跟浴室的鏡子關係很微妙。

它每天早上第一個跟我打招呼，
然後開始報壞消息：
這裡多了一條，那裡少了一根，
昨天的黑眼圈今天升級成套裝。

有天我忍不住問它：
「你就不能挑好聽的說嗎？」

鏡子安靜了一下，說：
「我如果會說謊，你早就把我換掉了。
這世界願意每天對你說實話的，
剩沒幾個了。

而且你有沒有發現——
不管你變成什麼樣子，
我從來沒有一天不出席。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個開始害怕照鏡子、覺得自己一天比一天崩壞的朋友。前半段的『黑眼圈升級成套裝』是笑點，最後鏡子那句『我從來沒有一天不出席』把整個笑話翻成溫柔——會對你說實話又天天陪著你的，真的沒幾個
variations: 進階版：我對鏡子說「你老了。」鏡子回：「我們是一起的，別假裝這是我一個人的事。」
鏡子從不修圖，這是它最殘忍也最溫柔的地方。

en.content:
My bathroom mirror and I have a complicated relationship.

It's the first thing to greet me every morning,
then it starts delivering the bad news:
a new line here, a missing strand there,
yesterday's eye bags upgraded to a full luggage set today.

One day I snapped:
"Could you maybe lead with something nice?"

The mirror went quiet, then said:
"If I knew how to lie, you'd have replaced me years ago.
The number of things in this world willing to tell you the truth every day
is getting pretty short.

And have you noticed —
no matter what you turn into,
I've never missed a single day."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's started dreading the mirror, who feels like they're falling apart a little more each day. The 'eye bags upgraded to a luggage set' is the laugh; the mirror's 'I've never missed a single day' flips it warm — the things that tell you the truth AND show up daily are rarer than you think.
variations: Follow-up: I told the mirror, "You've gotten old." It said, "We're in this together. Don't pretend it's just my problem."
The mirror never uses a filter. That's the cruelest and kindest thing about it.

items.sourceNote: 衍生自「warranty void if seal is broken」與「the human body is out of warranty」的梗，重寫成把身體當成過保商品拿去維修站、店員說原廠早就不修了、但你自己還在運轉的情境，人物與對白原創

zh-tw.content:
我把我的身體拿去維修站，
跟店員說：「這裡會痛，那裡會響，早上開機超級慢。」

店員查了一下型號，搖搖頭：
「先生，你這台早就過保了。
原廠現在只出新機，不修舊的。」

我問：「那還能怎麼辦？」

店員：「就這樣用啊。
過保之後的每一天，
嚴格來說都是它自己撐的，
不在任何合約裡。」

我愣住。

「所以我現在用的，全部都是賺到的？」

店員笑了：「你可以這樣想。
過保的機器最勇，因為它已經沒有什麼好怕壞的了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個開始這裡痠那裡痛、覺得自己老機器一台的朋友。『早上開機超級慢』是共鳴笑點，最後『過保之後每一天都是賺到的』把對衰老的焦慮翻成一種豁達——沒有保固反而最自由
variations: 進階版：「有沒有延長保固？」店員：「有，叫做好好睡覺跟少生氣，而且要自己繳。」
身體過保那天沒有人通知你，你只是某天蹲下去，聽見膝蓋幫你唸了收據。

en.content:
I took my body into the repair shop and told the guy:
"It hurts here, it makes noises there, and boot-up in the morning is painfully slow."

He checked the model number and shook his head.
"Sir, this unit's way out of warranty.
Manufacturer only makes new ones now. They don't fix the old models."

I asked, "So what do I do?"

Him: "You just keep using it.
Every day after the warranty runs out
is technically the machine going it alone —
not covered by any contract."

I froze.

"So everything I'm running on now is basically a bonus?"

He grinned. "You could look at it that way.
Out-of-warranty machines are the bravest.
They've got nothing left to be afraid of breaking."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's started aching in new places and feels like an old machine. The 'boot-up in the morning is painfully slow' lands the laugh; the closing 'everything after the warranty is a bonus' flips the dread of aging into something freeing — no warranty means nothing left to lose.
variations: Follow-up: "Any extended warranty?" "Yeah. It's called sleep well and stay calm. And you pay for it yourself."
Nobody notifies you the day your body goes out of warranty. You just crouch down one day and hear your knee read you the receipt.

items.sourceNote: 原創，把「來世會投胎成什麼」的想像設定成投胎成一個天天被按掉的鬧鐘——被需要卻總被忽略，最後反轉成『被按掉代表你每天都成功叫醒了某個人』的黑色幽默療癒角度

zh-tw.content:
我問神明：「我下輩子會投胎成什麼？」

神明翻了翻本子：「鬧鐘。」

我：「……就那種每天被人狠狠按掉的鬧鐘？」

「對。清晨五點半盡責地響，
然後被一巴掌拍安靜，
沒人感謝你，還被嫌吵。」

我沮喪地說：「這也太慘。
被需要，卻永遠被討厭。」

神明抬起頭：
「你換個角度想。
那顆鬧鐘一輩子沒失敗過——
它每天都成功把一個人叫回了這個世界。

被按掉，不是被否定。
是任務完成的聲音。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總覺得自己在公司在家裡都是『被使喚、被嫌、沒人謝』的朋友。前半段的鬧鐘設定很好笑也很扎心，最後『被按掉是任務完成的聲音』把那種不被感謝的委屈翻成一種默默的價值——你以為你被討厭，其實你每天都在把人叫醒
variations: 進階版：「那我可以選音樂嗎？」神明：「不行，但你可以選要不要繼續響——大部分鬧鐘都選了繼續。」
沒有人會謝謝鬧鐘，但沒有鬧鐘的那天，大家都遲到了。

en.content:
I asked the gods, "What will I be reincarnated as?"

The god flipped through a ledger. "An alarm clock."

Me: "...The kind that gets angrily smacked off every single morning?"

"Correct. You'll dutifully go off at 5:30 a.m.,
get slapped into silence,
nobody thanks you, and everybody calls you annoying."

I slumped. "That's brutal.
Needed, but forever resented."

The god looked up.
"Try another angle.
That alarm never once failed at its job —
every single day it successfully called someone back into the world.

Getting smacked off isn't rejection.
It's the sound of a mission completed."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who feels like they're always the one being ordered around, criticized, never thanked — at work, at home. The alarm-clock setup is funny and a little too real; the closing 'getting smacked off is the sound of a mission completed' flips that unthanked resentment into quiet worth — you think you're resented, but every day you're waking someone up.
variations: Follow-up: "Can I at least pick the ringtone?" God: "No. But you can choose whether to keep ringing. Most alarms choose to keep going."
Nobody thanks the alarm clock. But the day it doesn't ring, everyone's late.
